topics.title: Design a database schema for an application with ER diagram from a problem description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design a database schema for an application with ER diagram from a problem description # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Entity-Relationship (ER) Modeling** is a conceptual data modeling technique introduced by **Peter Chen (1976)** that uses a diagrammatic representation to capture the logical structure of a database. It identifies **entities** (real-world objects), **attributes** (properties of entities), and **relationships** (associations between entities) before any physical database implementation begins. A **Database Schema** is the formal blueprint of how data is organized. It defines **tables (relations)**, **columns (attributes)**, **data types**, **constraints** (primary key, foreign key, NOT NULL, UNIQUE, CHECK), and the **relationships** among tables using referential integrity. In the **KTU 2024 DBMS Lab syllabus (PCCSL408)**, Experiment 2 specifically tests the student's ability to: 1. Read a natural-language problem statement. 2. Identify entities, attributes, and relationships. 3. Draw an **ER diagram** using **Chen's notation** or **Crow's Foot notation**. 4. Convert the ER diagram into a **relational schema** using standard mapping rules. 5. Optionally implement the schema as **SQL `CREATE TABLE`** statements. ## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary) | Term | Definition | Example | |------|------------|---------| | **Entity** | A real-world object with independent existence | Student, Book, Department | | **Entity Set** | Collection of similar entities | All Students in a University | | **Attribute** | A property describing an entity | Student.name, Book.price | | **Primary Key** | Attribute that uniquely identifies an entity | Student.roll_no | | **Relationship** | An association among entities | Student **enrolls in** Course | | **Cardinality** | Number of entity instances participating | 1:1, 1:N, M:N | | **Participation** | Whether all entities participate (total) or some (partial) | Total vs Partial | | **Weak Entity** | An entity that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes | Dependent (of Employee) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Designing a House Before Construction** > > Imagine you want to build a house. Before laying a single brick, an architect creates a **blueprint** showing rooms (entities), their features like doors and windows (attributes), and how rooms connect (relationships). You would never start construction without this plan. > > An **ER diagram is exactly that blueprint for a database**. The *rooms* are your **tables** (Student, Course, Department). The *features* are your **columns** (name, id, credits). The *connections* are your **foreign keys and JOINs** (a Student *enrolls in* a Course). Just as a missing room in a blueprint means a missing feature in the house, a missing entity in an ER diagram means a missing table in your database. ## 1.4 Two Standard Notations Used in KTU Exams | Notation | Visual Style | Most Common Use | |----------|--------------|-----------------| | **Chen's Notation** | Rectangles (entities), Diamonds (relationships), Ovals (attributes) | Academic textbooks, KTU theory papers | | **Crow's Foot Notation** | Boxes with crow's foot symbols (>, |, <) at line ends | Industry tools (Visio, Lucidchart, ER/Studio) | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Tip**: For university exams, **Chen's notation is preferred** because it is the syllabus standard. However, if you draw Crow's Foot, label cardinality clearly to avoid ambiguity and loss of marks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Step Methodology for Schema Design The KTU 2024 lab manual mandates a **systematic five-step approach**. Following this sequence guarantees full marks in the design experiment. ### Step 1: Requirement Analysis (Read the Problem Carefully) - Read the problem description **twice**. - **Highlight nouns** → these are candidate **entities**. - **Highlight verbs** → these are candidate **relationships**. - **Highlight adjectives/descriptors** → these are candidate **attributes**. - Identify **business rules** (e.g., "each course is taught by exactly one professor"). ### Step 2: Identify Entities and Attributes - Filter out nouns that are actually attributes of another entity. - Example: In "the student has a name", *name* is an attribute of *Student*, not a separate entity. - **Classify attributes**: - **Simple / Atomic** — cannot be subdivided (e.g., age, roll_no) - **Composite** — can be subdivided (e.g., address → street, city, pincode) - **Multivalued** — multiple values (e.g., phone_numbers of a person) - **Derived** — computable from others (e.g., age from DOB) - **Key attribute** — uniquely identifies (e.g., roll_no) ### Step 3: Identify Relationships and Cardinality - For each pair of related entities, determine the **cardinality ratio**: - **1:1 (One-to-One)** — Each A relates to exactly one B and vice versa. Example: A person has one passport; a passport belongs to one person. - **1:N (One-to-Many)** — One A relates to many B, but each B relates to only one A. Example: One department has many professors. - **M:N (Many-to-Many)** — Many A relate to many B. Example: Students enroll in many courses; courses have many students. - Determine **participation constraint**: - **Total (mandatory)** — every entity instance must participate. Shown by a **double line** in Chen's notation. - **Partial (optional)** — some entities may not participate. Shown by a **single line**. ### Step 4: Draw the ER Diagram - Use rectangles for entities, ellipses for attributes, diamonds for relationships. - Underline primary key attributes. - Use double ellipses for multivalued, dashed ellipses for derived. - Draw double rectangles for weak entities, double diamonds for identifying relationships. ### Step 5: Convert ER to Relational Schema (Mapping) - This is the **most heavily tested** step in KTU lab exams. Apply the mapping rules below. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet: ER-to-Relational Mapping Rules > [!IMPORTANT] > These **7 mapping rules** are the *core algorithmic content* of KTU Module Experiment 2. Memorize them in this exact order. | Rule # | ER Construct | Mapping Action | Resulting Schema | |--------|--------------|----------------|------------------| | **R1** | Strong Entity Set E with primary key PK | Create a relation R(E) with PK as primary key | `R(PK, attr1, attr2, ...)` | | **R2** | Weak Entity Set W with owner E and partial key `pk` | Create relation W with composite key `(E.PK, W.pk)` | `W(E_PK, pk, attrs...)` | | **R3** | 1:1 Relationship R between E1 and E2 | Merge into either E1 or E2 by adding the other's PK as FK | `E1(PK1, ..., E2_PK)` | | **R4** | 1:N Relationship R between E1 (1-side) and E2 (N-side) | Add E1.PK as foreign key in E2 | `E2(PK2, ..., E1_PK)` | | **R5** | M:N Relationship R between E1 and E2 | Create a **new separate relation** with composite PK | `R(E1_PK, E2_PK, attrs...)` | | **R6** | Multivalued Attribute A of Entity E | Create a **new separate relation** with composite key `(E.PK, A)` | `E_A(E_PK, A, ...)` | | **R7** | n-ary Relationship (n > 2) | Create a new relation with PKs of all participating entities | `R(E1_PK, E2_PK, ..., En_PK)` | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Pitfall**: For 1:1 and 1:N, **NEVER** create a separate table. Only M:N and multivalued attributes require new tables. KTU examiners routinely deduct **2 marks** for this mistake. ## 2.3 Cardinality Notation Quick Reference $$ \text{Cardinality Ratio} \in \{ 1:1,\ 1:N,\ M:N \} $$ $$ \text{Participation Constraint} \in \{ \text{Total (double line)},\ \text{Partial (single line)} \} $$ > [!NOTE] > In **Crow's Foot notation**, the symbols are: > - `|` → **Exactly One** (mandatory participation, 1-side) > - `O\|` or `O` → **Zero or One** (optional 1-side) > - `<` (crow's foot) → **Many** (N-side or M-side) > - `O<` → **Zero or Many** (optional M-side) > > These map directly to SQL `JOIN` semantics when the schema is implemented. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking Systems**: ER modeling is used to design accounts, customers, transactions, and loans before any SQL DDL is written. - **E-Commerce Platforms**: Entities like Customer, Order, Product, Cart, Payment, and Shipment are modeled first to ensure no data redundancy. - **Hospital Information Systems (HIS)**: Patient, Doctor, Ward, Prescription, and Appointment are ER-modeled to enforce referential integrity. - **University Management**: Student, Course, Faculty, Department, Enrollment, Exam — exactly the KTU lab problem domain. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Sample Problem (University Course Registration System) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU Model Question)**: > *"Design an ER diagram for a University database with the following description:* > *The University has several departments. Each department has a unique Dept_ID, name, and is headed by a professor (HOD). Every professor has a Prof_ID, name, salary, and belongs to exactly one department. Students have a Roll_No, name, and DOB. A student can enroll in many courses, and each course can have many students. Each course has a Course_ID, name, credits, and is taught by exactly one professor. A course belongs to exactly one department. Students must enroll in at least one course (total participation)."* ### Step 1: Identify Entities (Nouns) Extracting nouns: **University, Department, Professor, Student, Course**. - `University` → context, not an entity (treated as the entire scope). - `Department` → **Entity** (unique Dept_ID). - `Professor` → **Entity** (unique Prof_ID). - `Student` → **Entity** (unique Roll_No). - `Course` → **Entity** (unique Course_ID). **Analytical Matrix of Entities and Attributes:** | Entity | Primary Key | Other Attributes | Notes | |--------|-------------|------------------|-------| | **Department** | `Dept_ID` | `name`, `HOD_Prof_ID` (FK) | HOD is a professor, not a string | | **Professor** | `Prof_ID` | `name`, `salary` | Belongs to one department (1:N from Dept → Prof) | | **Student** | `Roll_No` | `name`, `DOB` | Enrolls in many courses | | **Course** | `Course_ID` | `name`, `credits` | Taught by one professor, belongs to one department | ### Step 2: Identify Relationships (Verbs) Extracting verbs and their cardinalities: | Relationship | Entity1 | Cardinality | Entity2 | Reasoning | |--------------|---------|-------------|---------|-----------| | `heads` | Department | **1:1** | Professor | A department has exactly one HOD; a prof heads at most one dept | | `works_in` | Department | **1:N** | Professor | One dept has many profs; each prof in one dept | | `enrolls_in` | Student | **M:N** | Course | A student takes many courses; a course has many students | | `teaches` | Professor | **1:N** | Course | One prof teaches many courses; each course taught by one prof | | `offers` | Department | **1:N** | Course | One dept offers many courses; each course in one dept | ### Step 3: Identify Participation Constraints | Relationship | Side | Participation | Reason | |--------------|------|---------------|--------| | `enrolls_in` | Student side | **Total** | "Students must enroll in at least one course" (explicit) | | `enrolls_in` | Course side | **Partial** | A new course may have no students yet | | `teaches` | Course side | **Total** | Every course is taught by exactly one professor | | `works_in` | Professor side | **Total** | Every professor belongs to a department | ### Step 4: Apply the 7 Mapping Rules (ER → Relational Schema) This is the **derivation step** — each rule is applied mechanically. **Rule R1 — Strong Entity Mapping:** $$ \text{Department}(\underline{\text{Dept\_ID}},\ \text{name}) $$ $$ \text{Professor}(\underline{\text{Prof\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{salary}) $$ $$ \text{Student}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}},\ \text{name},\ \text{DOB}) $$ $$ \text{Course}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{credits}) $$ **Rule R4 — 1:N Relationship (works_in, teaches, offers):** Add the "1-side" primary key as a foreign key on the "N-side" table. $$ \text{Professor}(\underline{\text{Prof\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{salary},\ \text{Dept\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) $$ $$ \text{Course}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{credits},\ \text{Dept\_ID}^{\text{FK}},\ \text{Prof\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) $$ **Rule R3 — 1:1 Relationship (heads):** Add the HOD's `Prof_ID` as a foreign key in `Department`. $$ \text{Department}(\underline{\text{Dept\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{HOD\_Prof\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) $$ **Rule R5 — M:N Relationship (enrolls_in):** Create a **new separate table** with composite primary key. $$ \text{Enrollment}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Course\_ID}^{\text{FK}}},\ \text{enroll\_date},\ \text{grade}) $$ ### Step 5: Final Optimized Relational Schema $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Department}(\underline{\text{Dept\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{HOD\_Prof\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) \\ &\textbf{Professor}(\underline{\text{Prof\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{salary},\ \text{Dept\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) \\ &\textbf{Student}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}},\ \text{name},\ \text{DOB}) \\ &\textbf{Course}(\underline{\text{Course\_ID}},\ \text{name},\ \text{credits},\ \text{Dept\_ID}^{\text{FK}},\ \text{Prof\_ID}^{\text{FK}}) \\ &\textbf{Enrollment}(\underline{\text{Roll\_No}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{Course\_ID}^{\text{FK}}},\ \text{enroll\_date},\ \text{grade}) \end{aligned} } $$ **Foreign Key Constraint Summary (Analytical Matrix):** | Child Table | Foreign Key | References | Rule Applied | |-------------|-------------|------------|--------------| | Professor | `Dept_ID` | Department(Dept_ID) | R4 (1:N) | | Course | `Dept_ID` | Department(Dept_ID) | R4 (1:N) | | Course | `Prof_ID` | Professor(Prof_ID) | R4 (1:N) | | Department | `HOD_Prof_ID` | Professor(Prof_ID) | R3 (1:1) | | Enrollment | `Roll_No` | Student(Roll_No) | R5 (M:N part 1) | | Enrollment | `Course_ID` | Course(Course_ID) | R5 (M:N part 2) | ### Step 6: SQL DDL Implementation (Optional KTU Lab Extension) ```sql CREATE TABLE Department ( Dept_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, HOD_Prof_ID VARCHAR(10), FOREIGN KEY (HOD_Prof_ID) REFERENCES Professor(Prof_ID) ); CREATE TABLE Professor ( Prof_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0), Dept_ID VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (Dept_ID) REFERENCES Department(Dept_ID) ); CREATE TABLE Student ( Roll_No VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, DOB DATE NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Course ( Course_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, credits INT CHECK (credits > 0 AND credits <= 6), Dept_ID VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, Prof_ID VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (Dept_ID) REFERENCES Department(Dept_ID), FOREIGN KEY (Prof_ID) REFERENCES Professor(Prof_ID) ); CREATE TABLE Enrollment ( Roll_No VARCHAR(15), Course_ID VARCHAR(10), enroll_date DATE NOT NULL, grade CHAR(2), PRIMARY KEY (Roll_No, Course_ID), FOREIGN KEY (Roll_No) REFERENCES Student(Roll_No), FOREIGN KEY (Course_ID) REFERENCES Course(Course_ID) ); ``` ## 3.2 Verification: The Mapping is Lossless The 7-rule algorithm is **provably lossless** — no information from the ER diagram is lost during conversion. This can be formally verified by checking: 1. Every entity has a relation. 2. Every attribute is placed in exactly one relation. 3. Every relationship is represented either as an FK or a separate table. 4. No null foreign key is forced in a 1:1 total participation scenario. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ER Diagram (Chen's Notation — Mermaid Representation) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following Mermaid `erDiagram` block represents the **complete ER diagram** for the University problem. This is the diagram students must draw in the KTU lab record. Every line below should be transcribed into the lab record with proper rectangles, ellipses, and diamonds if Chen's notation is required by the examiner. ```mermaid erDiagram DEPARTMENT ||--o{ PROFESSOR : "works_in (1:N)" DEPARTMENT ||--o{ COURSE : "offers (1:N)" DEPARTMENT ||--|| PROFESSOR : "heads (1:1, HOD)" PROFESSOR ||--o{ COURSE : "teaches (1:N)" STUDENT }o--o{ COURSE : "enrolls_in (M:N, Student side TOTAL)" DEPARTMENT { string Dept_ID PK string name string HOD_Prof_ID FK } PROFESSOR { string Prof_ID PK string name decimal salary string Dept_ID FK } STUDENT { string Roll_No PK string name date DOB } COURSE { string Course_ID PK string name int credits string Dept_ID FK string Prof_ID FK } ENROLLMENT { string Roll_No PK,FK string Course_ID PK,FK date enroll_date string grade } STUDENT ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "has" COURSE ||--o{ ENROLLMENT : "has" ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (ER-to-Schema Pipeline) This diagram shows the **five-stage transformation pipeline** from raw problem statement to executable SQL DDL. It is the exact sequence students should follow during the KTU lab viva and record submission. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Step 2: Extract Nouns and Verbs] B --> C[Step 3: Classify into Entities, Attributes, Relationships] C --> D[Step 4: Determine Cardinality and Participation] D --> E[Step 5: Draw ER Diagram in Chen Notation] E --> F[Step 6: Apply the 7 Mapping Rules] F --> G[Step 7: Generate Relational Schema with Keys] G --> H[Step 8: Write SQL CREATE TABLE Statements] H --> I[Step 9: Add Constraints PK FK NOT NULL CHECK] I --> J[Step 10: Verify Lossless Join Property] A1[NLP Tokenization] -.-> A B1[Highlight Nouns Entities] -.-> B B2[Highlight Verbs Relationships] -.-> B B3[Highlight Adjectives Attributes] -.-> B C1[Filter Out Anomalies] -.-> C D1[1:1, 1:N, M:N Ratios] -.-> D D2[Total vs Partial] -.-> D F1[R1 Strong Entity] -.-> F F2[R3 1:1 Relationship] -.-> F F3[R4 1:N Relationship] -.-> F F4[R5 M:N New Table] -.-> F F5[R6 Multivalued New Table] -.-> F style A fill:#FFE4B5 style E fill:#98FB98 style G fill:#87CEEB style J fill:#FFB6C1 ``` ## 4.3 Cardinality Decision Tree (Analytical Aid) Use this decision tree to determine the correct cardinality for each relationship in your ER diagram during the KTU exam. ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is one instance of A
related to at most one B?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is one instance of B
related to at most one A?} Q1 -- No --> M1[Many-to-Many M:N] M1 --> M1note[CREATE new table R with
composite PK A.PK, B.PK] Q2 -- Yes --> OneOne[1:1 Relationship] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is one B related
to many A?} Q3 -- Yes --> OneMany[1:N Relationship
A is 1-side, B is N-side] Q3 -- No --> Q4{Re-evaluate problem statement} Q4 --> Q1 OneOne --> O1[Merge: Add A.PK as FK in B
OR B.PK as FK in A] OneMany --> O2[Add A.PK as FK in B table
Total participation implies NOT NULL] style OneOne fill:#FFD700 style OneMany fill:#FFD700 style M1 fill:#90EE90 style M1note fill:#FFB6C1 style O1 fill:#ADD8E6 style O2 fill:#ADD8E6 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Define the following with one example each:** **(a) Entity and Entity Set** **(b) Total Participation** **Model Answer:** **(a)** An **Entity** is a real-world object with independent existence that can be uniquely identified. Example: A specific student named "Rahul" with roll number `S001` is an entity. An **Entity Set** is a collection of similar entities. Example: The set of all students currently enrolled in a college is a *Student* entity set. **(b)** **Total Participation** means every entity in the entity set must participate in at least one instance of the relationship. It is represented by a **double line** connecting the entity to the relationship diamond in Chen's notation. Example: In a "Department-Employs-Professor" relationship, every Professor must belong to some department (total participation of Professor in *employs*). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between strong entity and weak entity with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Strong Entity | Weak Entity | |---------|---------------|-------------| | **Existence** | Has independent existence | Depends on a strong (owner) entity | | **Identifier** | Has a primary key of its own | Cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone | | **Notation** | Single rectangle | Double rectangle | | **Relationship** | Not dependent | Connected to owner via a **double-diamond identifying relationship** | | **Example** | `Student(Roll_No, name, DOB)` — Roll_No is sufficient PK | `Dependent(Dep_Name, age, Emp_ID)` — needs `Emp_ID` from `Employee` plus `Dep_Name` to form PK | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** > **Design an ER diagram for the following Library Management System and convert it into a relational schema:** > > *"A library has multiple branches, each with a unique branch_id and location. Each branch holds many books. A book is identified by ISBN and has a title, price, and a single category. A book can be written by many authors, and an author can write many books. A book may have multiple copies, and each copy has a copy_id and a shelf location. Members of the library have a member_id, name, and phone (a member can have multiple phone numbers). A member can borrow many book copies, and a copy can be borrowed by many members over time. Each borrowing records the issue_date and return_date."* #### **Part (a) — Draw the complete ER diagram with all entities, attributes, relationships, cardinalities, and participation constraints. (7 Marks)** **Step 1 — Identify Entities:** - `Branch` (branch_id, location) - `Book` (ISBN, title, price, category) - `Author` (author_id, name) - `Book_Copy` (copy_id, ISBN_FK, branch_id_FK, shelf_location) - `Member` (member_id, name) - `Borrow` (issue_date, return_date, member_id_FK, copy_id_FK) **Step 2 — Identify Relationships:** | Relationship | Between | Cardinality | Participation | |--------------|---------|-------------|---------------| | `has` | Branch — Book_Copy | 1:N | Book_Copy side **Total** | | `written_by` | Book — Author | M:N | Both **Partial** | | `borrows` | Member — Book_Copy | M:N (with attributes) | Both **Partial** | **Step 3 — Special Attributes:** - `phone` in Member is a **multivalued attribute** (double ellipse). - `Book_Copy` is a **weak entity** (double rectangle) with partial key `copy_id`, depending on Book and Branch. **Step 4 — ER Diagram in Mermaid:** ```mermaid erDiagram BRANCH ||--o{ BOOK_COPY : "holds (1:N)" BOOK ||--o{ BOOK_COPY : "has_copies (1:N, identifying)" BOOK }o--o{ AUTHOR : "written_by (M:N)" MEMBER }o--o{ BOOK_COPY : "borrows (M:N with Borrow attrs)" MEMBER ||--o{ MEMBER_PHONE : "has (multivalued)" BRANCH { string branch_id PK string location } BOOK { string ISBN PK string title decimal price string category } AUTHOR { string author_id PK string name } BOOK_COPY { string copy_id PK string ISBN PK,FK string branch_id PK,FK string shelf_location } MEMBER { string member_id PK string name } MEMBER_PHONE { string member_id PK,FK string phone PK } BORROW { string member_id PK,FK string copy_id PK,FK string ISBN PK,FK string branch_id PK,FK date issue_date date return_date } ``` #### **Part (b) — Convert the above ER diagram into a fully normalized relational schema with primary keys, foreign keys, and constraint specification. (7 Marks)** **Applying the 7 Mapping Rules:** ```sql -- Rule R1: Strong Entities CREATE TABLE Branch ( branch_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, location VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Book ( ISBN VARCHAR(13) PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL, price DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (price > 0), category VARCHAR(50) ); CREATE TABLE Author ( author_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Member ( member_id VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL ); -- Rule R2: Weak Entity (Book_Copy depends on Book and Branch) CREATE TABLE Book_Copy ( copy_id VARCHAR(10), ISBN VARCHAR(13), branch_id VARCHAR(10), shelf_location VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (ISBN, branch_id, copy_id), FOREIGN KEY (ISBN) REFERENCES Book(ISBN), FOREIGN KEY (branch_id) REFERENCES Branch(branch_id) ); -- Rule R5: M:N Relationship (written_by) CREATE TABLE Book_Author ( ISBN VARCHAR(13), author_id VARCHAR(10), PRIMARY KEY (ISBN, author_id), FOREIGN KEY (ISBN) REFERENCES Book(ISBN), FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES Author(author_id) ); -- Rule R6: Multivalued Attribute (phone) CREATE TABLE Member_Phone ( member_id VARCHAR(10), phone VARCHAR(15), PRIMARY KEY (member_id, phone), FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES Member(member_id) ); -- Rule R5: M:N Relationship with attributes (borrows) CREATE TABLE Borrow ( member_id VARCHAR(10), copy_id VARCHAR(10), ISBN VARCHAR(13), branch_id VARCHAR(10), issue_date DATE NOT NULL, return_date DATE, PRIMARY KEY (member_id, copy_id, ISBN, branch_id, issue_date), FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES Member(member_id), FOREIGN KEY (copy_id, ISBN, branch_id) REFERENCES Book_Copy(copy_id, ISBN, branch_id) ); ``` **Final Optimized Relational Schema:** $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Branch}(\underline{\text{branch\_id}},\ \text{location}) \\ &\textbf{Book}(\underline{\text{ISBN}},\ \text{title},\ \text{price},\ \text{category}) \\ &\textbf{Author}(\underline{\text{author\_id}},\ \text{name}) \\ &\textbf{Book\_Copy}(\underline{\text{ISBN}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{branch\_id}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{copy\_id}},\ \text{shelf\_location}) \\ &\textbf{Member}(\underline{\text{member\_id}},\ \text{name}) \\ &\textbf{Member\_Phone}(\underline{\text{member\_id}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{phone}}) \\ &\textbf{Book\_Author}(\underline{\text{ISBN}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{author\_id}^{\text{FK}}}) \\ &\textbf{Borrow}(\underline{\text{member\_id}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{copy\_id}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{ISBN}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{branch\_id}^{\text{FK}}},\ \underline{\text{issue\_date}},\ \text{return\_date}) \end{aligned} } $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** > **Design an ER diagram and relational schema for the following Hospital Management System:** > > *"A hospital has many departments, each with a Dept_ID, name, and location. Each department has multiple doctors, identified by Doc_ID, name, specialization, and salary. A doctor treats many patients, and a patient can be treated by multiple doctors. A patient has a Pat_ID, name, age, and may have multiple phone numbers. Patients are admitted to rooms. A room has a room_no, type (general/ICU/private), and a daily charge. A patient may be admitted multiple times (each admission has an admission_id, admit_date, and discharge_date). Each admission is to exactly one room, but a room can host many admissions over time."* #### **Part (a) — Identify all entities, attributes (including multivalued and derived), relationships, cardinalities, and participation. Draw the ER diagram. (7 Marks)** **Entities & Attributes:** | Entity | Primary Key | Other Attributes | |--------|-------------|------------------| | Department | `Dept_ID` | `name`, `location` | | Doctor | `Doc_ID` | `name`, `specialization`, `salary` | | Patient | `Pat_ID` | `name`, `age` (derived from DOB), `DOB`, `phone` (multivalued) | | Room | `room_no` | `type`, `daily_charge` | | Admission | `admission_id` | `admit_date`, `discharge_date` | **Relationships & Cardinalities:** | Relationship | Cardinality | Participation | Notes | |--------------|-------------|---------------|-------| | Department — Doctor | 1:N | Doctor side **Total** | Each doctor belongs to one dept | | Doctor — Patient (treats) | M:N | Both **Partial** | Many-to-many treatment relationship | | Patient — Room (admitted_via) | M:N (through Admission) | Patient side **Total** (during admission) | Need association entity Admission | | Admission — Room | 1:N (one admission → one room; one room → many admissions) | Admission side **Total** | Each admission is to exactly one room | **Derived attribute:** `age` is derived from `DOB` (shown with dashed ellipse). **Multivalued attribute:** `phone` of Patient (shown with double ellipse). **ER Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid erDiagram DEPARTMENT ||--o{ DOCTOR : "employs (1:N)" DOCTOR }o--o{ PATIENT : "treats (M:N)" PATIENT ||--o{ ADMISSION : "has (1:N, identifying)" ROOM ||--o{ ADMISSION : "hosts (1:N)" PATIENT ||--o{ PATIENT_PHONE : "has (multivalued)" DEPARTMENT { string Dept_ID PK string name string location } DOCTOR { string Doc_ID PK string name string specialization decimal salary string Dept_ID FK } PATIENT { string Pat_ID PK string name date DOB int age } PATIENT_PHONE { string Pat_ID PK,FK string phone PK } ROOM { string room_no PK string type decimal daily_charge } ADMISSION { string admission_id PK string Pat_ID PK,FK string room_no PK,FK date admit_date date discharge_date } ``` #### **Part (b) — Convert the ER diagram to a normalized relational schema and write the corresponding SQL DDL. (7 Marks)** ```sql -- R1: Strong Entities CREATE TABLE Department ( Dept_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, location VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Doctor ( Doc_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, specialization VARCHAR(50), salary DECIMAL(10,2) CHECK (salary > 0), Dept_ID VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, FOREIGN KEY (Dept_ID) REFERENCES Department(Dept_ID) ); CREATE TABLE Patient ( Pat_ID VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL, DOB DATE NOT NULL ); CREATE TABLE Room ( room_no VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY, type VARCHAR(20) CHECK (type IN ('General', 'ICU', 'Private')), daily_charge DECIMAL(8,2) CHECK (daily_charge >= 0) ); -- R5: M:N Doctor-Patient (treats) CREATE TABLE Treatment ( Doc_ID VARCHAR(10), Pat_ID VARCHAR(15), treatment_date DATE, diagnosis TEXT, PRIMARY KEY (Doc_ID, Pat_ID, treatment_date), FOREIGN KEY (Doc_ID) REFERENCES Doctor(Doc_ID), FOREIGN KEY (Pat_ID) REFERENCES Patient(Pat_ID) ); -- R6: Multivalued phone CREATE TABLE Patient_Phone ( Pat_ID VARCHAR(15), phone VARCHAR(15), PRIMARY KEY (Pat_ID, phone), FOREIGN KEY (Pat_ID) REFERENCES Patient(Pat_ID) ); -- R1 + R4: Admission (associative entity linking Patient and Room) CREATE TABLE Admission ( admission_id VARCHAR(15) PRIMARY KEY, Pat_ID VARCHAR(15) NOT NULL, room_no VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL, admit_date DATE NOT NULL, discharge_date DATE, FOREIGN KEY (Pat_ID) REFERENCES Patient(Pat_ID), FOREIGN KEY (room_no) REFERENCES Room(room_no) ); ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **M:N Trap**: Students often treat `Doctor–Patient` as 1:N because "one doctor treats many patients" — but forget that "one patient can be treated by many doctors". This makes it **M:N** and **REQUIRES a separate `Treatment` table**. (Penalty: −2 marks) > 2. **Multivalued Attribute Neglect**: Forgetting to create `Patient_Phone` for the `phone` attribute. In SQL, this violates **1NF**. (Penalty: −1 mark) > 3. **Weak Entity Confusion**: Treating `Admission` as a strong entity. It is a strong entity here (it has its own PK `admission_id`), but `Book_Copy` in the previous question was a weak entity. Read the problem carefully! (Penalty: −1 mark) > 4. **Missing Cardinality Labels**: Drawing relationships without `1:1`, `1:N`, `M:N` labels. (Penalty: −1 mark) > 5. **FK Notation**: Writing `FOREIGN KEY` but forgetting to mark it as FK in the schema box. Always annotate with `FK` superscript. (Penalty: −1 mark) > 6. **Derived Attribute**: Storing `age` in the table. Derived attributes must be **computed at query time**, not stored. (Penalty: −1 mark) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 2** ### ER Modeling Essentials - ☐ **Entity = Noun**, **Attribute = Adjective**, **Relationship = Verb** - ☐ Primary key must be **underlined** in the ER diagram - ☐ **Multivalued** = double ellipse, **Derived** = dashed ellipse, **Composite** = ellipse with sub-ellipses - ☐ **Weak entity** = double rectangle, **Identifying relationship** = double diamond - ☐ **Cardinality** must be explicitly written: 1:1, 1:N, M:N - ☐ **Participation** must be shown: single line (partial) or double line (total) ### Mapping Rules (The 7 Commandments) - ☐ **R1** — Every strong entity → one table - ☐ **R2** — Weak entity → table with composite PK `(owner_PK, partial_key)` - ☐ **R3** — 1:1 relationship → merge (add FK to either side) - ☐ **R4** — 1:N relationship → add 1-side PK as FK on N-side - ☐ **R5** — M:N relationship → **new separate table** with composite PK - ☐ **R6** — Multivalued attribute → **new separate table** with `(entity_PK, attribute)` - ☐ **R7** — n-ary relationship (n ≥ 3) → new table with all participating PKs ### Schema Writing Best Practices - ☐ Always specify **PRIMARY KEY** and **FOREIGN KEY** constraints - ☐ Use **NOT NULL** for mandatory attributes (e.g., name, primary keys) - ☐ Use **CHECK** constraints for domain validation (e.g., `salary > 0`, `credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6`) - ☐ Choose appropriate **data types**: `VARCHAR`, `INT`, `DECIMAL`, `DATE`, `CHAR` - ☐ Composite primary keys are written as `PRIMARY KEY (col1, col2)` - ☐ Foreign key references must match the referenced table's column(s) ### KTU Exam-Specific Reminders - ☐ Draw ER diagram **before** writing the schema — **always** - ☐ Use **Chen's notation** by default (rectangles + diamonds + ellipses) - ☐ Label every relationship with a verb and cardinality - ☐ If using Crow's Foot, label with `|, O|, <, O<` symbols - ☐ For SQL DDL questions, include all constraints in `CREATE TABLE` statements - ☐ Do **not** include sample data (`INSERT` statements) unless asked - ☐ Verify normalization — the schema should be in **3NF** minimum - ☐ Common exam duration: 3 hours, full experiment should be solvable in 45 minutes ### Quick Memory Trick > 🎯 **"1:1 Merge, 1:N FK, M:N New"** — Repeat this 10 times before the exam. ### Bonus Conceptual Check - ☐ Can you identify a **recursive relationship**? (e.g., Employee supervises Employee) - ☐ Can you identify a **ternary relationship**? (e.g., Doctor prescribes Drug to Patient — all 3 entities) - ☐ Can you convert a **generalization/specialization** hierarchy to a schema? (Use "ISA" relationship)

topics.title: Creation of database schema - DDL (create tables, set constraints, enforce relationships, create indices, delete and modify tables). Export ER diagram from the database and verify relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of database schema - DDL (create tables, set constraints, enforce relationships, create indices, delete and modify tables). Export ER diagram from the database and verify relationships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme DBMS Laboratory (PCCSL408)**, *Experiment 2* focuses on the **Data Definition Language (DDL)** subset of SQL. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the objective of this experiment is to **create a database schema** using DDL statements, **define integrity constraints**, **enforce referential relationships**, **create indices** for performance, and to **modify or delete** existing schema objects. Subsequently, the learner is expected to **reverse-engineer an ER diagram** from the created database to visually verify the relationships.

**DDL (Data Definition Language)** is the subset of SQL used to define, alter, and remove database structures (schemas, tables, views, indexes). Unlike DML (Data Manipulation Language), DDL commands are **auto-committed** — their effect is permanent and cannot be rolled back without an explicit backup.

The principal DDL commands mandated by this experiment are:

| Command | Purpose | Auto-Commit? |
|---|---|---|
| `CREATE DATABASE` | Creates a new database container | Yes |
| `CREATE TABLE` | Defines a new table and its columns | Yes |
| `ALTER TABLE` | Modifies an existing table structure | Yes |
| `DROP TABLE` | Permanently deletes a table | Yes |
| `TRUNCATE TABLE` | Removes all rows but keeps structure | Yes |
| `CREATE INDEX` | Builds a search-acceleration structure | Yes |
| `DROP INDEX` | Removes an index | Yes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For the lab record and university practical examination, students are expected to (a) write the schema for a mini-world problem (such as *Library*, *Hospital*, *Student-Course*, *Banking*, or *Inventory*), (b) implement it in **MySQL / Oracle / PostgreSQL**, (c) verify it with `DESC table_name;` or equivalent, and (d) generate the ER diagram using tools like **MySQL Workbench (Reverse Engineer)**, **DBeaver**, or **dbdiagram.io**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building a Filing Cabinet" Model

Imagine a **company office** that wants to store employee records. Before the clerk can write any name into a register (DML), the manager must first:

1. **Buy a cabinet** → `CREATE DATABASE company;`
2. **Install drawers** (e.g., *Employees*, *Departments*, *Projects*) → `CREATE TABLE ...`
3. **Label every column and decide the data type** (Name = text, Salary = number) → column definitions with datatypes.
4. **Set rules** — *Salary cannot be negative*, *Employee ID cannot be duplicated*, *Every department must have a unique code* → **constraints** (`CHECK`, `PRIMARY KEY`, `UNIQUE`).
5. **Create a link** between drawers — e.g., a "Project Assignment" drawer must always reference a real employee → **foreign key relationships**.
6. **Add a quick-lookup index tab** on the most-searched column (say, *Last Name*) → `CREATE INDEX`.
7. Later, if a drawer is renamed or a column is added → `ALTER TABLE`.
8. If a drawer is no longer needed → `DROP TABLE`.

Once the cabinet is fully built, the auditor can take photographs of all the drawers and the wiring between them to produce an **ER diagram** — verifying that the rules were installed correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** *DDL is to the database what a blueprint is to a building. It defines the structure, not the contents.*

---

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Evaluation

The KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory evaluation for PCCSL408 follows a **continuous assessment + end-semester practical** model. Experiment 2 typically tests:

- **CO2 (Apply):** Ability to design a normalized schema from a given problem statement.
- **CO3 (Analyze):** Choosing correct constraints, datatypes, and referential actions.
- **CO4 (Create):** Implementing the design and verifying it via ER reverse-engineering.

Mastering DDL is foundational — every later experiment (DML, joins, views, triggers) depends on a well-defined schema.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The DDL Command Family — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 `CREATE DATABASE` and `USE`

A **database** in MySQL/Oracle is a logical container of related tables, views, procedures, and indexes.

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS college_db;
USE college_db;
```

`IF NOT EXISTS` prevents an error if the database already exists — a KTU-exam best practice.

### 2.1.2 `CREATE TABLE` — The Core Construct

The general syntactic skeleton is:

```sql
CREATE TABLE table_name (
    column1 datatype [constraint],
    column2 datatype [constraint],
    ...
    [table-level constraint]
);
```

### 2.1.3 Datatypes (KTU High-Yield List)

| Category | Datatypes | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Integer | `INT`, `SMALLINT`, `BIGINT`, `TINYINT` | Use `INT` for IDs |
| Decimal | `DECIMAL(p,s)`, `FLOAT`, `DOUBLE` | `DECIMAL(8,2)` for currency |
| Character | `CHAR(n)`, `VARCHAR(n)`, `TEXT` | `VARCHAR` is variable-length |
| Date/Time | `DATE`, `TIME`, `DATETIME`, `TIMESTAMP`, `YEAR` | Format: `YYYY-MM-DD` |
| Boolean | `BOOLEAN` (alias of `TINYINT(1)`) | MySQL specific |
| Binary | `BLOB`, `MEDIUMBLOB`, `LONGBLOB` | For images/files |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oracle vs MySQL Pitfall:** Oracle uses `VARCHAR2(n)` instead of `VARCHAR(n)`, and `NUMBER(p,s)` instead of `DECIMAL`. Always declare the target RDBMS at the top of your lab record.

### 2.1.4 Integrity Constraints — The Five Pillars

Constraints are **rules enforced by the DBMS** to maintain data integrity. They are the heart of this experiment.

| # | Constraint | Purpose | Example Syntax |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PRIMARY KEY** | Uniquely identifies each row; NOT NULL + UNIQUE | `sid INT PRIMARY KEY` |
| 2 | **FOREIGN KEY** | Enforces referential integrity between tables | `FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)` |
| 3 | **UNIQUE** | Prevents duplicate values; allows multiple NULLs | `email VARCHAR(60) UNIQUE` |
| 4 | **NOT NULL** | Disallows empty values | `name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL` |
| 5 | **CHECK** | Validates a domain/range condition | `CHECK (marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100)` |
| 6 | **DEFAULT** | Provides a fallback value | `status VARCHAR(10) DEFAULT 'Active'` |
| 7 | **AUTO_INCREMENT** | Auto-generates sequential values (MySQL) | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` |

### 2.1.5 Referential Actions (ON DELETE / ON UPDATE)

When a parent row is deleted or updated, the foreign-key child row behaviour is governed by:

- `CASCADE` — propagate the change (delete/update child rows).
- `SET NULL` — set child FK to NULL (requires FK to be nullable).
- `RESTRICT` / `NO ACTION` — reject the parent change if children exist (default in most RDBMS).
- `SET DEFAULT` — set child FK to its default value.

```sql
FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE
    ON UPDATE CASCADE;
```

### 2.1.6 `CREATE INDEX` — Performance Tuning

An **index** is a separate data structure (typically a B+ tree) that allows the DBMS to locate rows faster than a full table scan.

```sql
-- Simple index
CREATE INDEX idx_student_name ON Student(sname);

-- Unique index
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_student_email ON Student(email);

-- Composite index (multi-column)
CREATE INDEX idx_student_dept_year ON Student(dept_id, year);

-- Drop index
DROP INDEX idx_student_name ON Student;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not index every column?** Each index consumes storage and slows down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes indexing only on **search-heavy, join-heavy, and WHERE-clause-heavy** columns.

### 2.1.7 `ALTER TABLE` — Schema Evolution

```sql
-- Add a column
ALTER TABLE Student ADD phone VARCHAR(15);

-- Modify a column
ALTER TABLE Student MODIFY sname VARCHAR(60) NOT NULL;

-- Rename a column
ALTER TABLE Student RENAME COLUMN phone TO mobile;

-- Drop a column
ALTER TABLE Student DROP COLUMN mobile;

-- Add a constraint after table creation
ALTER TABLE Student ADD CONSTRAINT chk_marks CHECK (marks >= 0 AND marks <= 100);

-- Rename a table
ALTER TABLE Student RENAME TO Student_Info;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Oracle syntax differs:** `ALTER TABLE Student MODIFY (sname VARCHAR2(60) NOT NULL);` and `RENAME TO` is `RENAME Student TO Student_Info;`.

### 2.1.8 `DROP TABLE` vs `TRUNCATE TABLE` vs `DELETE`

| Operation | Removes Structure? | Removes Data? | Can Rollback? | Fires Triggers? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `DROP TABLE` | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `TRUNCATE TABLE` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (all) | ❌ No (DDL) | ❌ No |
| `DELETE FROM` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (selective) | ✅ Yes (DML) | ✅ Yes |

```sql
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Old_Student;
TRUNCATE TABLE Temp_Log;
```

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield DDL Cheat Sheet

| Feature | MySQL Syntax | Oracle Syntax |
|---|---|---|
| Auto-increment | `id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY` | `id NUMBER GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY` |
| String | `VARCHAR(n)` | `VARCHAR2(n)` |
| Decimal | `DECIMAL(p,s)` | `NUMBER(p,s)` |
| Date | `DATE` (with time) | `DATE` (date+time) |
| Drop table | `DROP TABLE t;` | `DROP TABLE t CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;` (if FK exists) |
| View structure | `DESC t;` | `DESCRIBE t;` |
| Show FKs | `SHOW CREATE TABLE t\G` | `SELECT * FROM USER_CONSTRAINTS WHERE TABLE_NAME='T';` |

## 2.3 Reverse-Engineering the ER Diagram — Real-World Utility

After implementing the schema, the KTU lab manual requires an **ER diagram export**. The workflow is:

1. **MySQL Workbench:** Database → *Reverse Engineer* → select schema → tool generates EER diagram showing all tables, columns, PK/FK, and crow's-foot relationships.
2. **DBeaver:** Right-click schema → *View Diagram*.
3. **dbdiagram.io:** Use the *Import from MySQL* feature with the generated DDL.
4. **Oracle SQL Developer:** File → *Data Modeler* → *Import* → *Data Dictionary*.

The exported diagram must be **visually inspected** to verify:
- All entities (tables) are present.
- Primary keys are underlined.
- Foreign-key links exist between parent and child tables.
- Cardinality (1:1, 1:N, M:N via junction table) is correct.
- No orphan tables (tables with no relationship).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Reverse-engineering is a critical skill in **legacy database migration**, **system refactoring**, and **audit compliance** (e.g., understanding the data model of a 20-year-old banking system before modernization).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

In this section, we build a complete **University Course Enrollment** schema — a classic KTU lab problem — step by step. Every SQL block is annotated with the *intent* of the statement, mirroring the **lab record viva expectations**.

## 3.1 Problem Statement

> Design a database for a university with the following requirements:
> - A **Department** has a unique name, a department code, and a head (professor name).
> - A **Student** belongs to exactly one Department, has a unique roll number, name, date of birth, email, and current semester.
> - A **Course** is offered by one Department, has a unique course code, title, and credits.
> - A **Faculty** member belongs to one Department, has a unique ID, name, designation, and salary.
> - A **Faculty** can teach multiple **Courses**.
> - A **Student** can enroll in many **Courses** and vice versa — track enrollment date and grade.
> - Marks obtained by a student in a course must be between 0 and 100.
> - Salary of faculty must be greater than zero.
> - Email of student and faculty must be unique.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step DDL Implementation

### Step 1 — Create and Select the Database

```sql
-- Create a fresh database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS UniversityDB;

-- Activate it
USE UniversityDB;
```

> **Logic:** The `IF NOT EXISTS` clause is a defensive practice — it prevents an *Error 1007: Can't create database 'UniversityDB'; database exists* in the lab evaluation environment.

### Step 2 — Create the Parent Table: `Department`

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id      INT             PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name    VARCHAR(50)     NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    dept_code    CHAR(5)         NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    head_name    VARCHAR(60)     NOT NULL
);
```

> **Logic:**
> - `dept_id` is the **surrogate primary key** — small, integer, immutable.
> - `dept_name` and `dept_code` are `UNIQUE` because a university cannot have two CS departments.
> - `head_name` is `NOT NULL` because every department must have a head.

### Step 3 — Create the `Faculty` Table (FK to Department)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Faculty (
    faculty_id   INT             PRIMARY KEY,
    fname        VARCHAR(50)     NOT NULL,
    designation  VARCHAR(30)     NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Assistant Professor',
    salary       DECIMAL(10,2)   NOT NULL,
    email        VARCHAR(80)     NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    dept_id      INT             NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT  fk_fac_dept
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  chk_fac_salary
        CHECK (salary > 0)
);
```

> **Logic:**
> - The **named constraint** `fk_fac_dept` improves readability and easier `ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT` later.
> - `ON DELETE RESTRICT` prevents deleting a department that has faculty assigned — a sensible business rule.
> - `CHECK (salary > 0)` enforces the third business rule.

### Step 4 — Create the `Student` Table (FK to Department)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Student (
    roll_no      INT             PRIMARY KEY,
    sname        VARCHAR(50)     NOT NULL,
    dob          DATE            NOT NULL,
    email        VARCHAR(80)     NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    semester     INT             NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
    dept_id      INT             NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT  fk_stu_dept
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  chk_stu_sem
        CHECK (semester BETWEEN 1 AND 12)
);
```

> **Logic:**
> - `dob DATE` uses the canonical `YYYY-MM-DD` format.
> - `semester` is bounded between 1 and 12.
> - The FK uses **table-level** constraint syntax — useful when the constraint spans multiple columns.

### Step 5 — Create the `Course` Table (FK to Department)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Course (
    course_code  VARCHAR(10)     PRIMARY KEY,
    ctitle       VARCHAR(80)     NOT NULL,
    credits      INT             NOT NULL,
    dept_id      INT             NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT  fk_course_dept
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  chk_course_credits
        CHECK (credits BETWEEN 1 AND 6)
);
```

> **Logic:** A natural key (`course_code` like `CSL201`) is used as the primary key because it is short, unique, and meaningful.

### Step 6 — Create the Junction Table: `Faculty_Course` (M:N Relationship)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Faculty_Course (
    faculty_id   INT             NOT NULL,
    course_code  VARCHAR(10)     NOT NULL,
    academic_year VARCHAR(9)    NOT NULL,    -- e.g., '2024-2025'
    PRIMARY KEY (faculty_id, course_code, academic_year),
    CONSTRAINT  fk_fc_fac
        FOREIGN KEY (faculty_id) REFERENCES Faculty(faculty_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  fk_fc_course
        FOREIGN KEY (course_code) REFERENCES Course(course_code)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE
);
```

> **Logic:** A **composite primary key** ensures a faculty member cannot be assigned the same course twice in the same academic year. M:N relationships are modelled via a **junction (associative) table**.

### Step 7 — Create the Junction Table: `Enrollment` (Student–Course M:N)

```sql
CREATE TABLE Enrollment (
    roll_no       INT            NOT NULL,
    course_code   VARCHAR(10)    NOT NULL,
    enroll_date   DATE           NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    marks         INT            DEFAULT NULL,
    grade         CHAR(2)        DEFAULT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, course_code),
    CONSTRAINT  fk_en_stu
        FOREIGN KEY (roll_no) REFERENCES Student(roll_no)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  fk_en_course
        FOREIGN KEY (course_code) REFERENCES Course(course_code)
        ON DELETE CASCADE
        ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT  chk_en_marks
        CHECK (marks IS NULL OR (marks BETWEEN 0 AND 100))
);
```

> **Logic:** Marks are allowed to be `NULL` (grade not yet published) but bounded between 0–100 once entered. `CURRENT_DATE` is the default enrollment timestamp.

### Step 8 — Create Indices for Performance

```sql
-- Frequently searched columns
CREATE INDEX idx_student_name  ON Student(sname);
CREATE INDEX idx_faculty_name  ON Faculty(fname);
CREATE INDEX idx_course_dept   ON Course(dept_id);

-- Composite index for common JOIN+WHERE combinations
CREATE INDEX idx_enroll_grade ON Enrollment(grade, marks);
```

> **Logic:** We do **not** index primary-key columns (DBMS does that automatically) or foreign-key columns unnecessarily (FKs in MySQL/InnoDB are auto-indexed).

### Step 9 — Demonstrate Schema Modification (`ALTER TABLE`)

```sql
-- Add a new column to Student
ALTER TABLE Student ADD phone VARCHAR(15);

-- Modify the phone column to be NOT NULL
ALTER TABLE Student MODIFY phone VARCHAR(15) NOT NULL;

-- Rename the column
ALTER TABLE Student RENAME COLUMN phone TO mobile_no;

-- Add a CHECK constraint post-creation
ALTER TABLE Faculty
    ADD CONSTRAINT chk_fac_designation
    CHECK (designation IN ('Assistant Professor','Associate Professor','Professor','HOD'));

-- Drop the column we just added
ALTER TABLE Student DROP COLUMN mobile_no;
```

> **Logic:** `ALTER TABLE` operations in MySQL are **metadata operations**; they rewrite the table's `.ibd` file (in InnoDB) but the data is preserved.

### Step 10 — Demonstrate Deletion (`DROP` / `TRUNCATE`)

```sql
-- Create a temporary table for demo
CREATE TABLE Temp_Log (
    log_id   INT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    message  VARCHAR(200),
    log_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Remove all rows, keep structure
TRUNCATE TABLE Temp_Log;

-- Drop the table entirely
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Temp_Log;
```

> **Logic:** `TRUNCATE` resets `AUTO_INCREMENT` to 1; `DROP` removes the table file from the database directory.

## 3.3 Verification Block — Run These to Confirm Schema

```sql
-- View table structure
DESC Student;
DESC Faculty;

-- View all tables
SHOW TABLES;

-- View constraint details
SELECT CONSTRAINT_NAME, CONSTRAINT_TYPE, TABLE_NAME
FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_CONSTRAINTS
WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'UniversityDB';

-- View foreign keys explicitly
SELECT
    TABLE_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, CONSTRAINT_NAME,
    REFERENCED_TABLE_NAME, REFERENCED_COLUMN_NAME
FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.KEY_COLUMN_USAGE
WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA = 'UniversityDB'
  AND REFERENCED_TABLE_NAME IS NOT NULL;
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each DDL Element

| Decision | Choose | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Surrogate vs Natural PK? | **Surrogate INT AUTO_INCREMENT** (e.g., `roll_no`, `dept_id`) | Stability, performance, immutability |
| `CHAR` vs `VARCHAR`? | `CHAR` for fixed-length codes (`dept_code`), `VARCHAR` for names | Storage efficiency |
| `CASCADE` vs `RESTRICT`? | `CASCADE` for child-dependent rows (Enrollment), `RESTRICT` for organizational parent (Department) | Preserve historical data vs prevent orphans |
| When to add `INDEX`? | On columns used in `WHERE`, `JOIN`, `ORDER BY` | Avoid full table scans |
| `CHECK` vs `TRIGGER`? | `CHECK` for simple domain rules, `TRIGGER` for cross-table business logic | Constraint engine is faster |
| `DROP` vs `TRUNCATE`? | `DROP` for full removal, `TRUNCATE` for resetting test data | `DROP` is more destructive |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ER Diagram of the UniversityDB Schema

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **logical ER model** of the UniversityDB schema we built in Section 3. Every relationship is annotated with its **cardinality** (1:1, 1:N, M:N).

```mermaid
erDiagram
    DEPARTMENT {
        INT     dept_id PK
        VARCHAR dept_name
        CHAR    dept_code
        VARCHAR head_name
    }

    STUDENT {
        INT     roll_no PK
        VARCHAR sname
        DATE    dob
        VARCHAR email
        INT     semester
        INT     dept_id FK
    }

    FACULTY {
        INT      faculty_id PK
        VARCHAR  fname
        VARCHAR  designation
        DECIMAL  salary
        VARCHAR  email
        INT      dept_id FK
    }

    COURSE {
        VARCHAR course_code PK
        VARCHAR ctitle
        INT     credits
        INT     dept_id FK
    }

    FACULTY_COURSE {
        INT     faculty_id PK_FK
        VARCHAR course_code PK_FK
        VARCHAR academic_year PK
    }

    ENROLLMENT {
        INT     roll_no PK_FK
        VARCHAR course_code PK_FK
        DATE    enroll_date
        INT     marks
        CHAR    grade
    }

    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ STUDENT       : "has many"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ FACULTY       : "employs"
    DEPARTMENT ||--o{ COURSE        : "offers"
    FACULTY    ||--o{ FACULTY_COURSE : "teaches"
    COURSE     ||--o{ FACULTY_COURSE : "is taught by"
    STUDENT    ||--o{ ENROLLMENT    : "enrolls in"
    COURSE     ||--o{ ENROLLMENT    : "has"
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - `||` (double bar) = "exactly one" (mandatory participation).
> - `o{` (circle + crow's foot) = "zero or many".
> - The `Faculty_Course` and `Enrollment` tables are **associative entities** that resolve the M:N relationships.

## 4.2 DDL Command-Category Block Diagram

This block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of DDL — its categories, their responsibilities, and the inter-dependencies (akin to a module map in a software architecture course).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DDL Command Family] --> B[Schema Definition]
    A --> C[Schema Modification]
    A --> D[Schema Removal]
    A --> E[Index Management]

    B --> B1[CREATE DATABASE]
    B --> B2[CREATE TABLE<br/>with constraints]
    B --> B3[CREATE VIEW]
    B --> B4[CREATE INDEX]

    C --> C1[ALTER TABLE ADD]
    C --> C2[ALTER TABLE MODIFY]
    C --> C3[ALTER TABLE DROP]
    C --> C4[ALTER TABLE RENAME]

    D --> D1[DROP DATABASE]
    D --> D2[DROP TABLE]
    D --> D3[TRUNCATE TABLE]
    D --> D4[DROP INDEX]

    E --> E1[CREATE INDEX]
    E --> E2[CREATE UNIQUE INDEX]
    E --> E3[CREATE COMPOSITE INDEX]
    E --> E4[DROP INDEX]

    B2 -.enforces.-> F[Integrity Constraints]
    F --> F1[PRIMARY KEY]
    F --> F2[FOREIGN KEY]
    F --> F3[UNIQUE / NOT NULL]
    F --> F4[CHECK / DEFAULT]

    C --> G[Reverse Engineer ER]
    G --> G1[MySQL Workbench]
    G --> G2[DBeaver]
    G --> G3[dbdiagram.io]
    G --> G4[Oracle Data Modeler]
```

## 4.3 Schema-Creation Workflow Flowchart

This flowchart models the **operational sequence** a KTU student must follow during the lab exam to construct a schema from a problem statement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step1: Read Problem Statement] --> P2[Step2: Identify Entities]
    P2 --> P3[Step3: Identify Attributes and Datatypes]
    P3 --> P4[Step4: Identify Primary Keys]
    P4 --> P5[Step5: Identify Relationships and Cardinality]
    P5 --> P6[Step6: Resolve M:N into Junction Tables]
    P6 --> P7[Step7: Define Foreign Keys with Actions]
    P7 --> P8[Step8: Apply CHECK UNIQUE NOT NULL]
    P8 --> P9[Step9: Create Indices on Search Columns]
    P9 --> P10[Step10: Implement in RDBMS]
    P10 --> P11[Step11: Verify with DESC and SHOW]
    P11 --> P12[Step12: Reverse-Engineer ER Diagram]
    P12 --> P13[Step13: Cross-Check with Problem Statement]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - PCCSL408 Model Paper 1, Q1.a]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between `DROP TABLE`, `TRUNCATE TABLE`, and `DELETE FROM` statements in SQL. State one scenario for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `DROP TABLE` | `TRUNCATE TABLE` | `DELETE FROM` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removes structure? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Removes data? | ✅ All rows | ✅ All rows | ✅ Selected rows (via `WHERE`) |
| Rollback possible? | ❌ No (DDL) | ❌ No (DDL) | ✅ Yes (DML) |
| Fires `DELETE` trigger? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (per row) |
| Resets `AUTO_INCREMENT`? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Speed | Fastest | Very fast | Slowest (per-row logging) |

**Scenarios:**
- `DROP TABLE` — Removing an obsolete table like `Old_Employee_2020` from production.
- `TRUNCATE TABLE` — Resetting a `Temp_Log` or staging table before a fresh ETL run.
- `DELETE FROM` — Removing specific rows, e.g., `DELETE FROM Student WHERE semester > 8;`

---

> **[KTU University Exam - PCCSL408 Model Paper 2, Q1.b]**
> **Q2. What is the difference between a `PRIMARY KEY` and a `UNIQUE` constraint? Can a table have multiple `UNIQUE` constraints? Justify with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `PRIMARY KEY` | `UNIQUE` |
|---|---|---|
| NULL allowed? | ❌ Never | ✅ One NULL per column (DBMS-dependent) |
| Number per table | Exactly **one** | **Multiple** allowed |
| Creates clustered index? | Usually yes | Usually no (non-clustered) |
| Purpose | Row identity | Business uniqueness |

**Example:** In a `Faculty` table, `faculty_id` is the `PRIMARY KEY`; `email` and `aadhar_no` can both be `UNIQUE` — preventing two faculty from sharing the same email or Aadhaar number, while still allowing exactly one row identifier.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - PCCSL408 End-Sem Practical Model, Q2]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> Design a database schema for an **Online Bookstore** with the following requirements. Write complete DDL statements with appropriate constraints, demonstrate `ALTER` and `DROP` operations, and create at least two indices.
>
> **Requirements:**
> - `Publisher` has publisher_id (PK), name, country, contact_email.
> - `Author` has author_id (PK), name, nationality, dob.
> - `Book` has isbn (PK), title, price, publication_year, publisher_id (FK).
> - `Book_Author` resolves the M:N relationship between Book and Author (with role — e.g., 'Lead', 'Co-Author').
> - `Customer` has customer_id (PK), name, email (unique), phone, registration_date.
> - `Order` has order_id (PK), order_date, total_amount, customer_id (FK), with status defaulting to 'Pending'.
> - `Order_Item` resolves the M:N between Order and Book, with quantity and unit_price.
> - Price must be greater than 0; quantity must be ≥ 1; total_amount must be ≥ 0.

### (a) — 7 Marks: Write the complete DDL with constraints

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS BookstoreDB;
USE BookstoreDB;

-- Publisher (parent)
CREATE TABLE Publisher (
    pub_id        INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    pub_name      VARCHAR(80)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    country       VARCHAR(40)  NOT NULL,
    contact_email VARCHAR(80)  NOT NULL UNIQUE
);

-- Author (parent)
CREATE TABLE Author (
    author_id    INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    aname        VARCHAR(60)  NOT NULL,
    nationality  VARCHAR(30),
    dob          DATE
);

-- Book (FK to Publisher)
CREATE TABLE Book (
    isbn             VARCHAR(13)    PRIMARY KEY,
    title            VARCHAR(150)   NOT NULL,
    price            DECIMAL(8,2)   NOT NULL,
    publication_year INT            NOT NULL,
    pub_id           INT            NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT fk_book_pub
        FOREIGN KEY (pub_id) REFERENCES Publisher(pub_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_book_price
        CHECK (price > 0),
    CONSTRAINT chk_book_year
        CHECK (publication_year BETWEEN 1450 AND 2100)
);

-- Book_Author (M:N junction)
CREATE TABLE Book_Author (
    isbn       VARCHAR(13)   NOT NULL,
    author_id  INT           NOT NULL,
    role       VARCHAR(20)   NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Author',
    PRIMARY KEY (isbn, author_id),
    CONSTRAINT fk_ba_book
        FOREIGN KEY (isbn) REFERENCES Book(isbn)
        ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT fk_ba_author
        FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES Author(author_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_ba_role
        CHECK (role IN ('Lead','Co-Author','Editor','Author'))
);

-- Customer (independent)
CREATE TABLE Customer (
    cust_id           INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    cname             VARCHAR(60)  NOT NULL,
    email             VARCHAR(80)  NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    phone             VARCHAR(15)  NOT NULL,
    registration_date DATE         NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)
);

-- Order (FK to Customer)
CREATE TABLE Orders (
    order_id     INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    order_date   DATE         NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    total_amount DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
    cust_id      INT          NOT NULL,
    status       VARCHAR(15)  NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Pending',
    CONSTRAINT fk_ord_cust
        FOREIGN KEY (cust_id) REFERENCES Customer(cust_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_ord_total
        CHECK (total_amount >= 0),
    CONSTRAINT chk_ord_status
        CHECK (status IN ('Pending','Shipped','Delivered','Cancelled'))
);

-- Order_Item (M:N junction with payload)
CREATE TABLE Order_Item (
    order_id    INT           NOT NULL,
    isbn        VARCHAR(13)   NOT NULL,
    quantity    INT           NOT NULL,
    unit_price  DECIMAL(8,2)  NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, isbn),
    CONSTRAINT fk_oi_order
        FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES Orders(order_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT fk_oi_book
        FOREIGN KEY (isbn) REFERENCES Book(isbn)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_oi_qty
        CHECK (quantity >= 1),
    CONSTRAINT chk_oi_price
        CHECK (unit_price > 0)
);
```

### (b) — 7 Marks: Demonstrate ALTER, DROP, and INDEX operations

```sql
-- (i) Create indices
CREATE INDEX idx_book_title      ON Book(title);
CREATE INDEX idx_book_pub_year   ON Book(pub_id, publication_year);
CREATE INDEX idx_order_cust      ON Orders(cust_id);
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_cust_email ON Customer(email);

-- (ii) ALTER operations
-- Add a 'pages' column to Book
ALTER TABLE Book ADD pages INT;

-- Modify the 'pages' column to NOT NULL with a default
ALTER TABLE Book MODIFY pages INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 100;

-- Add a CHECK constraint to Customer
ALTER TABLE Customer
    ADD CONSTRAINT chk_cust_phone
    CHECK (LENGTH(phone) >= 10);

-- Rename the 'status' column of Orders
ALTER TABLE Orders RENAME COLUMN status TO order_status;

-- Rename the entire 'Orders' table to 'CustomerOrder'
ALTER TABLE Orders RENAME TO CustomerOrder;

-- (iii) DROP operations
-- Drop a specific index
DROP INDEX idx_book_title ON Book;

-- Drop the temporary pages column
ALTER TABLE Book DROP COLUMN pages;

-- Drop a CHECK constraint
ALTER TABLE Customer DROP CONSTRAINT chk_cust_phone;

-- Drop the entire CustomerOrder table
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS CustomerOrder;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Online Bookstore Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to declare a FOREIGN KEY with the same datatype and width** as the referenced PK (e.g., `isbn VARCHAR(13)` in both `Book` and `Order_Item`). This causes `ERROR 1215 (HY000): Cannot add foreign key constraint`.
> 2. **Using `CASCADE` on `Book → Publisher`** — if you delete a publisher, all their books are wiped out, losing order history. Use `RESTRICT` and reassign books first.
> 3. **Forgetting `IF EXISTS` in `DROP TABLE`** — causes a syntax error if rerun.
> 4. **Indexing the primary key** — the DBMS does it automatically; doing it again is wasteful and earns negative marks.
> 5. **In MySQL, `CHECK` constraints are ignored in older versions (< 8.0.16).** The KTU lab environment should be checked for version. Use a `BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE` trigger as a fallback if needed.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - PCCSL408 End-Sem Practical Model, Q2 Alternative]**
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> Design a database schema for a **Hospital Management System** with the following requirements. Write complete DDL with constraints, perform schema modification, and create appropriate indices.
>
> **Requirements:**
> - `Department` (dept_id PK, dname, location, head_doctor_id).
> - `Doctor` (doctor_id PK, dname, specialization, salary, dept_id FK, join_date).
> - `Patient` (patient_id PK, pname, age, gender, admission_date, disease, dept_id FK).
> - `Room` (room_no PK, room_type, charges_per_day, status).
> - `Admission` (admission_id PK, patient_id FK, room_no FK, admit_date, discharge_date, total_bill).
> - Constraints: doctor salary > 0; patient age between 0 and 130; room charges > 0; total_bill ≥ 0; one doctor can head at most one department.

### (a) — 7 Marks: DDL with Constraints

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS HospitalDB;
USE HospitalDB;

CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id          INT         PRIMARY KEY,
    dname            VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    location         VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    head_doctor_id   INT
);

CREATE TABLE Doctor (
    doctor_id     INT            PRIMARY KEY,
    dname         VARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,
    specialization VARCHAR(40)   NOT NULL,
    salary        DECIMAL(10,2)  NOT NULL,
    dept_id       INT            NOT NULL,
    join_date     DATE           NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    CONSTRAINT fk_doc_dept
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_doc_salary
        CHECK (salary > 0)
);

-- Now add the head_doctor FK back to Department (self-reference)
ALTER TABLE Department
    ADD CONSTRAINT fk_dept_head
    FOREIGN KEY (head_doctor_id) REFERENCES Doctor(doctor_id)
    ON DELETE SET NULL ON UPDATE CASCADE;

-- Add UNIQUE constraint to enforce "one doctor heads at most one department"
ALTER TABLE Department
    ADD CONSTRAINT uq_dept_head UNIQUE (head_doctor_id);

CREATE TABLE Patient (
    patient_id     INT          PRIMARY KEY,
    pname          VARCHAR(60)  NOT NULL,
    age            INT          NOT NULL,
    gender         CHAR(1)      NOT NULL,
    admission_date DATE         NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    disease        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    dept_id        INT          NOT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT fk_pat_dept
        FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_pat_age
        CHECK (age BETWEEN 0 AND 130),
    CONSTRAINT chk_pat_gender
        CHECK (gender IN ('M','F','O'))
);

CREATE TABLE Room (
    room_no         INT            PRIMARY KEY,
    room_type       VARCHAR(20)    NOT NULL,
    charges_per_day DECIMAL(8,2)   NOT NULL,
    status          VARCHAR(15)    NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Available',
    CONSTRAINT chk_room_charges
        CHECK (charges_per_day > 0),
    CONSTRAINT chk_room_status
        CHECK (status IN ('Available','Occupied','Maintenance'))
);

CREATE TABLE Admission (
    admission_id    INT            PRIMARY KEY,
    patient_id      INT            NOT NULL,
    room_no         INT            NOT NULL,
    admit_date      DATE           NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    discharge_date  DATE           DEFAULT NULL,
    total_bill      DECIMAL(10,2)  DEFAULT NULL,
    CONSTRAINT fk_adm_pat
        FOREIGN KEY (patient_id) REFERENCES Patient(patient_id)
        ON DELETE CASCADE ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT fk_adm_room
        FOREIGN KEY (room_no) REFERENCES Room(room_no)
        ON DELETE RESTRICT ON UPDATE CASCADE,
    CONSTRAINT chk_adm_bill
        CHECK (total_bill IS NULL OR total_bill >= 0)
);
```

### (b) — 7 Marks: ALTER, DROP, INDEX

```sql
-- Indices
CREATE INDEX idx_doc_specialization ON Doctor(specialization);
CREATE INDEX idx_pat_disease       ON Patient(disease);
CREATE INDEX idx_adm_admit_date    ON Admission(admit_date);

-- ALTER: add 'phone' to Patient
ALTER TABLE Patient ADD phone VARCHAR(15);

-- ALTER: enforce NOT NULL on disease
ALTER TABLE Patient MODIFY disease VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL;

-- ALTER: rename Department.location to building
ALTER TABLE Department RENAME COLUMN location TO building;

-- DROP index
DROP INDEX idx_doc_specialization ON Doctor;

-- DROP column
ALTER TABLE Patient DROP COLUMN phone;

-- DROP table
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Admission;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Hospital Question:**
> 1. **Circular FK problem:** `Department.head_doctor_id → Doctor` and `Doctor.dept_id → Department` form a cycle. You **must** create one of them first without the FK, then `ALTER TABLE` to add it.
> 2. **Forgetting the `UNIQUE` constraint on `head_doctor_id`** — without it, one doctor could head multiple departments, violating the business rule.
> 3. **`ON DELETE CASCADE` on Admission** — fine, because admission depends on the patient. But **`CASCADE` on Room** would erase all admission history if a room is deleted — use `RESTRICT`.
> 4. **Confusing `CHAR(1)` for gender with `VARCHAR(6)`** — gender is a single code letter; using `VARCHAR` wastes storage and slows joins.
> 5. **Discharge date before admit date** — no `CHECK` constraint was added. In a real system, use a `BEFORE INSERT` trigger or application-level validation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Revision Points — Module 2 (DDL & Schema Creation)**

- ✅ **DDL is auto-committed** — always wrap critical DDL inside a transaction (where supported) or backup the schema first.
- ✅ **Use `IF EXISTS` / `IF NOT EXISTS`** to make scripts re-runnable.
- ✅ **Parent tables must be created before child tables**; drop child tables first or use `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` (Oracle).
- ✅ **FK column datatype and width must EXACTLY match** the referenced PK column.
- ✅ **Composite primary keys** are used in junction tables to resolve M:N relationships.
- ✅ **`CHECK` constraints** are enforced in MySQL 8.0.16+; use triggers in older versions.
- ✅ **`ON DELETE CASCADE`** is appropriate for **dependent child** rows; **`RESTRICT`** is appropriate for **organizational parents**.
- ✅ **Index** only on frequently searched / joined columns; do not index small lookup tables or high-write tables.
- ✅ **`DESC table_name;`** (MySQL) or **`DESCRIBE table_name;`** (Oracle) verifies schema.
- ✅ **Reverse-Engineer the ER diagram** using MySQL Workbench → *Database → Reverse Engineer*; visually verify that every FK line exists and the cardinality matches the problem statement.
- ✅ **Naming convention:** Use `fk_<child>_<parent>` for foreign keys and `chk_<table>_<column>` for checks — improves viva score.
- ✅ **Lab Record Tip:** Always include the `SHOW CREATE TABLE <name>` output in your record — it is the single most KTU-expected verification artifact.
- ✅ **Common MySQL errors to recognize:**
    - `ERROR 1062` → duplicate key (PK/UNIQUE violation).
    - `ERROR 1215` → FK column mismatch.
    - `ERROR 1451` → cannot delete/update parent row (RESTRICT violation).
    - `ERROR 1452` → cannot add child row (FK reference missing).
- ✅ **For the viva:** Be ready to explain *why* you chose `CASCADE` vs `RESTRICT` for each FK, and *why* you indexed (or did not index) a specific column.

---

> **End of Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank**
