- In MySQL, `REVOKE ... FROM user;` is sufficient.

### 2.3.3 The Two-Tier Privilege Model in Oracle

| Tier | Statement Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **System Privileges** | Rights to perform actions on the DB engine itself | `CREATE TABLE`, `CREATE USER`, `CREATE SESSION` |
| **Object Privileges** | Rights to perform actions on a specific object | `SELECT ON emp`, `UPDATE ON dept` |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Syntax Rule | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transaction Boundary** | Begins at first DML (or at `COMMIT`/`ROLLBACK` of previous tx); Ends at `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK` / DDL statement | Banking ATM — entire withdrawal is one atomic transaction |
| 2 | **ACID Check** | $\text{ACID} = \{\text{Atomicity},\ \text{Consistency},\ \text{Isolation},\ \text{Durability}\}$ | Stock trading systems — must be both atomic and durable |
| 3 | **Savepoint Stack Rule** | After `ROLLBACK TO sp_n`, all `sp_k` where `k > n` are erased | Long ETL jobs — checkpoint and resume |
| 4 | **DDL Implicit Commit** | Any DDL (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME`) **forces** an automatic commit | DDLs end the current transaction silently |
| 5 | **Autocommit Default** | Oracle: **OFF**; MySQL: **ON**; PostgreSQL: **ON** | Lab exam setup — verify with `SHOW AUTOCOMMIT;` |
| 6 | **Privilege Grant Cascade** | A grants B WITH GRANT OPTION; B grants C; A revokes B → C is also revoked (in Oracle CASCADE) | Hospital admin chain |
| 7 | **Object Privilege List** | $\{ \text{SELECT}, \text{INSERT}, \text{UPDATE}, \text{DELETE}, \text{REFERENCES}, \text{ALTER}, \text{INDEX}, \text{ALL} \}$ | All count = **8** standard object privileges |
| 8 | **System Privilege List (key ones)** | $\text{CREATE USER},\ \text{DROP USER},\ \text{CREATE SESSION},\ \text{CREATE TABLE},\ \text{GRANT ANY PRIVILEGE}$ | DBA-only actions |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Banking & FinTech:** TCL implements atomicity — a money transfer either debits *and* credits or does nothing.
- **E-Commerce:** TCL prevents overselling — the order placement and stock decrement are one transaction.
- **Healthcare EMRs:** DCL enforces *role-based access* — a nurse has SELECT on patient records but cannot DELETE.
- **IoT Telemetry:** TCL with savepoints enables checkpointing during long batch writes.
- **Defence/Military Systems:** DCL `WITH GRANT OPTION` chains are used to delegate authority down a command hierarchy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides a **fully worked-out, exhaustive walkthrough** of every experiment, in the form KTU evaluators expect for the lab record.

## 3.1 Reference Schema (Use this for all experiments)

We use the standard `EMP` and `DEPT` schema. **Commit it to memory** — KTU questions are typically posed on this exact schema.

```sql
CREATE TABLE DEPT (
    DEPTNO  NUMBER(2) PRIMARY KEY,
    DNAME   VARCHAR2(14),
    LOC     VARCHAR2(13)
);

CREATE TABLE EMP (
    EMPNO   NUMBER(4) PRIMARY KEY,
    ENAME   VARCHAR2(10),
    JOB     VARCHAR2(9),
    MGR     NUMBER(4),
    HIREDATE DATE,
    SAL     NUMBER(7,2),
    COMM    NUMBER(7,2),
    DEPTNO  NUMBER(2) REFERENCES DEPT(DEPTNO)
);
```

Sample data (insert before starting TCL experiments):

```sql
INSERT INTO DEPT VALUES (10, 'ACCOUNTING', 'NEW YORK');
INSERT INTO DEPT VALUES (20, 'RESEARCH',   'DALLAS');
INSERT INTO DEPT VALUES (30, 'SALES',      'CHICAGO');
INSERT INTO DEPT VALUES (40, 'OPERATIONS', 'BOSTON');

INSERT INTO EMP VALUES
(7369, 'SMITH',  'CLERK',     7902, DATE '1980-12-17',  800, NULL, 20);
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES
(7499, 'ALLEN',  'SALESMAN',  7698, DATE '1981-02-20', 1600,  300, 30);
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES
(7521, 'WARD',   'SALESMAN',  7698, DATE '1981-02-22', 1250,  500, 30);
COMMIT;
```

## 3.2 Experiment 3.2-A — Demonstration of `COMMIT` and `ROLLBACK`

### Step 1: View Original Data

```sql
SELECT EMPNO, ENAME, SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output:**

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 7369 | SMITH | 800.00 |

### Step 2: Update a Row (Uncommitted State)

```sql
UPDATE EMP
SET SAL = 900
WHERE EMPNO = 7369;

SELECT EMPNO, ENAME, SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output (this is the *current session* view):**

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 7369 | SMITH | 900.00 |

> **Conversion Logic:** The `UPDATE` statement modifies the row **in the transaction's private buffer**. The change is **not** yet on disk. This is the **Active → Partially Committed** state.

### Step 3: Open a Second Session (Pseudocode)

In SQL\*Plus / SQL Developer, open a **new connection** as a different user. From that session, query:

```sql
SELECT SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output (this is the *other session* view):**

| SAL |
|---|
| 800.00 |

> **Conversion Logic:** This proves the **Isolation** property: an uncommitted change in Session A is **invisible** to Session B. Until `COMMIT` is issued, the world sees the old salary of 800.

### Step 4: Roll Back the Update

```sql
ROLLBACK;
SELECT SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output:**

| SAL |
|---|
| 800.00 |

> **Conversion Logic:** `ROLLBACK` restores the data to the state at the **last commit point**. The 900 update is permanently discarded from the buffer.

### Step 5: Update Again and Commit

```sql
UPDATE EMP
SET SAL = 950
WHERE EMPNO = 7369;

COMMIT;

SELECT SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output (visible to BOTH sessions now):**

| SAL |
|---|
| 950.00 |

> **Conversion Logic:** `COMMIT` flushes the buffer to disk and releases all row-level locks held by the transaction. The new value of 950 is now **durable**.

### Step 6: Verify Persistence After Disconnect

Disconnect and reconnect. Run:

```sql
SELECT SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7369;
```

**Expected Output:** 950.00 (the change **survived** the session — this is the **Durability** property).

## 3.3 Experiment 3.3-B — Demonstration of `SAVEPOINT` and Partial `ROLLBACK`

### Step 1: Begin a Transaction with Multiple Changes

```sql
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES
(7876, 'ADAMS',  'CLERK',     7788, DATE '1983-01-12', 1100, NULL, 20);

UPDATE EMP
SET SAL = 1500
WHERE EMPNO = 7499;

SELECT EMPNO, ENAME, SAL FROM EMP
WHERE EMPNO IN (7876, 7499);
```

**Expected Output:**

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 7876 | ADAMS | 1100.00 |
| 7499 | ALLEN | 1500.00 |

### Step 2: Create a SAVEPOINT

```sql
SAVEPOINT sp_after_insert;
```

> **Conversion Logic:** A `SAVEPOINT` is a **named marker** placed in the transaction's log. The log now records: `INSERT(ADAMS)` → `UPDATE(ALLEN)` → `SAVEPOINT sp_after_insert`.

### Step 3: Make a Mistake We Want to Undo

```sql
UPDATE EMP
SET SAL = 9999
WHERE EMPNO = 7521;   -- wrong row!

SELECT EMPNO, ENAME, SAL FROM EMP WHERE EMPNO = 7521;
```

**Expected Output:**

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 7521 | WARD | 9999.00 |

### Step 4: Roll Back to the Savepoint (Partial Undo)

```sql
ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp_after_insert;
```

**Expected Output of:**

```sql
SELECT EMPNO, ENAME, SAL FROM EMP
WHERE EMPNO IN (7876, 7499, 7521);
```

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 7876 | ADAMS | 1100.00 | (still present — was before savepoint) |
| 7499 | ALLEN | 1500.00 | (still present — was before savepoint) |
| 7521 | WARD  | 1250.00 | (reverted — was after savepoint) |

> **Conversion Logic:** `ROLLBACK TO sp_after_insert` erases only the changes made **after** that savepoint. The INSERT of ADAMS and the UPDATE of ALLEN's salary remain.

### Step 5: Final Commit

```sql
COMMIT;
```

> **Conversion Logic:** Now everything is permanently saved. WARD's salary is back to 1250 (because we rolled back his wrong update), but ADAMS and ALLEN are saved.

## 3.4 Experiment 3.4-C — Demonstration of Implicit COMMIT by DDL

```sql
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES
(7900, 'JAMES', 'CLERK', 7698, DATE '1981-12-03', 950, NULL, 30);

CREATE INDEX idx_emp_deptno ON EMP(DEPTNO);   -- DDL statement

ROLLBACK;
```

**Expected Result:** The INSERT **is NOT rolled back**. Why? Because `CREATE INDEX` is a DDL statement, and **Oracle implicitly commits the transaction** when DDL is issued. The 7900 row stays.

> **Conversion Logic:** The DDL's implicit commit is a **silent transaction terminator**. This is one of the most common KTU exam traps.

## 3.5 Experiment 3.5-D — DCL: GRANT and REVOKE on a Table

### Step 1: Connect as DBA

```sql
-- (In Oracle, connect as SYSTEM or a user with CREATE USER privilege)
CREATE USER alice IDENTIFIED BY alice123;
CREATE USER bob   IDENTIFIED BY bob123;

GRANT CREATE SESSION TO alice;
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO bob;
```

### Step 2: Connect as the Owner of the Table (say, `scott`)

```sql
-- As scott, give alice SELECT and INSERT on the EMP table
GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON scott.emp TO alice;
```

### Step 3: Connect as Alice and Verify

```sql
-- As alice
SELECT * FROM scott.emp WHERE DEPTNO = 20;     -- works
INSERT INTO scott.emp (EMPNO, ENAME, JOB, HIREDATE, SAL, DEPTNO)
VALUES (9001, 'ALICE_USER', 'CLERK', SYSDATE, 2000, 20);  -- works
```

### Step 4: Verify What Alice CANNOT Do

```sql
-- As alice
DELETE FROM scott.emp WHERE EMPNO = 9001;
```

**Expected Error:** `ORA-01031: insufficient privileges`.

> **Conversion Logic:** Alice has only `SELECT` and `INSERT`. `DELETE` is not in her privilege set, so the engine rejects the operation at the **authorisation** layer (one of the layers in the SQL execution model).

### Step 5: Test `GRANT` with `WITH GRANT OPTION`

```sql
-- As scott
GRANT SELECT ON scott.emp TO bob WITH GRANT OPTION;
```

Bob can now:

```sql
-- As bob
GRANT SELECT ON scott.emp TO alice;   -- works because of WITH GRANT OPTION
```

### Step 6: Revoke the Privilege

```sql
-- As scott
REVOKE SELECT ON scott.emp FROM bob;   -- CASCADE not needed in this form
```

Bob can no longer SELECT from `scott.emp`, and his grant to alice is **also automatically revoked** because of the `WITH GRANT OPTION` dependency. This is the **cascade revoke rule**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Command | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| A user wants all changes permanently stored | `COMMIT` | Ends transaction, makes changes durable |
| A user inserted wrong data and wants to undo | `ROLLBACK` | Reverts entire transaction |
| A user made a mistake in the middle of a long operation | `SAVEPOINT` + `ROLLBACK TO` | Undoes only the later part |
| A new intern should be able to read a table | `GRANT SELECT` | Read-only privilege |
| A data-entry operator must add rows but never delete | `GRANT INSERT` only | Principle of least privilege |
| A team lead must delegate his own access to juniors | `... WITH GRANT OPTION` | Sub-granting capability |
| A user has resigned — block all access | `REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES` | Complete withdrawal |
| DDL executed accidentally | Automatic `COMMIT` triggered | Be aware before DDL |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transaction State Transition Diagram

This is the diagram KTU most frequently asks for in Part A (3 marks).

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateActive : First DML statement
    stateActive --> stateActive : INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE
    stateActive --> statePartial : Last DML completed
    statePartial --> stateCommitted : COMMIT succeeds
    statePartial --> stateFailed : Crash / Error / ROLLBACK
    stateActive --> stateFailed : Constraint violation
    stateFailed --> stateAborted : ROLLBACK finishes
    stateAborted --> stateActive : New transaction begins
    stateCommitted --> [*] : End of transaction
    stateAborted --> [*] : End of transaction

    note right of stateCommitted : Data is now DURABLE on disk
    note right of statePartial : All changes in log buffer only
    note left of stateFailed : Locks released, buffer flushed
```

**Caption:** `stateDiagram-v2` used. Node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `stateActive`) — no reserved keywords used as bare node names.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of TCL + DCL Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User A Session] --> sqlParser1[SQL Engine Parser]
    userB[User B Session] --> sqlParser2[SQL Engine Parser]
    admin[Database Administrator] --> dclHandler[DCL Handler]
    dclHandler --> privStore[(Privilege Data Dictionary)]
    privStore --> sqlParser1
    privStore --> sqlParser2
    sqlParser1 --> checkAuth1{Authorisation Check}
    sqlParser2 --> checkAuth2{Authorisation Check}
    checkAuth1 -->|Allowed| execDML1[Execute DML]
    checkAuth2 -->|Allowed| execDML2[Execute DML]
    checkAuth1 -->|Denied| errA[ORA-01031 Error]
    checkAuth2 -->|Denied| errB[ORA-01031 Error]
    execDML1 --> txMgr1[Transaction Manager A]
    execDML2 --> txMgr2[Transaction Manager B]
    txMgr1 --> saveLog1[(Log Buffer A)]
    txMgr2 --> saveLog2[(Log Buffer B)]
    saveLog1 --> cmdDecision1{COMMIT or ROLLBACK?}
    saveLog2 --> cmdDecision2{COMMIT or ROLLBACK?}
    cmdDecision1 -->|COMMIT| flush1[Flush to Disk]
    cmdDecision1 -->|ROLLBACK| undo1[Undo via Before-Image]
    cmdDecision2 -->|COMMIT| flush2[Flush to Disk]
    cmdDecision2 -->|ROLLBACK| undo2[Undo via Before-Image]
    cmdDecision1 -->|SAVEPOINT| sp1[Set Marker]
    flush1 --> doneA[Transaction Complete A]
    flush2 --> doneB[Transaction Complete B]
    undo1 --> doneA
    undo2 --> doneB
    sp1 --> doneA
```

**Caption:** Shows the separation of **DCL** (privilege checking before execution) and **TCL** (deciding what to do with buffered changes at transaction end). Node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`userA`, `checkAuth1`, etc.) per the engine rules.

## 4.3 Privilege Grant / Revoke Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant DBA as Database Admin
    participant DBMS as DBMS Engine
    participant Owner as Table Owner scott
    participant Alice as User alice
    participant Bob as User bob

    DBA->>DBMS: CREATE USER alice, bob
    DBA->>DBMS: GRANT CREATE SESSION TO alice, bob
    Owner->>DBMS: GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON emp TO alice
    DBMS-->>Alice: Privilege row stored
    Alice->>DBMS: SELECT * FROM scott.emp
    DBMS-->>Alice: Rows returned
    Alice->>DBMS: DELETE FROM scott.emp
    DBMS-->>Alice: ORA-01031 insufficient privileges
    Owner->>DBMS: GRANT SELECT ON emp TO bob WITH GRANT OPTION
    Bob->>DBMS: GRANT SELECT ON scott.emp TO alice
    DBMS-->>Bob: Sub-grant accepted
    Owner->>DBMS: REVOKE SELECT ON emp FROM bob
    DBMS-->>Bob: Privilege removed
    DBMS-->>Alice: Auto-cascade revoke applied
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between DDL, DML, DCL, and TCL with one example command for each.**

**Model Answer (Concise — must fit within 3-mark space):**

| Category | Full Form | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DDL** | Data Definition Language | Defines / alters database objects | `CREATE TABLE emp (...);` |
| **DML** | Data Manipulation Language | Manipulates data inside tables | `UPDATE emp SET sal=1000;` |
| **DCL** | Data Control Language | Controls user access / privileges | `GRANT SELECT ON emp TO alice;` |
| **TCL** | Transaction Control Language | Manages transactions | `COMMIT;` / `ROLLBACK;` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *D-D-D-T* in execution order — Define, Data-fill, Decide-access, Transact.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a SAVEPOINT? How is it different from a COMMIT?**

**Model Answer:**

A **SAVEPOINT** is a named marker within a transaction that allows partial rollback. The key differences:

| Property | SAVEPOINT | COMMIT |
|---|---|---|
| Permanent? | **No** — only a logical marker | **Yes** — changes flushed to disk |
| Ends transaction? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Visible to other users? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Releases locks? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Syntax | `SAVEPOINT sp1;` | `COMMIT;` |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Exam Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts of **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. A minimum of one sub-part is compulsory.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** `[KTU Model – Module 2]`

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Consider the following `EMP` table:**

| EMPNO | ENAME | JOB | SAL | DEPTNO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | ANU | CLERK | 1500 | 10 |
| 102 | BINU | MANAGER | 3000 | 20 |
| 103 | CINU | SALESMAN | 2000 | 30 |

**Write SQL TCL statements to perform the following sequence of operations and show the state of the table after each step:**
1. Insert a new row `(104, 'DINU', 'ANALYST', 3500, 20)`.
2. Update ANU's salary to 1800.
3. Set a SAVEPOINT named `sp1`.
4. Insert another row `(105, 'EINU', 'CLERK', 1200, 10)`.
5. Update BINU's salary to 3500.
6. ROLLBACK to `sp1`.
7. COMMIT.

**Model Solution (Step-by-step, with state after each):**

**Step 1: Insert DINU**
```sql
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES (104, 'DINU', 'ANALYST', 3500, 20);
```

| EMPNO | ENAME | JOB | SAL | DEPTNO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | ANU | CLERK | 1500 | 10 |
| 102 | BINU | MANAGER | 3000 | 20 |
| 103 | CINU | SALESMAN | 2000 | 30 |
| **104** | **DINU** | **ANALYST** | **3500** | **20** |

**Step 2: Update ANU's salary**
```sql
UPDATE EMP SET SAL = 1800 WHERE EMPNO = 101;
```

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 101 | ANU | **1800** ← updated |
| 102 | BINU | 3000 |
| 103 | CINU | 2000 |
| 104 | DINU | 3500 |

**Step 3: SAVEPOINT sp1**
```sql
SAVEPOINT sp1;
```
→ Buffer state is "marked" at this position. The transaction is still **Active**.

**Step 4: Insert EINU**
```sql
INSERT INTO EMP VALUES (105, 'EINU', 'CLERK', 1200, 10);
```

| EMPNO | ENAME |
|---|---|
| 101 | ANU |
| 102 | BINU |
| 103 | CINU |
| 104 | DINU |
| **105** | **EINU** ← newly added |

**Step 5: Update BINU's salary**
```sql
UPDATE EMP SET SAL = 3500 WHERE EMPNO = 102;
```

| EMPNO | ENAME | SAL |
|---|---|---|
| 102 | BINU | **3500** ← updated |

**Step 6: ROLLBACK TO sp1**
```sql
ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;
```

> **Conversion Logic:** This **undoes** Step 4 and Step 5. Step 1 and Step 2 are **preserved** because they occurred *before* `sp1`.

**Resulting State of Table after Step 6:**

| EMPNO | ENAME | JOB | SAL | DEPTNO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | ANU | CLERK | 1800 | 10 |
| 102 | BINU | MANAGER | 3000 | 20 |
| 103 | CINU | SALESMAN | 2000 | 30 |
| 104 | DINU | ANALYST | 3500 | 20 |
| ~~105~~ | ~~EINU~~ | — | — | — (rolled back) |

Note: **BINU's salary is back to 3000** (not 3500) and **EINU's row is gone** — these are the two operations after `sp1`.

**Step 7: COMMIT**
```sql
COMMIT;
```
→ The state shown above is now **permanent and durable**.

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Explain the ACID properties of a transaction. How does TCL help enforce them in Oracle?**

**Model Answer:**

**ACID stands for:**

1. **Atomicity** — "All or Nothing". A transaction is a single, indivisible unit. Either **every** operation succeeds, or **none** of them are reflected in the database.
   - *Enforced in Oracle by:* The **undo/rollback segments**. If a transaction is aborted or crashes, Oracle uses the *before-images* stored in the rollback segments to undo all changes.
   - *TCL role:* `COMMIT` makes all changes permanent; `ROLLBACK` undoes everything.

2. **Consistency** — A transaction takes the database from one **valid state** to another valid state. All integrity constraints (PK, FK, CHECK) must be satisfied before and after the transaction.
   - *Enforced by:* The integrity constraints defined in the schema (e.g., `FOREIGN KEY`, `CHECK`, `NOT NULL`).
   - *TCL role:* A `COMMIT` is allowed **only if** the transaction respects all constraints; otherwise, it automatically rolls back.

3. **Isolation** — Concurrent transactions must not see each other's **uncommitted (dirty) data**. Each transaction appears to execute in isolation.
   - *Enforced by:* **Locks** (row-level, table-level) and the **Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC)** architecture in Oracle.
   - *TCL role:* `COMMIT` releases the locks; until then, other transactions cannot see the uncommitted data.

4. **Durability** — Once a transaction commits, its changes survive **any subsequent failure** (power loss, crash, OS error).
   - *Enforced by:* The **Online Redo Log Files** and the **Write-Ahead Log (WAL)** protocol. Before any change is made to the data file, the change is written to the redo log. On restart, Oracle replays the log to recover the committed state.
   - *TCL role:* `COMMIT` triggers a `LGWR` (Log Writer) process that flushes the log buffer to disk synchronously, ensuring durability.

**Summary Table:**

| Property | Problem it solves | Oracle Mechanism | TCL Command Involved |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atomicity | Partial failures | Rollback segments | `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK` |
| Consistency | Constraint violations | Integrity constraints | Implicit rollback on error |
| Isolation | Concurrent updates | Locks + MVCC | `COMMIT` releases locks |
| Durability | System crashes | Redo logs | `COMMIT` triggers log flush |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU Model – Module 2]`

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Write SQL DCL statements to perform the following:**
1. Create two new users `ravi` and `meena` with password `kerala2024`.
2. Grant `ravi` the privilege to `SELECT` and `UPDATE` on the table `EMP` owned by user `scott`.
3. Allow `ravi` to further pass this privilege to others.
4. Grant `meena` only the `INSERT` privilege on `EMP`.
5. Revoke the `UPDATE` privilege from `ravi`.
6. Verify the resulting privileges of both users.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Create users (must be run as DBA, e.g., SYSTEM)**
```sql
CREATE USER ravi  IDENTIFIED BY kerala2024;
CREATE USER meena IDENTIFIED BY kerala2024;
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO ravi;
GRANT CREATE SESSION TO meena;
```

**Step 2: Grant SELECT and UPDATE to ravi**
```sql
-- Connect as scott
GRANT SELECT, UPDATE ON scott.emp TO ravi;
```

**Step 3: Allow ravi to sub-grant**
```sql
GRANT SELECT, UPDATE ON scott.emp TO ravi WITH GRANT OPTION;
```
*(Note: For the WITH GRANT OPTION to apply, it must be in the same GRANT statement, as in this revised combined form, or scott must re-grant with the option.)*

**Step 4: Grant INSERT to meena**
```sql
GRANT INSERT ON scott.emp TO meena;
```

**Step 5: Revoke UPDATE from ravi**
```sql
REVOKE UPDATE ON scott.emp FROM ravi;
```

> **Important cascade effect:** Because of `WITH GRANT OPTION`, ravi may have already passed `SELECT` (and possibly `UPDATE`) to other users. Oracle will **automatically cascade** the revoke — anyone ravi had granted `UPDATE` to will also lose it.

**Step 6: Verify**
```sql
-- As ravi
SELECT * FROM scott.emp;            -- works
UPDATE scott.emp SET sal=2000 WHERE empno=101;  -- ORA-01927 (now denied)
INSERT INTO scott.emp VALUES (...); -- ORA-01031 (INSERT was never granted to ravi)

-- As meena
INSERT INTO scott.emp (empno, ename, job, sal, deptno)
VALUES (201, 'MEENA_USER', 'CLERK', 1500, 10);  -- works
SELECT * FROM scott.emp;            -- ORA-01031 (SELECT not granted to meena)
```

**Final Privilege Matrix:**

| User | SELECT on scott.emp | INSERT on scott.emp | UPDATE on scott.emp |
|---|---|---|---|
| ravi | ✅ (with grant option) | ❌ | ❌ (revoked) |
| meena | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Explain the difference between system privileges and object privileges in Oracle with examples. What happens during a `CASCADE` revoke?**

**Model Answer:**

**System Privileges:**
- A right to perform a specific **action on the database engine itself** (creating users, dropping tablespaces, etc.) or on a particular **type of object**.
- Granted by the DBA or by a user who has the `GRANT ANY PRIVILEGE` system privilege.
- Examples: `CREATE USER`, `CREATE TABLE`, `CREATE SESSION`, `DROP ANY TABLE`, `BACKUP ANY TABLE`.

**Object Privileges:**
- A right to perform a specific **action on a specific object** (a specific table, view, sequence, etc.).
- Granted by the **owner** of the object, or by a user with `GRANT OPTION` on that object.
- Examples: `SELECT ON scott.emp`, `INSERT ON scott.dept`, `UPDATE ON scott.emp`, `ALL ON scott.emp`.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | System Privilege | Object Privilege |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Database-wide or schema-level | A single object |
| **Granted by** | DBA | Object owner |
| **Example** | `GRANT CREATE TABLE TO ravi;` | `GRANT SELECT ON scott.emp TO ravi;` |
| **Revoke impact** | Affects all objects of that type | Affects only the named object |
| **Number of privileges** | Hundreds in Oracle | **8 standard** (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, REFERENCES, ALTER, INDEX, ALL) |

**CASCADE Revoke — Detailed Behaviour:**

When you write:
```sql
REVOKE SELECT ON scott.emp FROM ravi CASCADE CONSTRAINTS;
```

- The word `CASCADE CONSTRAINTS` drops any **referential integrity** constraints that depend on the privilege.
- For privileges granted **with `WITH GRANT OPTION`**, Oracle automatically revokes any **downstream grants** made by `ravi` to other users.

**Step-by-step scenario for cascade:**
1. `scott` → grants SELECT to `ravi` **WITH GRANT OPTION**.
2. `ravi` → grants SELECT to `meena`.
3. `meena` → grants SELECT to `john`.
4. `scott` → `REVOKE SELECT ON emp FROM ravi;`
5. **Effect:** `ravi`, `meena`, and **all** downstream users (`john`, etc.) lose their SELECT privilege on `scott.emp` in one atomic step.

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *grant dependency tree* — when the root is revoked, the entire subtree falls.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Marks Lost on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that DDL causes implicit COMMIT** — Students write `INSERT; CREATE TABLE; ROLLBACK;` and are surprised that the INSERT is committed. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing `SAVEPOINT` with `COMMIT`** — A savepoint does *not* end the transaction; data is still invisible to other users. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Writing `GRANT ALL ON emp TO ravi;` without `WITH GRANT OPTION` and then later expecting ravi to sub-grant.** **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Missing the `CASCADE` effect during `REVOKE`** — saying "only ravi loses the privilege" when in fact his sub-grantees also lose it. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Not writing `COMMIT;` at the end of a TCL sequence** — The examiner cannot give full credit if the final state is shown but not committed. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Mixing up AUTOCOMMIT defaults** — saying "Oracle autocommit is ON" is wrong; it's **OFF by default**. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 7. **In Part B, failing to show the table state after each step** — KTU wants **intermediate states**, not just the final answer. **Loss: up to 4 marks.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **TCL = Transaction Control Language** → `COMMIT`, `ROLLBACK`, `SAVEPOINT`.
- ⭐ **DCL = Data Control Language** → `GRANT`, `REVOKE`.
- ⭐ **ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — the four guarantees of a transaction.
- ⭐ **`COMMIT`** makes changes **permanent and visible** to other users; **releases all locks**; **ends the transaction**.
- ⭐ **`ROLLBACK`** undoes **all** changes of the current transaction.
- ⭐ **`ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT sp1;`** undoes only the operations **after** `sp1`.
- ⭐ **DDL statements (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`, `TRUNCATE`, `RENAME`)** cause an **implicit COMMIT**.
- ⭐ **In Oracle, AUTOCOMMIT is OFF by default**; **in MySQL, it is ON** by default.
- ⭐ **Object privileges** are the **8 standard ones**: `SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, REFERENCES, ALTER, INDEX, ALL`.
- ⭐ **System privileges** include `CREATE USER`, `CREATE TABLE`, `CREATE SESSION`, `GRANT ANY PRIVILEGE`.
- ⭐ **`GRANT ... WITH GRANT OPTION`** lets the grantee pass the privilege further.
- ⭐ **`REVOKE ... CASCADE`** (in Oracle) removes all **downstream grants** automatically.
- ⭐ **SAVEPOINT** does **not** release locks, does **not** make data visible, does **not** end the transaction.
- ⭐ **Transaction Lifecycle:** Active → Partially Committed → Committed (or Failed → Aborted).
- ⭐ **Durability is enforced by** the **Online Redo Log** (Oracle) and **WAL protocol**.
- ⭐ **Isolation is enforced by** **locks** (row-level) and **MVCC** (multi-version concurrency control).
- ⭐ **Atomicity is enforced by** the **rollback segments** (Oracle's undo mechanism).
- ⭐ **A `GRANT` on a table** can only be issued by the **owner** of that table or by a user with **system-level `GRANT ANY OBJECT PRIVILEGE`**.
- ⭐ **Order in TCL statements matters:** once a `ROLLBACK` is issued, *all* savepoints in the transaction are erased.

---

**End of KTU-Premier Notes — Module Experiment 2: TCL & DCL Commands**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practice of SQL commands for creation of views and assertions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Practice of SQL commands for creation of views and assertions # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **View** in SQL is a **virtual table** derived from one or more base tables (or other views) using a stored `SELECT` query. It does **not** physically store data; instead, it dynamically fetches results whenever queried. An **Assertion** is a declarative SQL constraint that specifies a condition that the database must **always satisfy** (a generalized form of integrity constraints spanning multiple tables). In the context of the **DBMS Lab (PCCSL408)**, this experiment trains students to use the Data Definition Language (DDL) sub-language of SQL — specifically `CREATE VIEW`, `DROP VIEW`, `UPDATE` through views, `WITH CHECK OPTION`, and the `CREATE ASSERTION` statement — within a standard RDBMS environment such as **Oracle**, **PostgreSQL**, or **MySQL**. ### View — The Academic Distinction A view is officially classified as a **derived relation** in the relational algebra framework. The defining expression of a view is maintained in the **system catalog (data dictionary)** of the DBMS. The result of executing this expression produces a virtual table at query time. Because the underlying base tables can change between invocations, two successive retrievals through the same view may return different data — a property known as **view non-materialization**. ### Assertion — The Academic Distinction An assertion is a **database-wide integrity constraint**. Unlike `CHECK` (table-level) or `KEY` / `FOREIGN KEY` (column/table-level), an assertion references columns across **multiple tables** and the DBMS is responsible for checking the condition after **every transaction** that could potentially violate it. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Window Pane" Analogy for a View** > Imagine you live in a house (the database). The **base tables** are the actual furniture and rooms. A **View** is like a **decorative window pane** installed in your wall. The window does not have its own room behind it — but when you look through the window, you see a curated, filtered, or rearranged view of your house. If you rearrange the furniture (update the base tables) and look through the same window again, you will see the new arrangement. The window itself never stores anything. > [!NOTE] > **The "House Rule Notice" Analogy for an Assertion** > An **Assertion** is like a notice pinned on the front door of your house: *"The total number of people living inside must never exceed 10."* Every time something happens (someone moves in or out), you re-read the rule. The DBMS automatically rechecks the assertion after every relevant transaction — it is a **permanent invariant** enforced by the database engine. ## 1.3 Highlighted Vocabulary | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Virtual Table** | A table that does not physically occupy storage but appears as a table to the user | | **Base Table** | An actual table whose tuples are physically stored on disk | | **Data Dictionary** | The system catalog that stores metadata, including view definitions | | **Updatable View** | A view through which `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE` can be applied to the base table | | **WITH CHECK OPTION** | A clause ensuring that rows modified through the view continue to satisfy the view's `WHERE` clause | | **Assertion** | A schema-level integrity constraint that must always hold across multiple tables | --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Exam Note:** Many widely used RDBMS products — including **Oracle** and **MySQL** — do **NOT support the `CREATE ASSERTION` statement** as of their standard builds. For KTU lab records, students should: > - Implement assertions using **equivalent triggers** in Oracle/MySQL, **OR** > - Use **PostgreSQL** (which fully supports `CREATE ASSERTION` in standard SQL compliance mode). > > Examiners frequently award marks for **mentioning this compatibility caveat** in your lab record. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SQL Syntax — Quick Reference ### 2.1.1 `CREATE VIEW` Syntax ```sql CREATE [OR REPLACE] VIEW view_name [(column_alias_list)] AS subquery [WITH [CASCADED | LOCAL] CHECK OPTION]; ``` **Breakdown of each clause:** | Clause | Purpose | |---|---| | `OR REPLACE` | Recreates the view if it already exists (avoids `DROP` + `CREATE` cycle) | | `view_name` | Identifier for the virtual table | | `column_alias_list` | Optional renaming of the projected columns | | `AS subquery` | The defining `SELECT` query stored in the data dictionary | | `WITH CHECK OPTION` | Prevents updates that would cause rows to "disappear" from the view | | `CASCADED` | Check propagates through all underlying views | | `LOCAL` | Check applies only to the current view's `WHERE` clause | ### 2.1.2 `DROP VIEW` Syntax ```sql DROP VIEW [IF EXISTS] view_name [CASCADE | RESTRICT]; ``` | Option | Behavior | |---|---| | `CASCADE` | Automatically drops dependent objects (views, constraints) | | `RESTRICT` (default) | Refuses to drop if any object depends on it | | `IF EXISTS` | Avoids error if the view does not exist | ### 2.1.3 `CREATE ASSERTION` Syntax ```sql CREATE ASSERTION assertion_name CHECK (search_condition); ``` The `search_condition` is a Boolean predicate that **must evaluate to TRUE for every state** of the database. The DBMS checks it automatically after any transaction that modifies referenced tables. ### 2.1.4 `DROP ASSERTION` Syntax ```sql DROP ASSERTION assertion_name; ``` ## 2.2 Types of Views (KTU High-Yield Classification) | View Type | Definition | Updatable? | |---|---|---| | **Simple View** | Derived from a single table, no aggregates, no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY` | ✅ Yes | | **Complex View** | Uses joins, aggregates, subqueries, `DISTINCT`, or `GROUP BY` | ❌ Generally No | | **Read-Only View** | Explicitly marked `WITH READ ONLY` (Oracle syntax) | ❌ No | | **Inline View** | Subquery in the `FROM` clause of a `SELECT`, not a stored object | ❌ No | | **Materialized View** | Physically stores the result (requires `REFRESH`) | ✅ Indirectly | | **Updatable View** | Allows `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` that propagate to base table | ✅ Yes | ## 2.3 Conditions for an Updatable View (ANSI SQL Standard) A view is **updatable** if **ALL** of the following hold: 1. It is built from a **single base table** (`FROM` clause contains exactly one table reference). 2. It does **not** use `DISTINCT`, `GROUP BY`, `HAVING`, `UNION`, `INTERSECT`, or `EXCEPT`. 3. It does **not** use **set functions** (`COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`). 4. It does **not** use **subqueries** in the `SELECT` or `WHERE` clauses that reference the base table itself. 5. Every column of the base table **without a default value** must be exposed in the view (unless `NULL` is acceptable). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter in KTU lab?** When a student attempts `INSERT INTO view_name VALUES (...)` on a non-updatable view, the DBMS throws **ORA-01733 / ORA-01732** (Oracle) or **ER_NON_UPDATABLE_VIEW** (MySQL). Examiners look for the ability to **diagnose** this error. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Rule / Formula | |---|---|---| | 1 | View Resolution | `Query on View` $\equiv$ `Query on Base Table` via **substitution (query modification)** | | 2 | View Storage | $S_{\text{view}} = 0$ bytes (only metadata stored) | | 3 | View Retrieval Latency | $T_{\text{view}} \approx T_{\text{base}} + T_{\text{resolve}}$ | | 4 | Materialized View Storage | $S_{\text{mat}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{row}_i \cdot \text{row\_size}_i$ | | 5 | Assertion Check Frequency | Once per `COMMIT` involving referenced tables | | 6 | `WITH CHECK OPTION` Predicate | $\forall r \in \text{view}:\, W(r) = \text{TRUE}$ | | 7 | `CASCADED` vs `LOCAL` Propagation | `CASCADED`: $P_{\text{effective}} = P_{\text{view}} \wedge \bigwedge P_{\text{ancestor}}$
`LOCAL`: $P_{\text{effective}} = P_{\text{view}} \wedge P_{\text{direct ancestor}}$ | | 8 | Cartesian Product of Views | $\text{V1} \bowtie \text{V2} \iff \text{Base}_1 \bowtie \text{Base}_2$ under same join key | | 9 | Assertion Scope | $\forall t_1 \in T_1, t_2 \in T_2, \ldots:\, C(t_1, t_2, \ldots) = \text{TRUE}$ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Area | Use of Views | |---|---| | **Banking Systems** | Hide sensitive columns (e.g., `account_balance`) from tellers; expose only to managers | | **Data Warehousing** | Materialized views accelerate OLAP cube roll-ups | | **Healthcare EHRs** | Doctors see only assigned patients; views enforce row-level security | | **Multi-Tenant SaaS** | Each tenant sees a slice of the global table via parameterized views | | **Audit Systems** | Assertions enforce invariants like *"loan amount must not exceed 5× annual salary"* | ## 2.6 Why Assertions Matter — Engineering Rationale > [!IMPORTANT] > An assertion is essentially a **database invariant**. It is the formal mechanism by which business rules are encoded directly into the schema. In real-world engineering, this is critical for **regulatory compliance** (e.g., Basel-III banking norms, FDA healthcare rules) where the database itself must guarantee that an invariant is never violated — regardless of application code quality. **The relational algebra equivalence:** $$ \sigma_C(R_1 \bowtie R_2 \bowtie \ldots \bowtie R_n) \neq \emptyset $$ must hold for all valid database states if $C$ is an assertion. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section walks through the **logical derivation** of view-resolution behavior, the `WITH CHECK OPTION` predicate derivation, and assertion correctness proofs — the kind of analytical depth KTU examiners reward. ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough 1 — View Resolution via Query Modification **Problem Setup.** Suppose we have: $$\text{Employee}(\underline{eid},\ \text{ename},\ \text{salary},\ \text{dno})$$ $$\text{Department}(\underline{dno},\ \text{dname},\ \text{location})$$ A view is defined as: $$ V_{\text{IT\_High}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{salary} > 50000 \,\wedge\, \text{dname} = \text{'IT'}}(\text{Employee} \bowtie \text{Department}) $$ Now consider a query against the view: $$ Q_1 \;=\; \pi_{\text{ename},\ \text{salary}}(\sigma_{\text{ename} = \text{'Ram'}}(V_{\text{IT\_High}})) $$ **Step 1 — Substitute the view definition (query modification):** $$ Q_1' \;=\; \pi_{\text{ename},\ \text{salary}}\bigl(\sigma_{\text{ename} = \text{'Ram'}} \bigl(\sigma_{\text{salary} > 50000 \,\wedge\, \text{dname} = \text{'IT'}}(\text{Employee} \bowtie \text{Department})\bigr)\bigr) $$ **Step 2 — Apply predicate conjunction (selection cascade):** For relational algebra, multiple selections commute: $\sigma_{p_1}(\sigma_{p_2}(R)) = \sigma_{p_1 \wedge p_2}(R)$. $$ Q_1' \;=\; \pi_{\text{ename},\ \text{salary}}\bigl(\sigma_{\text{ename} = \text{'Ram'} \,\wedge\, \text{salary} > 50000 \,\wedge\, \text{dname} = \text{'IT'}}(\text{Employee} \bowtie \text{Department})\bigr) $$ **Step 3 — Apply pushdown optimization (selection before join):** Since `ename` lives in `Employee` and `dname` lives in `Department`, only `dname = 'IT'` can be pushed below the join: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_1'' \; &= \; \pi_{\text{ename},\ \text{salary}}\bigl(\text{Employee} \bowtie \sigma_{\text{dname} = \text{'IT'}}(\text{Department})\bigr) \\ &\quad \text{with selection } \sigma_{\text{ename} = \text{'Ram'} \,\wedge\, \text{salary} > 50000} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The view itself **does not store** the joined result. Every query is re-executed against the base tables at runtime — confirming the **virtual** nature. ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough 2 — `WITH CHECK OPTION` Predicate Derivation **Setup.** View definition: $$ V \;=\; \sigma_{\text{salary} \geq 30000}(\text{Employee}) \quad\text{with}\quad \text{WITH CHECK OPTION} $$ **Step 1 — Formulate the invariant.** For every row $r$ that enters the table **via this view**, the DBMS must enforce: $$ \forall r \in \text{Employee}:\, \bigl(r.\text{salary} \geq 30000\bigr) \;\lor\; \bigl(r \text{ inserted without view}\bigr) $$ When the insertion/update is performed **through** the view: $$ \text{Check}(r) \;=\; (r.\text{salary} \geq 30000) $$ If `Check(r) = FALSE`, the DBMS raises **ORA-01402** (Oracle) / **CHECK OPTION failed** (MySQL). **Step 2 — Derive the `CASCADED` case.** If the view $V_1$ is defined on top of another view $V_2$, and both have `WITH CHECK OPTION`: $$ \text{Check}_{\text{CASCADED}}(r) \;=\; \bigwedge_{i \,=\, 1}^{n} P_i(r) $$ where $P_i$ is the predicate of each view in the dependency chain. **Step 3 — Derive the `LOCAL` case.** $$ \text{Check}_{\text{LOCAL}}(r) \;=\; P_1(r) \;\wedge\; P_{\text{direct\_ancestor}}(r) $$ Only the immediate parent's predicate is checked, **not** transitive ancestors. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** If the question asks *"Why is `WITH CHECK OPTION` necessary?"* — the formal answer is: *It preserves the invariant that all rows visible through the view continue to satisfy the view's defining predicate after an `INSERT` or `UPDATE`*, preventing **invisible updates**. ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough 3 — Assertion Correctness Derivation **Setup.** We want to enforce: *"No department may have more than 10 employees."* **Step 1 — Express as a relational calculus formula:** $$ \forall d \in \text{Department}:\; |\{e \in \text{Employee} \mid e.\text{dno} = d.\text{dno}\}| \leq 10 $$ **Step 2 — Translate into SQL assertion:** ```sql CREATE ASSERTION max_emp_per_dept CHECK ( 10 >= ALL ( SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employee GROUP BY dno ) ); ``` **Step 3 — Verify the check semantics.** After every transaction that inserts/updates/deletes from `Employee` or `Department`, the DBMS evaluates: $$ \text{Result} \;=\; \bigvee_{d \,\in\, \text{Department}} \bigl(|\{e \mid e.\text{dno} = d.\text{dno}\}| > 10\bigr) $$ If `Result = TRUE`, the assertion is violated and the transaction is **rolled back**. ## 3.4 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix | Scenario | Use VIEW | Use ASSERTION | Use TRIGGER | |---|---|---|---| | Hide columns for security | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | Enforce cross-table invariant | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (fallback) | | Restrict rows by user role | ✅ | ❌ | ⚠️ Possible | | Compute aggregate on the fly | ✅ (complex view) | ❌ | ❌ | | Materialize for performance | ✅ (materialized) | ❌ | ❌ | | Validate per-statement rules | ❌ | ⚠️ Possible | ✅ | ## 3.5 Worked Example — Composite Lab Question **Given Schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE Sailors ( sid INT PRIMARY KEY, sname VARCHAR(50), rating INT, age INT ); CREATE TABLE Boats ( bid INT PRIMARY KEY, bname VARCHAR(50), color VARCHAR(20) ); CREATE TABLE Reserves ( sid INT REFERENCES Sailors(sid), bid INT REFERENCES Boats(bid), day DATE, PRIMARY KEY (sid, bid, day) ); ``` **Question:** Create a view `RedBoatReservations` showing sailor names who have reserved **red boats**. **Step 1 — Identify participating tables:** `Sailors`, `Reserves`, `Boats`. **Step 2 — Construct join chain:** `Sailors.sid = Reserves.sid` AND `Reserves.bid = Boats.bid`. **Step 3 — Filter condition:** `Boats.color = 'red'`. **Step 4 — Compose view:** ```sql CREATE VIEW RedBoatReservations AS SELECT s.sname, b.bname, r.day FROM Sailors s JOIN Reserves r ON s.sid = r.sid JOIN Boats b ON r.bid = b.bid WHERE b.color = 'red'; ``` **Step 5 — Verify updatability:** This view is **NOT updatable** because it uses: - A multi-table `JOIN` - No `DISTINCT`/`GROUP BY` (good) - Multiple tables in `FROM` → violates updatability rule #1 **Step 6 — Try to insert:** ```sql INSERT INTO RedBoatReservations VALUES ('Ram', 'Titanic', '2024-01-15'); ``` **Expected DBMS Response (Oracle):** ``` ORA-01733: view not updatable ``` **Step 7 — Add an assertion to forbid reservation of green boats:** ```sql CREATE ASSERTION no_green_reservations CHECK (NOT EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM Reserves r JOIN Boats b ON r.bid = b.bid WHERE b.color = 'green' )); ``` --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight Box — Practical Reality** > When running these statements on **Oracle** or **MySQL**, the `CREATE ASSERTION` will **fail with a syntax error** because these engines do not implement it. The examiner expects the student to **demonstrate awareness** of this and either: > 1. Switch to **PostgreSQL**, or > 2. Provide the equivalent **trigger** solution. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 View Resolution Flow — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A["USER ISSUES QUERY ON VIEW"] --> B["SQL PARSER"] B --> C{"VIEW NAME FOUND IN CATALOG?"} C -- "NO" --> D["ERROR: VIEW NOT FOUND"] C -- "YES" --> E["FETCH VIEW DEFINITION FROM DATA DICTIONARY"] E --> F["QUERY MODIFIER: SUBSTITUTE VIEW WITH BASE TABLES"] F --> G["QUERY OPTIMIZER"] G --> H["EXECUTION ENGINE"] H --> I["READ FROM BASE TABLES"] I --> J["APPLY WHERE JOIN PROJECT FILTERS"] J --> K["RETURN RESULT SET TO USER"] K --> L["NO PHYSICAL STORAGE UPDATED"] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 style K fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style L fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Updatability Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["VIEW DEFINITION ANALYSIS"] --> B{"SINGLE BASE TABLE?"} B -- "NO" --> C["NOT UPDATABLE"] B -- "YES" --> D{"USES DISTINCT GROUP BY UNION?"} D -- "YES" --> C D -- "NO" --> E{"USES AGGREGATE FUNCTIONS?"} E -- "YES" --> C E -- "NO" --> F{"USES SUBQUERIES IN WHERE?"} F -- "YES" --> C F -- "NO" --> G["UPDATABLE VIEW"] G --> H{"WITH CHECK OPTION?"} H -- "YES" --> I["VERIFY INSERT UPDATE SATISFIES PREDICATE"] H -- "NO" --> J["ALLOW DIRECT MODIFICATION"] style G fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style C fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Architecture — Assertion Enforcement Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX["TRANSACTION LIFECYCLE"] A1["BEGIN"] --> A2["DML STATEMENTS"] A2 --> A3["COMMIT REQUESTED"] end A3 --> B["ASSERTION EVALUATOR"] B --> C{"ALL ASSERTIONS SATISFIED?"} C -- "YES" --> D["COMMIT PERSISTED"] C -- "NO" --> E["ROLLBACK + ERROR TO USER"] B --> F["REFERENCED TABLES CATALOG SCAN"] F --> B style D fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style E fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 View Types — Classification Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD V["SQL VIEW CLASSIFICATION"] --> V1["SIMPLE VIEW"] V --> V2["COMPLEX VIEW"] V --> V3["MATERIALIZED VIEW"] V --> V4["INLINE VIEW"] V1 --> V1A["SINGLE TABLE NO AGGREGATE"] V1 --> V1B["UPDATABLE BY DEFAULT"] V2 --> V2A["JOINS SUBQUERIES AGGREGATES"] V2 --> V2B["USUALLY READ ONLY"] V3 --> V3A["PHYSICALLY STORED"] V3 --> V3B["REQUIRES REFRESH"] V4 --> V4A["SUBQUERY IN FROM CLAUSE"] V4 --> V4B["NOT STORED IN CATALOG"] style V1B fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 style V3A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.5 WITH CHECK OPTION — Cascaded vs Local Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD A["VIEW V1: sigma salary greater than 30000 WITH CHECK OPTION"] --> B["VIEW V2: sigma dno equals 5 from V1 WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION"] A --> C["VIEW V3: sigma dno equals 5 from V1 WITH LOCAL CHECK OPTION"] B --> D["INSERT employee salary 25000 dno 5"] B --> E["INSERT employee salary 40000 dno 8"] C --> F["INSERT employee salary 25000 dno 5"] C --> G["INSERT employee salary 40000 dno 8"] D --> H["RESULT: REJECTED salary fails V1"] E --> I["RESULT: REJECTED dno fails V2"] F --> J["RESULT: REJECTED salary fails V1"] G --> K["RESULT: ACCEPTED only V3s predicate checked"] style H fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 style I fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 style J fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 style K fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.6 View Dependencies (Catalog-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["BASE TABLE EMPLOYEE"] --> C["DATA DICTIONARY"] B["BASE TABLE DEPARTMENT"] --> C C --> V1["VIEW V DEPT EMP JOIN"] V1 --> V2["VIEW V2 HIGH SALARY FROM V"] C --> AS1["ASSERTION MAX EMP PER DEPT"] A --> AS1 B --> AS1 style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#333 style AS1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#333 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Define a view in SQL. Mention any two advantages of using views.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **view** is a virtual table derived from one or more base tables through a stored `SELECT` query. The view does not store data physically; it stores only the query definition in the data dictionary. **Two advantages:** 1. **Data security / access control** — Sensitive columns can be hidden from certain users by exposing only specific views. 2. **Query simplification** — Complex joins and aggregations can be encapsulated inside a view, allowing users to query a virtual table directly. 3. *(Bonus point)* Logical data independence — Application programs can be written against views, insulating them from changes in the base table schema. *(Full marks requires the definition + 2 distinct advantages)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is an SQL assertion? How does it differ from a `CHECK` constraint?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **assertion** is a schema-level integrity constraint that specifies a condition that must always hold true across **one or more tables** in the database. | Aspect | `CHECK` | `ASSERTION` | |---|---|---| | Scope | Single table or column | Entire database (multiple tables) | | Form | Part of `CREATE TABLE` or `ALTER TABLE` | Standalone `CREATE ASSERTION` statement | | Re-evaluation | On every row insert/update of the same table | On every transaction touching referenced tables | *(Full marks: definition + correct tabular comparison)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE** choice (either A or B). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Consider the following schema and write SQL DDL statements for the given requirements. Justify whether each created view is **updatable** or **not updatable**. **Schema:** ```sql CREATE TABLE Customer ( cid INT PRIMARY KEY, cname VARCHAR(50), city VARCHAR(30) ); CREATE TABLE Account ( accno INT PRIMARY KEY, cid INT REFERENCES Customer(cid), balance DECIMAL(12,2), branch VARCHAR(30) ); ``` **Requirements:** 1. Create a view `CustAcc` showing customer name, account number, balance, and branch. 2. Create a view `HighBalance` showing account numbers and balance where balance > 50,000 — with `WITH CHECK OPTION`. 3. Create an assertion ensuring that the **sum of all balances in any single branch does not exceed 10,00,000**. **(b)** Explain the architectural difference between a **view** and a **materialized view**. List two situations where a materialized view is preferred over a regular view. --- ### **Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Requirement 1 — `CustAcc` View (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW CustAcc (cname, accno, balance, branch) AS SELECT c.cname, a.accno, a.balance, a.branch FROM Customer c JOIN Account a ON c.cid = a.cid; ``` **Updatability Analysis (1 mark):** This view is **NOT updatable** because: - It uses a **JOIN** of two tables. - Per the ANSI SQL standard, an updatable view must be based on exactly **one** base table. **Requirement 2 — `HighBalance` View with CHECK OPTION (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW HighBalance AS SELECT accno, balance FROM Account WHERE balance > 50000 WITH CHECK OPTION; ``` **Updatability Analysis (1 mark):** This view is **updatable**: - Based on a single base table `Account`. - No aggregates, no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY`. - With `CHECK OPTION`, any `UPDATE` that lowers the balance to ≤ 50,000 will be **rejected** because it would make the row invisible to the view. **Requirement 3 — Branch Balance Assertion (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE ASSERTION branch_balance_cap CHECK ( 1000000 >= ALL ( SELECT SUM(balance) FROM Account GROUP BY branch ) ); ``` **Note:** This statement works in **PostgreSQL** and standard SQL; in **Oracle/MySQL** it will raise a syntax error, in which case a trigger-based equivalent must be implemented. --- #### Part (b) — 7 marks **Architectural Difference (4 marks):** | Feature | View | Materialized View | |---|---|---| | **Data Storage** | None — only definition stored | Physically stored on disk | | **Query Time** | Re-executes base query every access | Reads pre-computed result | | **Freshness** | Always current | Stale until refreshed | | **Storage Cost** | Minimal | High (depends on row count) | | **Update Mechanism** | Implicit (via base tables) | Explicit `REFRESH` command | | **SQL Standard** | `CREATE VIEW` | `CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW` (Oracle/PostgreSQL) | **When Materialized View is Preferred (3 marks):** 1. **OLAP / Data Warehousing:** When queries involve expensive aggregations over millions of rows (e.g., daily sales roll-up), a materialized view stores the pre-computed aggregate and serves it instantly. 2. **Read-heavy, write-light environments:** If a query is run thousands of times per hour against slowly-changing data (e.g., a monthly report on customer demographics), the cost of one nightly refresh is far less than recomputing each time. 3. *(Bonus)* **Cross-table joins on large tables:** Materializing a heavy join (e.g., 1 M × 5 M rows) avoids repeated join costs at query time. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Consider the `Sailors`, `Boats`, and `Reserves` schema provided in Section 3.5. Write SQL DDL for: 1. A view `SailorReservations` listing sailor ID, sailor name, and total number of boats reserved. 2. A view `GreenBoatBan` listing sailors who have reserved green boats — with `WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION`. 3. An assertion ensuring **no sailor can reserve more than 5 distinct boats**. Also explain why `SailorReservations` cannot be used to insert rows. **(b)** Differentiate between `WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION` and `WITH LOCAL CHECK OPTION`. Provide one example for each demonstrating when one succeeds and the other fails. --- ### **Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — 7 marks **Requirement 1 — `SailorReservations` View (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW SailorReservations AS SELECT s.sid, s.sname, COUNT(r.bid) AS total_reservations FROM Sailors s LEFT JOIN Reserves r ON s.sid = r.sid GROUP BY s.sid, s.sname; ``` **Why it cannot accept `INSERT` (1 mark):** ```sql INSERT INTO SailorReservations VALUES (99, 'New Sailor', 0); -- ORA-01733: view not updatable ``` This view is **NOT updatable** because: - It uses the aggregate function `COUNT()`. - It uses `GROUP BY`. - Per the SQL standard, both violate updatability. **Requirement 2 — `GreenBoatBan` View (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW GreenBoatBan AS SELECT DISTINCT s.sid, s.sname FROM Sailors s JOIN Reserves r ON s.sid = r.sid JOIN Boats b ON r.bid = b.bid WHERE b.color = 'green' WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION; ``` **Requirement 3 — Reservation Cap Assertion (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE ASSERTION max_boats_per_sailor CHECK ( 5 >= ALL ( SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT bid) FROM Reserves GROUP BY sid ) ); ``` --- #### Part (b) — 7 marks **`CASCADED` vs `LOCAL` — Conceptual Difference (3 marks):** | Aspect | `CASCADED CHECK OPTION` | `LOCAL CHECK OPTION` | |---|---|---| | **Scope of check** | All views in the dependency chain | Only the current view's predicate | | **Transitive parents** | All predicates are checked | Only the direct parent's predicate | | **Default** | Default if just `WITH CHECK OPTION` | Must be specified explicitly | **Example 1 — `CASCADED` rejects (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW EmpHighSalary AS SELECT * FROM Employee WHERE salary > 30000 WITH CHECK OPTION; CREATE VIEW EmpHR AS SELECT * FROM EmpHighSalary WHERE dept = 'HR' WITH CASCADED CHECK OPTION; INSERT INTO EmpHR VALUES (101, 'Ram', 25000, 'HR'); -- REJECTED: salary <= 30000 fails V1's predicate (CASCADED) ``` **Example 2 — `LOCAL` permits (2 marks):** ```sql CREATE VIEW EmpHR2 AS SELECT * FROM EmpHighSalary WHERE dept = 'HR' WITH LOCAL CHECK OPTION; INSERT INTO EmpHR2 VALUES (102, 'Shyam', 25000, 'HR'); -- ACCEPTED: only V1's direct predicate (dept = 'HR') is checked. -- V1's salary predicate is NOT enforced under LOCAL. ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting `OR REPLACE`** when modifying a view — students often `DROP VIEW` and recreate, losing privileges. Use `CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW` to retain grants. > 2. **Assuming all views are updatable** — examiners test whether you can identify a non-updatable view (joins, aggregates, `DISTINCT`). > 3. **Confusing assertion with check constraint** — an assertion must reference **multiple tables**; otherwise it is just a `CHECK` clause. > 4. **Not mentioning DBMS compatibility** for assertions — writing `CREATE ASSERTION` in Oracle/MySQL without acknowledging the syntax error **costs 1–2 marks**. > 5. **Wrong `WITH CHECK OPTION` placement** — the clause goes **after** the `WHERE` clause, not before. > 6. **Forgetting `CASCADE` in `DROP VIEW`** — if another view depends on the one you are dropping, the operation will fail unless you specify `CASCADE`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Fire Revision Points (Read 5 minutes before the exam):** - ⭐ A **view** is a **virtual** table; it stores only the `SELECT` query, not the data. - ⭐ The view definition is kept in the **system catalog / data dictionary**. - ⭐ `CREATE VIEW` syntax: `CREATE VIEW name AS subquery [WITH CHECK OPTION]`. - ⭐ `DROP VIEW name [CASCADE | RESTRICT]` removes the view definition. - ⭐ `WITH CHECK OPTION` ensures inserted/updated rows still satisfy the view's `WHERE`. - ⭐ `CASCADED` checks **all ancestor predicates**; `LOCAL` checks only the **immediate parent**. - ⭐ **Updatable views** must be based on **a single table** with **no aggregates, no `DISTINCT`, no `GROUP BY`, no subqueries**. - ⭐ Joins, aggregations, and unions typically produce **non-updatable views**. - ⭐ `INSERT INTO view` propagates to the underlying base table **if** the view is updatable. - ⭐ An **assertion** is a database-wide constraint checked after every transaction. - ⭐ Syntax: `CREATE ASSERTION name CHECK (predicate);` - ⭐ Assertions are supported in **PostgreSQL** but **not** in standard Oracle/MySQL builds. - ⭐ Use **triggers** as a fallback for assertion-like behavior in unsupported DBMS. - ⭐ Views provide **security**, **simplicity**, and **logical data independence**. - ⭐ **Materialized views** physically store results and need explicit `REFRESH`. - ⭐ Common errors: `ORA-01733` (view not updatable), `ORA-01402` (CHECK OPTION violation). --- > **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — DBMS Lab (PCCSL408) | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Creation of Procedures, Triggers and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Creation of Procedures, Triggers and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme DBMS Lab syllabus, Module Experiment 2 mandates hands-on implementation of three fundamental **Persistent Stored Program Units (PSPU)** of the SQL/PSM (Persistent Stored Modules) framework. The formal definitions, as aligned with the SQL:2011 standard and standard RDBMS documentation, are as follows:

> [!NOTE]
> **Stored Procedure** — A *named, precompiled block of procedural SQL* stored persistently in the system catalog (`pg_proc` in PostgreSQL, `mysql.proc` in MySQL, `USER_OBJECTS` in Oracle). It is invoked explicitly using the `CALL` (or `EXEC` in Oracle SQL*Plus) statement and **may return zero, one, or multiple values** through `OUT` parameters. It does NOT participate in SQL expressions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stored Function** — A *named, precompiled routine* that **must return exactly one value** via the `RETURNS` clause. Unlike procedures, functions can be embedded directly inside SQL `SELECT` statements, `WHERE` clauses, and `CHECK` constraints (subject to determinism). This is the critical distinction between procedures and functions in KTU Board Examinations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Trigger** — A *system-cataloged, event-driven procedure* that is *automatically invoked* (fired) by the RDBMS engine when a specified **Data Manipulation Language (DML)** event (`INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE`) — or in advanced RDBMS, a **Data Definition Language (DDL)** event (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP`) — occurs on a specified table or view. Triggers have **no explicit user invocation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

To make these three concepts intuitively obvious before any code is written, consider the analogy of a **municipality office (RDBMS Server)**:

| PSPU | Municipal Analogy | Key Behavioral Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Procedure** | A *Grievance Counter* — a citizen (user) must physically walk up and submit a form (CALL). | **Explicit invocation** by caller. |
| **Function** | A *Stamp Duty Calculator* — embedded in every property form (SQL expression). | **Used inline** in SQL; returns a single value. |
| **Trigger** | A *Smoke/Fire Alarm* — automatically activates the moment smoke is detected (DML event), without any human calling it. | **Implicit, event-driven** execution. |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Memory Trick (Examiner's Pattern):** "A **P**rocedure is **P**honed-in by the user; a **F**unction is **F**itted into a SQL expression; a **T**rigger **T**riggers itself on table events."

## 1.3 The Three Execution Granularities — A Critical KTU Distinction

Every PSPU operates at one of three granularities, and KTU Board Questions frequently test the difference:

- **Statement-Level Trigger:** Fires **once per SQL statement**, regardless of how many rows are affected.
- **Row-Level Trigger:** Fires **once per affected row**. The engine provides `OLD` and `NEW` pseudo-records.
- **Block-Level Routine:** A procedure/function body is executed as a single block; no per-row iteration unless an explicit `FOR` loop / cursor is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The default in **MySQL** is `FOR EACH ROW` (row-level), while the default in **Oracle/PostgreSQL** is statement-level. Always read the RDBMS specification in the experiment manual.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Syntax Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic CREATE Anatomy

All three PSPUs share a common **6-part skeleton** that examiners love to test:

1. **Header** (`CREATE [OR REPLACE]`)
2. **Unit Type & Name** (`PROCEDURE name` / `FUNCTION name` / `TRIGGER name`)
3. **Parameter / Event Declaration Block**
4. **Declarative Section** (`DECLARE` / `AS` / `IS`) — local variables, cursors, exception names
5. **Executable Section** (`BEGIN ... END`) — mandatory logic body
6. **Exception Handler** (optional `EXCEPTION ... WHEN ...`)

## 2.2 Why Each Construct Exists — The "Why" Behind the "How"

- **Why Procedures?** To **enforce modularity, reduce network traffic, encapsulate business rules, and provide a security layer** (users can `EXECUTE` a procedure without having `SELECT` privilege on base tables — known as the *definer-rights principle*).
- **Why Functions?** To enable **reuse of derived logic inside SQL queries** (e.g., a `calculate_tax(salary)` function can appear in every `SELECT` list and `WHERE` clause).
- **Why Triggers?** To enforce **declarative constraints that cannot be expressed otherwise**: audit logging, automatic denormalization, derived-column maintenance, cascading derived values, and prevention of invalid state transitions.

## 2.3 KTU Syntax Cheat Sheet (RDBMS-Agnostic Master Template)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` delimiters in place of the literal pipe `\` character to preserve the markdown table structure, as per the engine's table-safety protocol.

| Construct | Generic Syntax Template | Returns Value? | Callable in SELECT? | Invocation Mode |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PROCEDURE** | `CREATE [OR REPLACE] PROCEDURE name([IN $\vert$ OUT $\vert$ INOUT] param datatype) AS \mid IS $<$declarations$>$ BEGIN $<$statements$>$ [EXCEPTION $<$handlers$>$] END;` | No (uses OUT params) | **NO** | Explicit `CALL proc(args);` |
| **FUNCTION** | `CREATE [OR REPLACE] FUNCTION name(params) RETURNS datatype AS $<$declarations$>$ BEGIN $<$statements$>$ RETURN value; END;` | **YES (exactly 1)** | **YES** | Inline expression or explicit call |
| **TRIGGER** | `CREATE [OR REPLACE] TRIGGER name {BEFORE $\vert$ AFTER $\vert$ INSTEAD OF} {INSERT $\vert$ UPDATE $\vert$ DELETE} [OF col] ON table [FOR EACH ROW] [WHEN(condition)] $<$declarations$>$ BEGIN $<$statements$>$ END;` | No | **NO** | **AUTOMATIC** on event |

## 2.4 The Parameter Modes — A Common 3-Mark Question

| Mode | Direction | Initialized by | Visible inside routine? | Usable in Functions? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IN** | Caller $\to$ Routine | Caller (read-only copy) | Yes (read-only) | **YES** |
| **OUT** | Routine $\to$ Caller | Uninitialized (NULL) | Yes (writable) | **NO** (disallowed in pure functions) |
| **INOUT** | Caller $\leftrightarrow$ Routine | Caller | Yes (read/write) | **NO** |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production engineering, these constructs power:

- **Banking Core Systems:** Triggers enforce *minimum balance* and generate *audit logs* on every transaction.
- **E-Commerce Order Pipelines:** Stored procedures encapsulate the **4-step atomicity contract** (deduct stock $\to$ charge card $\to$ create shipment $\to$ notify vendor).
- **IoT Telemetry Databases:** Functions compute derived sensor metrics (rolling averages, threshold breaches) inline within the dashboard's `SELECT` query.
- **ERPNext / SAP Backends:** Triggers maintain **denormalized counters** (e.g., `total_orders = SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders WHERE customer_id = NEW.id`) automatically.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three implementations below use the canonical **Employee-Department** schema that KTU examiners repeatedly rotate across question papers. Adapt the table names if your manual specifies a different schema (e.g., `Library`, `Student-Mark`, `Bank-Account`).

## 3.1 Foundation — Reference Schema (DDL Setup)

Execute this block once at the top of your lab record to establish the working schema:

```sql
CREATE TABLE Department (
    dept_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    dept_name VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL,
    location  VARCHAR(40)
);

CREATE TABLE Employee (
    emp_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
    emp_name   VARCHAR(40) NOT NULL,
    salary     DECIMAL(10,2),
    dept_id    INT,
    FOREIGN KEY (dept_id) REFERENCES Department(dept_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Audit_Log (
    log_id      INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,  -- use SERIAL in PostgreSQL
    emp_id      INT,
    old_salary  DECIMAL(10,2),
    new_salary  DECIMAL(10,2),
    changed_on  TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

---

## 3.2 Implementation 1 — A Stored Procedure

### 3.2.1 The Logic Contract

**Goal:** Atomically *promote an employee's salary* by a given percentage, with a 10% upper cap to prevent runaway increments. This is a classic 7-mark KTU Part-B sub-question.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Code Construction

```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE PROCEDURE promote_employee(
    IN  p_emp_id   INT,
    IN  p_percent  DECIMAL(5,2),
    OUT p_status   VARCHAR(50)
)
BEGIN
    DECLARE v_current_salary DECIMAL(10,2);
    DECLARE v_new_salary     DECIMAL(10,2);
    DECLARE v_capped_salary  DECIMAL(10,2);

    -- Step 1: Fetch the current salary
    SELECT salary INTO v_current_salary
    FROM   Employee
    WHERE  emp_id = p_emp_id;

    -- Step 2: Compute the raw new salary
    SET v_new_salary = v_current_salary * (1 + p_percent / 100);

    -- Step 3: Apply the 10% upper cap
    IF v_new_salary > v_current_salary * 1.10 THEN
        SET v_capped_salary = v_current_salary * 1.10;
    ELSE
        SET v_capped_salary = v_new_salary;
    END IF;

    -- Step 4: Persist the change
    UPDATE Employee
    SET    salary = v_capped_salary
    WHERE  emp_id = p_emp_id;

    SET p_status = CONCAT('Promotion done. New salary = ', v_capped_salary);
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

### 3.2.3 Line-by-Line Reasoning

| Line Segment | Purpose & Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `DELIMITER //` | **Mandatory preamble** in MySQL. The standard `;` terminator would prematurely end the `CREATE` statement at the first inner `;`. |
| `IN p_emp_id INT, IN p_percent DECIMAL(5,2), OUT p_status VARCHAR(50)` | **Mixed parameter modes.** Inputs are read-only; the output status is written back to the caller. |
| `DECLARE v_current_salary DECIMAL(10,2);` | **Local variable** with explicit precision matching the `salary` column. KTU expects this alignment. |
| `SELECT salary INTO v_current_salary ...` | The `SELECT ... INTO` pattern is the only legal way to assign a column value to a scalar variable in standard SQL/PSM. |
| `IF ... THEN ... ELSE ... END IF;` | Demonstrates **conditional flow control** — frequently tested as 3-mark short notes. |
| `UPDATE Employee SET salary = v_capped_salary` | The **persistence step** that mutates base state. |
| `DELIMITER ;` | **Restores** the standard statement terminator after the routine body. |

### 3.2.4 Invocation Block

```sql
-- Declare a host variable to receive the OUT parameter
CALL promote_employee(101, 8.5, @msg);
SELECT @msg;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **PostgreSQL / Oracle equivalence:** Replace `DECIMAL` with `NUMBER`/`NUMERIC`; replace `OUT` parameters with `INOUT` or use a `RETURNS` clause in a function instead.

---

## 3.3 Implementation 2 — A Stored Function

### 3.3.1 The Logic Contract

**Goal:** Compute the *annual tax* for an employee based on a slab-based tax rule, returning a single `DECIMAL` value. Functions are pure value-returners — perfect for inline SQL usage.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Code Construction

```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE FUNCTION calculate_tax(p_salary DECIMAL(10,2))
RETURNS DECIMAL(10,2)
DETERMINISTIC
BEGIN
    DECLARE v_annual DECIMAL(12,2);
    DECLARE v_tax    DECIMAL(12,2);

    SET v_annual = p_salary * 12;

    IF v_annual <= 250000 THEN
        SET v_tax = 0;
    ELSEIF v_annual <= 500000 THEN
        SET v_tax = (v_annual - 250000) * 0.05;
    ELSEIF v_annual <= 1000000 THEN
        SET v_tax = 12500 + (v_annual - 500000) * 0.20;
    ELSE
        SET v_tax = 112500 + (v_annual - 1000000) * 0.30;
    END IF;

    RETURN v_tax;
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

### 3.3.3 Critical Clause Analysis

| Clause | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| `RETURNS DECIMAL(10,2)` | **Mandatory** in functions; declares the return datatype. |
| `DETERMINISTIC` | **Optimizer hint** — declares that the same input *always* produces the same output. Required when the function is used inside a `VIEW`, a `CHECK` constraint, or generated columns. |
| `RETURN v_tax;` | The **single exit point** that hands the value back to the caller. |
| **No `OUT` parameters** | Functions **cannot** use `OUT`/`INOUT` modes. This is a KTU favorite distinction. |

### 3.3.4 Inline SQL Invocation — The Killer Feature

```sql
SELECT emp_id, emp_name, salary, calculate_tax(salary) AS annual_tax
FROM   Employee
WHERE  calculate_tax(salary) > 50000;
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "How is a function different from a procedure?", the *only* correct evidence is to show that the function is callable from a `SELECT` list and a `WHERE` clause. A procedure cannot do this.

---

## 3.4 Implementation 3 — A Trigger

### 3.4.1 The Logic Contract

**Goal:** Maintain a **denormalized audit trail**. Every time an employee's salary is updated, the OLD and NEW salaries must be logged in `Audit_Log` automatically — without the user calling anything.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Code Construction

```sql
DELIMITER //
CREATE TRIGGER trg_salary_audit
AFTER UPDATE OF salary ON Employee
FOR EACH ROW
BEGIN
    -- Guard: ignore no-op updates where salary didn't actually change
    IF OLD.salary <> NEW.salary THEN
        INSERT INTO Audit_Log(emp_id, old_salary, new_salary)
        VALUES (OLD.emp_id, OLD.salary, NEW.salary);
    END IF;
END //
DELIMITER ;
```

### 3.4.3 The Five Mandatory Trigger Components — Each Tested Individually

| Component | In Our Trigger | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Timing** | `AFTER` | Fires *after* the row is updated. `BEFORE` is used when you want to *modify* the row before persistence. |
| **Event** | `UPDATE OF salary` | The `OF column` clause restricts the trigger to fire **only** when the `salary` column is in the `SET` list — a powerful optimization. |
| **Level** | `FOR EACH ROW` | Row-level trigger. `OLD` and `NEW` are available. |
| **Subject Table** | `ON Employee` | The trigger is *bound* to this table. |
| **Trigger Action** | `INSERT INTO Audit_Log` | The body executes once per qualifying row. |

### 3.4.4 The `OLD` and `NEW` Pseudo-Records — The Most-Tested Concept

| Event | `OLD` Available? | `NEW` Available? | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `INSERT` | **NO** | YES | Pre-insert value doesn't exist. |
| `UPDATE` | YES | YES | Both versions accessible. |
| `DELETE` | YES | **NO** | The deleted row vanishes. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing `OLD.emp_id` in an `INSERT` trigger will cause a *compilation error* (not a runtime error). Examiners trap students with this.

### 3.4.5 Demonstration of Automatic Firing

```sql
UPDATE Employee SET salary = 75000 WHERE emp_id = 101;
-- The user wrote ONE statement; the trigger implicitly issued ONE INSERT into Audit_Log.

SELECT * FROM Audit_Log WHERE emp_id = 101;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Effect:** The single `UPDATE` statement caused **two** row operations: one `UPDATE` on `Employee` and one `INSERT` on `Audit_Log`. The audit row was *never* explicitly requested by the user. This is the essence of event-driven automation.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Frequently Asked as 3-Mark Questions)

| Property | Procedure | Function | Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Invoked by | `CALL` statement | SQL expression or call | Automatic event |
| Returns | Zero/Many values via `OUT` | Exactly one value | Nothing (side-effects only) |
| Usable in `SELECT` | **No** | **Yes** | **No** |
| `DML` allowed inside | Yes | Restricted (no `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on the triggering table in some RDBMS) | Yes (with care) |
| Parameters | `IN`, `OUT`, `INOUT` | Only `IN` | None (event data via `OLD`/`NEW`) |
| Compiled and stored | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Transactional | Yes (atomic body) | Yes | Yes (part of the firing statement's transaction) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The DBMS Stored Program Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application Layer SQLPLUS or PHP Frontend]
    Engine[SQL Parser and Optimizer Engine]
    Catalog[(System Catalog USER OBJECTS pg_proc mysql.proc)]
    ProcStore[(Procedure Code Repository)]
    FuncStore[(Function Code Repository)]
    TrigStore[(Trigger Code Repository)]
    TblDept[(Department Table)]
    TblEmp[(Employee Table)]
    TblAudit[(Audit Log Table)]
    ExecEnv[PLSQL Execution Engine Memory]
    OutParam[OUT Parameter Channel]

    Client -- Submit CREATE DDL --> Engine
    Engine -- Verify and Compile --> Catalog
    Engine -- Store Body --> ProcStore
    Engine -- Store Body --> FuncStore
    Engine -- Bind to Table --> TrigStore

    Catalog -.Metadata.-> ExecEnv

    Client -- CALL procedure args --> Engine
    Engine -- Load Body --> ExecEnv
    ExecEnv -- OUT result --> OutParam
    OutParam -- Return --> Client

    Client -- SELECT fn from Employee --> Engine
    Engine -- Inline Invoke --> ExecEnv

    Client -- UPDATE Employee --> Engine
    Engine -- Detect Event --> TrigStore
    TrigStore -- Auto Fire --> ExecEnv
    ExecEnv -- Side Effect INSERT --> TblAudit

    TblEmp -.Trigger Subject.-> TrigStore
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The dashed arrows represent *metadata references* (the catalog points to compiled bodies). The solid arrows are *runtime invocation paths*. Note how the trigger has a unique path: it never receives a call from the client; the engine itself routes the DML to the trigger repository.

## 4.2 Execution Sequence: A Single UPDATE Statement Triggering a Row-Level Audit

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Session
    participant E as SQL Engine
    participant T1 as Employee Table
    participant TR as Trigger trg_salary_audit
    participant T2 as Audit_Log Table
    participant TX as Transaction Manager

    U->>E: UPDATE Employee SET salary=75000 WHERE emp_id=101
    E->>TX: BEGIN TRANSACTION
    E->>T1: Locate row emp_id=101 FOR UPDATE
    T1-->>E: OLD row image salary=60000
    E->>TR: BEFORE UPDATE fired (if defined)
    TR-->>E: Pre-processing complete
    E->>T1: Write NEW row image salary=75000
    E->>TR: AFTER UPDATE fired
    TR->>T2: INSERT INTO Audit_Log values 101 60000 75000
    T2-->>TR: 1 row inserted
    TR-->>E: Trigger execution complete
    E->>TX: COMMIT
    TX-->>U: 1 row affected commit success
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Interpretation:** Trace step 6 $\to$ 7 $\to$ 8 carefully. The audit insert happens *inside* the same transaction as the salary update. If the audit insert fails (e.g., constraint violation), the salary update is **rolled back** automatically — a property called *trigger atomicity* that KTU examiners love to ask about.

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right PSPU

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to encapsulate business logic]) --> Q1{Does it need to fire automatically on table events?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> TrigPick[TRIGGER]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Must it return a single value usable in a SELECT expression?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> FuncPick[FUNCTION]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need to return multiple values or perform multiple DML operations?}

    Q3 -- Yes --> ProcPick[PROCEDURE]
    Q3 -- No --> ProcPick

    TrigPick --> End1([End])
    FuncPick --> End1
    ProcPick --> End1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Differentiate between a stored procedure and a stored function. List **any three** points.

**Model Answer:**

A stored procedure and a stored function are both named, precompiled PL/SQL blocks stored in the database catalog, but they differ as follows:

1. **Return Value:** A *procedure* returns zero, one, or multiple values through `OUT` or `INOUT` parameters. A *function* **must** return exactly one value via the `RETURNS` clause and a `RETURN` statement.
