topics.title: Perform basic CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on a Cassandra table
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Perform basic CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on a Cassandra table ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Apache Cassandra** is a highly scalable, distributed **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database** management system designed to handle very large amounts of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data across multiple commodity servers, providing high availability with no single point of failure. It uses a **wide-column store** data model and is built on the principles of the **Dynamo paper (Amazon)** combined with **Google's Bigtable** data model. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCSL408 - DBMS Lab)**, Experiment 2 requires students to perform fundamental **CRUD operations** (Create, Read, Update, Delete) on a Cassandra table using the **Cassandra Query Language (CQL)** through the **CQL Shell (cqlsh)** interactive terminal. | Term | Formal Definition | |---|---| | **CQL** | Cassandra Query Language - SQL-like syntax for interacting with Cassandra | | **cqlsh** | Command-line client for executing CQL commands | | **Keyspace** | Top-level container that defines data replication strategy (analogous to a database) | | **Table** | A column family consisting of rows and columns (analogous to a relational table) | | **Partition Key** | The primary lookup key used to distribute data across cluster nodes | | **Clustering Key** | Columns that determine the sort order of rows within a partition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment tests Course Outcomes **CO1** (Apply database concepts, DDL, DML commands) and **CO2** (Use NoSQL tools like Cassandra for real-world applications). Students must be able to independently start cqlsh, define a keyspace, and execute all four CRUD operations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of **Cassandra** as a massive **giant library with no single librarian**. Instead of one central registry (as in MySQL), data is duplicated across many library branches (nodes). If one branch catches fire, the books are still safe in others. Now consider **CRUD operations** in this library: - **CREATE (C)** = Adding a brand new book to the library shelves (`INSERT` command) - **READ (R)** = Searching for a book by its unique catalog ID (`SELECT` command) - **UPDATE (U)** = Editing a book's details (like changing its price or edition) (`UPDATE` command) - **DELETE (D)** = Permanently removing a book from the shelves (`DELETE` command) The **Keyspace** is like the **library building itself** (it defines rules about how many copies of each book are kept), and the **Table** is like a **specific bookshelf** inside that library (the Student Record shelf, the Library Book shelf, etc.). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Example:** Companies like **Netflix** (user watch history), **Instagram** (inbox messages), and **Apple** (iMessage data) use Cassandra because it scales linearly — just add more nodes to handle more load. The CRUD operations remain the same regardless of cluster size. ### 1.3 Engineering Metrics and Standards Standard metrics students must remember for KTU practical examinations: - **Default Cassandra Port:** **9042** (for CQL native protocol) - **Default cqlsh Port:** **9042** (for interactive shell connection) - **Default Replication Factor:** **1** (single node) or **3** (production clusters) - **cqlsh Prompt:** `cqlsh>` (regular) and `cqlsh:keyspace_name>` (after selecting keyspace) - **Standard Data Types:** `text`, `int`, `bigint`, `float`, `double`, `boolean`, `uuid`, `timestamp`, `date`, `time` ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Cassandra CRUD Unlike traditional RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL), Cassandra's CRUD model is built on the **CQL (Cassandra Query Language)** standard, which mimics SQL but operates under fundamentally different design principles: #### 2.1.1 The CAP Theorem Position Cassandra is an **AP system** (Availability + Partition tolerance) in the **CAP theorem**, meaning it prioritizes being always available even during network partitions, with **eventual consistency** rather than strong consistency. #### 2.1.2 Why CQL Exists CQL was introduced in **Cassandra 3.0** to lower the learning curve for SQL developers. Before CQL, developers had to use the **Thrift API** which was complex and required object serialization knowledge. #### 2.1.3 CRUD Mapping Table | CRUD Operation | SQL Equivalent | CQL Command | Why It Works | |---|---|---|---| | **C**reate | `INSERT INTO ... VALUES` | `INSERT INTO ... USING TTL` | Writes are append-only; data is written to a commit log first, then to memtable, finally flushed to SSTable on disk | | **R**ead | `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE` | `SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE` | Reads use the partition key to find the correct node; an index lookup is avoided | | **U**pdate | `UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE` | `UPDATE ... SET ... WHERE` | Updates are technically **write operations** — the old value is marked as a tombstone, and a new value is written | | **D**elete | `DELETE FROM ... WHERE` | `DELETE FROM ... WHERE` | Deletes create a **tombstone** (logical marker) with a TTL; actual data is purged during **compaction** | ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield CQL Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU Practical Exam:** Every cell is a high-yield command pattern that examiners test. | Operation Category | CQL Command Pattern | Mandatory Syntax Elements | KTU Examiner Focus | |---|---|---|---| | **Create Keyspace** | `CREATE KEYSPACE name WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': N};` | `WITH replication = {...}` clause | Replication strategy choice | | **Use Keyspace** | `USE keyspace_name;` | Semicolon terminator | Prompt change | | **Create Table** | `CREATE TABLE name (col type, col2 type, PRIMARY KEY (partition_key, clustering_col));` | `PRIMARY KEY` clause mandatory | Composite primary key structure | | **Insert Row** | `INSERT INTO name (col1, col2) VALUES (val1, val2);` | Must include **all** primary key components | Tuple order in PRIMARY KEY | | **Read All Rows** | `SELECT * FROM name;` | `*` for full select | Limit clause usage | | **Read Filtered** | `SELECT * FROM name WHERE partition_key = value;` | Cannot filter on non-indexed columns | Partition key usage | | **Update Row** | `UPDATE name SET col = value WHERE partition_key = value;` | Must specify full primary key | SET keyword usage | | **Delete Row** | `DELETE FROM name WHERE partition_key = value;` | Must specify full primary key | Tombstone creation | | **Drop Table** | `DROP TABLE name;` | Data definition change | Clean-up operation | | **Describe** | `DESCRIBE TABLE name;` | Schema introspection | Metadata viewing | ### 2.3 CQL Data Types (Most Common in KTU Labs) | CQL Type | Equivalent in RDBMS | Storage Size | Example Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | `text` | `VARCHAR` | Up to 2GB | Names, emails, descriptions | | `int` | `INT` | 4 bytes | Age, quantity | | `bigint` | `BIGINT` | 8 bytes | Timestamps in ms, large counters | | `float` | `FLOAT` | 4 bytes | Temperature readings | | `double` | `DOUBLE` | 8 bytes | Precise measurements | | `boolean` | `BOOLEAN` | 1 byte | True/false flags | | `uuid` | `UUID` | 16 bytes | Unique identifiers across nodes | | `timestamp` | `DATETIME` | 8 bytes | Event time, log creation | | `date` | `DATE` | 4 bytes | Birth date, registration date | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Cassandra's CRUD model powers: - **Time-series IoT data** (sensors write millions of inserts per second) - **Messaging platforms** (WhatsApp, Facebook Messenger store conversation history) - **Recommendation engines** (user-item interactions) - **Gaming leaderboards** (frequent updates to player scores) - **Financial trading platforms** (high-velocity write transactions) > [!NOTE] > The same CRUD pattern you learn in this KTU lab experiment is used by production engineers at **Uber, Spotify, and Twitter** to handle billions of writes per day. The syntax is identical; only cluster size changes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Pre-Lab Setup Steps Before any CRUD operation, the Cassandra environment must be operational. The complete setup sequence is: **Step 1: Verify Cassandra Installation** ```bash # Open terminal and check Cassandra version cqlsh --version ``` **Expected Output:** ``` cqlsh 6.2.0 ``` **Step 2: Start Cassandra Server (Linux/Mac)** ```bash # For systemd-based Linux sudo systemctl start cassandra # For older systems sudo service cassandra start # Or start in foreground for debugging cassandra -f ``` **Step 3: Connect to cqlsh** ```bash # Default connection (localhost, port 9042) cqlsh # If Cassandra is on a different host cqlsh 192.168.1.100 9042 ``` **Expected Prompt:** ``` Connected to Test Cluster at 127.0.0.1:9042. [cqlsh 6.2.0 | Cassandra 4.1.x | CQL spec 3.4.5 | Native protocol v5] Use HELP for help. cqlsh> ``` ### 3.2 CREATE Operation — Step-by-Step The CREATE operation has **three sub-phases**: Create Keyspace → Use Keyspace → Create Table. #### 3.2.1 Create a Keyspace ```cql cqlsh> CREATE KEYSPACE student_db ... WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 1}; ``` **Explanation of each parameter:** - `student_db` — Logical name of the keyspace (acts as a database name) - `'class': 'SimpleStrategy'` — Replication strategy for **single data center** environments (used in labs) - `'replication_factor': 1` — Number of copies of data; **1** is sufficient for a single-node lab setup > [!IMPORTANT] > **For production multi-DC deployments**, use `NetworkTopologyStrategy` instead: > ```cql > CREATE KEYSPACE prod_db WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'DC1': 3, 'DC2': 2}; > ``` #### 3.2.2 Use the Keyspace ```cql cqlsh> USE student_db; ``` **Output:** Prompt changes from `cqlsh>` to `cqlsh:student_db>`, confirming the active keyspace. #### 3.2.3 Create a Table ```cql cqlsh:student_db> CREATE TABLE student ( ... roll_no int, ... name text, ... department text, ... cgpa float, ... PRIMARY KEY (roll_no) ... ); ``` **Explanation of the PRIMARY KEY structure:** - `roll_no` is the **partition key** — every row is uniquely identified by it - Cassandra hashes the partition key to determine which node stores the row - Without a PRIMARY KEY, the CREATE TABLE will throw an error #### 3.2.4 Insert Rows (CREATE record) ```cql cqlsh:student_db> INSERT INTO student (roll_no, name, department, cgpa) ... VALUES (101, 'Arjun Krishna', 'CSE', 8.7); cqlsh:student_db> INSERT INTO student (roll_no, name, department, cgpa) ... VALUES (102, 'Meera Nair', 'ECE', 9.1); cqlsh:student_db> INSERT INTO student (roll_no, name, department, cgpa) ... VALUES (103, 'Rahul Menon', 'MECH', 7.8); ``` **Why order matters in VALUES:** The values must correspond positionally (or by name) to the columns specified in the INSERT statement. Always list columns explicitly to avoid silent bugs. ### 3.3 READ Operation — Step-by-Step The READ operation uses the `SELECT` statement, which is identical in structure to SQL. #### 3.3.1 Read All Rows ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT * FROM student; ``` **Expected Output:** ``` roll_no | department | cgpa | name ---------+------------+------+---------------- 103 | MECH | 7.8 | Rahul Menon 101 | CSE | 8.7 | Arjun Krishna 102 | ECE | 9.1 | Meera Nair (3 rows) ``` > [!NOTE] > Notice the **order is not insertion order**. Cassandra sorts by partition key hash. This is normal and expected behavior. #### 3.3.2 Read Specific Columns ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT name, cgpa FROM student; ``` #### 3.3.3 Read with WHERE Clause (Partition Key Filter) ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT * FROM student WHERE roll_no = 102; ``` **Expected Output:** ``` roll_no | department | cgpa | name ---------+------------+------+------------ 102 | ECE | 9.1 | Meera Nair (1 row) ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Trying to filter on a **non-primary-key** column (e.g., `WHERE department = 'CSE'`) will fail unless an index is created. This is a **frequently asked exam question**. #### 3.3.4 Read with LIMIT ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT * FROM student LIMIT 2; ``` ### 3.4 UPDATE Operation — Step-by-Step Updates in Cassandra are **not** in-place modifications. They are implemented as **write operations** that create a new version of the row and mark the old version as a tombstone. ```cql cqlsh:student_db> UPDATE student ... SET cgpa = 8.9, department = 'CSE-AI' ... WHERE roll_no = 101; ``` **Explanation of the WHERE clause:** The WHERE clause **must include the partition key** (and clustering key, if defined). Otherwise, Cassandra throws the error: ``` InvalidRequest: Error from server: code=2200 [Invalid query] message="Cannot execute UPDATE without all PRIMARY KEY columns" ``` #### 3.4.1 Verify the Update ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT * FROM student WHERE roll_no = 101; ``` **Expected Output:** ``` roll_no | department | cgpa | name ---------+------------+------+---------------- 101 | CSE-AI | 8.9 | Arjun Krishna ``` #### 3.4.2 Update with TTL (Time To Live) Cassandra supports automatic expiration of data: ```cql cqlsh:student_db> UPDATE student USING TTL 3600 ... SET cgpa = 9.5 ... WHERE roll_no = 102; ``` This row's new value will **automatically delete itself after 3600 seconds (1 hour)**. The underlying mechanism is a built-in tombstone with a timestamp. ### 3.5 DELETE Operation — Step-by-Step The DELETE operation in Cassandra creates a **tombstone** (a logical delete marker), not a physical row removal. Physical deletion happens later during **compaction**. ```cql cqlsh:student_db> DELETE FROM student WHERE roll_no = 103; ``` **Expected Output:** No rows are returned immediately, but verification shows the row is gone. #### 3.5.1 Verify the Deletion ```cql cqlsh:student_db> SELECT * FROM student; ``` **Expected Output:** ``` roll_no | department | cgpa | name ---------+------------+------+---------------- 101 | CSE-AI | 8.9 | Arjun Krishna 102 | ECE | 9.1 | Meera Nair (2 rows) ``` #### 3.5.2 Delete a Specific Column ```cql cqlsh:student_db> DELETE cgpa FROM student WHERE roll_no = 102; ``` This deletes **only the `cgpa` column** of roll_no 102, leaving the rest intact. #### 3.5.3 Drop the Table (End of Lab Cleanup) ```cql cqlsh:student_db> DROP TABLE student; cqlsh:student_db> DROP KEYSPACE student_db; ``` ### 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — CRUD Syntax Breakdown | Operation | Minimum Required Clause | Forbidden Patterns in KTU Exam | Common Error Message | |---|---|---|---| | **CREATE** | `PRIMARY KEY (...)` | No PRIMARY KEY definition | "No PRIMARY KEY specified" | | **INSERT** | All PRIMARY KEY components | Missing primary key columns | "Some partition key parts are missing" | | **READ** | `SELECT` keyword | `SELECT *` discouraged for production | Generic permission errors | | **UPDATE** | `SET col = val WHERE pk = val` | Omitting WHERE clause | "Cannot execute UPDATE without PRIMARY KEY" | | **DELETE** | `WHERE pk = val` | Omitting WHERE clause | "Cannot execute DELETE without PRIMARY KEY" | ### 3.7 Complete Working Example (Copy-Paste Ready for KTU Lab) ```cql -- STEP 1: Create Keyspace CREATE KEYSPACE college WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 1}; -- STEP 2: Use Keyspace USE college; -- STEP 3: Create Table CREATE TABLE library_books ( book_id int, title text, author text, year int, PRIMARY KEY (book_id) ); -- STEP 4: CREATE Operation (Insert) INSERT INTO library_books (book_id, title, author, year) VALUES (1, 'Database System Concepts', 'Korth', 2019); INSERT INTO library_books (book_id, title, author, year) VALUES (2, 'Clean Code', 'Robert Martin', 2008); INSERT INTO library_books (book_id, title, author, year) VALUES (3, 'Introduction to Algorithms', 'Cormen', 2009); -- STEP 5: READ Operation SELECT * FROM library_books; SELECT * FROM library_books WHERE book_id = 2; -- STEP 6: UPDATE Operation UPDATE library_books SET year = 2021 WHERE book_id = 1; -- STEP 7: DELETE Operation DELETE FROM library_books WHERE book_id = 3; -- STEP 8: Verify Final State SELECT * FROM library_books; -- STEP 9: Cleanup (Optional) DROP TABLE library_books; DROP KEYSPACE college; ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU Practical Exam:** Always show **all CRUD operations** with **outputs** in the lab record. Examiners specifically check for the **prompt change** from `cqlsh>` to `cqlsh:college>` after the `USE` command. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete CRUD Lifecycle on a Cassandra Table ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start cqlsh]) --> CreateKeyspace[CREATE KEYSPACE college
SimpleStrategy, RF=1] CreateKeyspace --> UseKS[USE college] UseKS --> CreateTable[CREATE TABLE student
roll_no PK int] CreateTable --> Insert1[INSERT roll_no 101
Arjun CSE 8.7] CreateTable --> Insert2[INSERT roll_no 102
Meera ECE 9.1] CreateTable --> Insert3[INSERT roll_no 103
Rahul MECH 7.8] Insert1 --> SelectAll[SELECT FROM student] Insert2 --> SelectAll Insert3 --> SelectAll SelectAll --> SelectFilter[SELECT WHERE roll_no equals 102] SelectFilter --> UpdateOp[UPDATE SET cgpa equals 8.9
WHERE roll_no equals 101] UpdateOp --> VerifyUpdate[SELECT WHERE roll_no equals 101] VerifyUpdate --> DeleteOp[DELETE WHERE roll_no equals 103] DeleteOp --> VerifyDelete[SELECT FROM student] VerifyDelete --> End([Lab Complete]) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cassandra CRUD Operation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Client[Student Client] -->|CQL via 9042| Node1[Cassandra Node 1] Node1 --> CommitLog[Commit Log Disk] Node1 --> MemTable[In-Memory MemTable] MemTable -->|Flush| SSTable[SSTable on Disk] SSTable --> Compaction[Background Compaction] Compaction -->|Removes tombstones| CleanedData[Cleaned SSTable] Node1 -->|Replication| Node2[Cassandra Node 2] Node1 -->|Replication| Node3[Cassandra Node 3] Node2 --> ReadResponse[Read Response to Client] Node3 --> ReadResponse ReadResponse --> Client ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Schema Diagram — Table Structure ```mermaid flowchart LR TableBox["Table: student"] --> PK["PRIMARY KEY: roll_no INT"] TableBox --> Col1["name TEXT"] TableBox --> Col2["department TEXT"] TableBox --> Col3["cgpa FLOAT"] PK --> Hash["Hash Function
Murmur3"] Hash --> NodeA[Node A] Hash --> NodeB[Node B] Hash --> NodeC[Node C] NodeA --> Row1[Row 101: Arjun] NodeB --> Row2[Row 102: Meera] NodeC --> Row3[Row 103: Rahul] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — UPDATE vs DELETE Internals ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> ActiveRow ActiveRow --> OldVersion: Initial Write OldVersion --> NewVersion: UPDATE SET col = val NewVersion --> Tombstoned: DELETE WHERE pk = val Tombstoned --> CompactionPhase: gc_grace_seconds expires CompactionPhase --> PhysicallyRemoved: Purged from SSTable PhysicallyRemoved --> [*] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Diagram 4.4:** This state machine illustrates a critical KTU concept — **UPDATE and DELETE in Cassandra are both write operations**. They produce tombstones, not in-place changes. The actual data is removed only when the **gc_grace_seconds** window (default 10 days) elapses and compaction runs. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is a Keyspace in Cassandra? How is it different from a database in MySQL?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Keyspace** in Cassandra is the top-level container that defines a **replication strategy** and a **replication factor** for a set of tables. It is analogous to a database in MySQL but with a critical difference: a keyspace **mandatorily specifies how data is replicated across nodes** at the time of creation. ```cql CREATE KEYSPACE student_db WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 3}; ``` In MySQL, a database is just a logical namespace with no replication rules — those are handled by external cluster configuration. In Cassandra, replication is part of the **DDL definition itself**. A keyspace in Cassandra also defines a **data center strategy** (`NetworkTopologyStrategy`) for geographically distributed deployments. **Allocation:** Definition 1 mark + CQL example 1 mark + MySQL contrast 1 mark. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Why must every CREATE TABLE statement in Cassandra include a PRIMARY KEY? What happens if it is omitted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **PRIMARY KEY** in Cassandra serves **two critical functions**: 1. **Partition Key Identification** — It defines which column(s) determine the physical node on which a row is stored. Cassandra uses a hash function (Murmur3) on the partition key to distribute data. 2. **Uniqueness Within Partition** — It ensures each row can be uniquely addressed via `WHERE pk = value` lookups. If a PRIMARY KEY is **omitted**, Cassandra throws the error: ``` InvalidRequest: No PRIMARY KEY specified ``` The CREATE TABLE statement **fails entirely** — the table is not created. This is fundamentally different from MySQL, where a table can be created without a primary key (though not recommended). Cassandra's distributed architecture **requires** every row to be addressable via its key. **Allocation:** Function 1 mark 1 + Hash distribution 1 mark + Error behavior 1 mark. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) #### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]** **Write a complete CQL script to perform all four CRUD operations on a `product` table with columns: `pid` (int, primary key), `pname` (text), `price` (float), and `category` (text). Demonstrate all operations with at least 3 records.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** ```cql -- CREATE Keyspace CREATE KEYSPACE inventory WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 1}; USE inventory; -- CREATE Table CREATE TABLE product ( pid int, pname text, price float, category text, PRIMARY KEY (pid) ); -- CREATE Operation: Insert 3 records INSERT INTO product (pid, pname, price, category) VALUES (1, 'Laptop', 75000.0, 'Electronics'); INSERT INTO product (pid, pname, price, category) VALUES (2, 'Office Chair', 8500.5, 'Furniture'); INSERT INTO product (pid, pname, price, category) VALUES (3, 'Notebook', 45.0, 'Stationery'); -- READ Operation: Display all records SELECT * FROM product; -- UPDATE Operation: Increase price of pid=1 UPDATE product SET price = 78000.0 WHERE pid = 1; -- DELETE Operation: Remove pid=3 DELETE FROM product WHERE pid = 3; -- Final READ to verify SELECT * FROM product; ``` **Verification of Output:** ``` pid | pname | price | category -----+---------------+---------+------------ 1 | Laptop | 78000 | Electronics 2 | Office Chair | 8500.5 | Furniture ``` **Mark Allocation:** Keyspace+Table 2 marks, INSERTs 2 marks, SELECT+UPDATE+DELETE 3 marks. --- **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Explain the difference between an SQL UPDATE and a Cassandra UPDATE. Why is Cassandra's update considered a "write operation" internally? Illustrate with a diagram.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** | Aspect | SQL UPDATE | Cassandra UPDATE | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | In-place modification of the row on disk | Creates a new row version, marks old as tombstone | | **I/O Pattern** | Read-Modify-Write cycle | Pure Write (append to memtable) | | **Performance** | Slower for high-frequency updates | Faster — single write path | | **Disk I/O** | Reuses the same physical location | New SSTable segment created | **Why it is a Write Operation:** In Cassandra, the storage engine is **log-structured merge (LSM tree)** based. When `UPDATE table SET col = newvalue WHERE pk = X` is executed: 1. Cassandra looks up the partition hash for `pk = X` 2. It writes a **new row** to the commit log (durability) and memtable (in-memory) 3. The previous version of the row becomes a **tombstone** when it is overwritten 4. The memtable is periodically flushed to a new SSTable on disk 5. The old SSTable containing the old version is **not modified** — it gets cleaned up only during compaction ```mermaid flowchart LR SQL["SQL: UPDATE"] --> Read["Read Old Row"] Read --> Modify["In-Place Modify"] Modify --> WriteBack["Write Back"] WriteBack --> SQLDone([Updated]) Cas["Cassandra: UPDATE"] --> Hash["Hash Partition Key"] Hash --> NewWrite["Write New Version"] NewWrite --> Tombstone["Old Version becomes Tombstone"] Tombstone --> CasDone([Updated - Same I/O Cost as Insert]) ``` **Mark Allocation:** Comparison table 3 marks, LSM explanation 3 marks, Diagram 1 mark. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **Consider an Employee table with columns `emp_id` (int), `emp_name` (text), `salary` (float), and `dept` (text). Write the CQL commands to: (i) create a keyspace `org_db` with replication factor 1, (ii) create the table with `emp_id` as primary key, (iii) insert 4 records, (iv) update salary of emp_id 102, (v) delete record of emp_id 104.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** ```cql -- (i) Create Keyspace CREATE KEYSPACE org_db WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 1}; USE org_db; -- (ii) Create Table CREATE TABLE employee ( emp_id int, emp_name text, salary float, dept text, PRIMARY KEY (emp_id) ); -- (iii) Insert 4 records INSERT INTO employee (emp_id, emp_name, salary, dept) VALUES (101, 'Anand Kumar', 55000.0, 'CSE'); INSERT INTO employee (emp_id, emp_name, salary, dept) VALUES (102, 'Priya Sharma', 62000.0, 'ECE'); INSERT INTO employee (emp_id, emp_name, salary, dept) VALUES (103, 'Vivek Nair', 48000.0, 'MECH'); INSERT INTO employee (emp_id, emp_name, salary, dept) VALUES (104, 'Sara Joseph', 71000.0, 'CSE'); -- (iv) Update salary UPDATE employee SET salary = 65000.0 WHERE emp_id = 102; -- (v) Delete record DELETE FROM employee WHERE emp_id = 104; ``` **Verification Query:** ```cql SELECT * FROM employee; ``` **Expected Output:** ``` emp_id | dept | emp_name | salary --------+------+---------------+-------- 101 | CSE | Anand Kumar | 55000 102 | ECE | Priya Sharma | 65000 103 | MECH | Vivek Nair | 48000 ``` (emp_id 104 Sara Joseph is deleted.) **Mark Allocation:** (i) 1 mark, (ii) 1.5 marks, (iii) 2 marks, (iv) 1.5 marks, (v) 1 mark. --- **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected]** **Explain the concept of Tombstones in Cassandra. How are they created? What is `gc_grace_seconds` and why is it important? What happens if a node is down for longer than `gc_grace_seconds`?** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** A **Tombstone** in Cassandra is a **logical marker** that indicates a row or column has been deleted. It is **not** a physical deletion. Tombstones are essential for maintaining consistency in a distributed environment. **How Tombstones Are Created:** 1. When a `DELETE` statement is executed, Cassandra does not remove the data immediately. 2. Instead, it writes a **tombstone record** with a timestamp and a marker indicating the deletion. 3. The tombstone is replicated to all nodes that hold a copy of that partition. 4. When `READ` queries encounter a tombstone, they treat the row as deleted. **`gc_grace_seconds` (Garbage Collection Grace Seconds):** - It is the **time period (default: 10 days / 864000 seconds)** during which tombstones are retained in SSTables. - During this window, compaction can reconcile the tombstone with the deleted row and physically remove it. - This parameter is critical because it allows time for **anti-entropy repair** to propagate tombstones to nodes that may have been offline. **What Happens if a Node is Down Longer than `gc_grace_seconds`:** ```mermaid flowchart LR NodeA[Node A Online] -->|DELETE issued| TombA[Tombstone created at A] NodeA -.->|Replication fails| NodeB[Node B Offline] TombA -->|Time passes| ExceedsGC[Exceeds gc_grace_seconds] ExceedsGC --> Compaction[Compaction runs on A] Compaction --> PurgedA[Tombstone physically removed from A] PurgedA --> NodeBReturns[Node B comes back online] NodeBReturns --> ResurrectionProblem["STALE DATA RESURRECTION! Node B has no tombstone"] ResurrectionProblem --> Repair[Manual repair required] ``` **The Resurrected Zombie Problem:** If `gc_grace_seconds` expires and the tombstone is purged from Node A, but Node B was offline and missed the DELETE, when Node B comes back online, it still has the **old (deleted) row**. Since there is no tombstone, the row is treated as **live** and "resurrects" — this is a serious data integrity issue. **Mitigation:** Run `nodetool repair` regularly on all nodes within `gc_grace_seconds` intervals. **Mark Allocation:** Tombstone definition 1.5 marks, creation process 1.5 marks, gc_grace_seconds 1.5 marks, Zombie resurrection 2.5 marks. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Frequent Mark Deduction Traps in KTU Lab Evaluation:** > > 1. **Forgetting `USE keyspace_name;` before CREATE TABLE** — causes "Keyspace does not exist" error. **(Lose 1 mark)** > 2. **Missing semicolons `;`** — CQL does not always auto-terminate. Statements fail silently. **(Lose 0.5 mark)** > 3. **Omitting PRIMARY KEY in CREATE TABLE** — table creation fails completely. **(Lose 1.5 marks)** > 4. **Using `WHERE` on non-primary-key columns without secondary index** — query fails. **(Lose 1 mark)** > 5. **UPDATE/DELETE without full PRIMARY KEY in WHERE clause** — error: "PRIMARY KEY column required". **(Lose 1.5 marks)** > 6. **Confusing partition key with clustering key** — students often write `PRIMARY KEY (roll_no, name)` thinking name is just a column. **(Lose 2 marks — concept error)** > 7. **Not including output screenshots in the lab record** — examiners deduct marks for missing verification. **(Lose 1 mark)** > 8. **Using `DROP KEYSPACE` without backing up** — in case of re-execution, data is lost. **(Lose 0.5 mark)** > 9. **Assuming insertion order is preserved** — Cassandra sorts by partition hash, not insertion time. **(Lose 0.5 mark)** > 10. **Forgetting to start Cassandra server before running cqlsh** — connection refused error. **(Lose 1 mark)** ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):** > > - **Cassandra** is a **distributed, decentralized, NoSQL wide-column store** designed for high availability and linear scalability. > - **CRUD** = **C**reate, **R**ead, **U**pdate, **D**elete — the four fundamental persistent storage operations. > - **cqlsh** is the interactive shell to execute CQL commands. Default port: **9042**. > - **Keyspace** = top-level container (analogous to a database) **with mandatory replication strategy**. > - **Replication strategies:** `SimpleStrategy` (single DC, used in labs) and `NetworkTopologyStrategy` (production multi-DC). > - **Replication factor** = number of copies of data stored across nodes. > - **Table** = a column family defined with mandatory **PRIMARY KEY**. > - **PRIMARY KEY = Partition Key + Clustering Key** (clustering key is optional). > - **INSERT** syntax: `INSERT INTO table (cols) VALUES (vals);` — must include **all PRIMARY KEY columns**. > - **SELECT** syntax: `SELECT cols FROM table WHERE pk = val;` — filter only on PRIMARY KEY (or indexed columns). > - **UPDATE** in Cassandra is technically a **write operation** that creates a new version and tombstones the old. > - **DELETE** creates a **tombstone** (logical marker) — physical deletion happens during **compaction**. > - **Tombstones** are retained for `gc_grace_seconds` (default 10 days) for repair safety. > - **Default port:** **9042** for CQL native protocol. > - **Common data types:** `int`, `text`, `float`, `double`, `boolean`, `uuid`, `timestamp`, `date`, `bigint`. > - **TTL** (Time To Live) feature allows automatic data expiration. > - **CQL is case-insensitive** for keywords but **case-sensitive** for string values. > - **Always show output verification** in lab records — KTU examiners value proof of execution. > - **Cassandra sorts output by partition hash**, not by insertion order — do not be surprised by reordered results. > - **Use `DESCRIBE TABLE name;`** to view table schema — useful for debugging in viva. > - **Lab exam tip:** Start Cassandra first, then cqlsh, then create keyspace, then create table, then perform CRUD in order.

topics.title: Write and execute CQL queries to retrieve specific data from Cassandra tables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write and execute CQL queries to retrieve specific data from Cassandra tables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cassandra Query Language (CQL)** is the declarative query language for Apache Cassandra, syntactically similar to SQL but architecturally constrained to support Cassandra's distributed, peer-to-peer, eventually-consistent data model. The retrieval operation in CQL is performed using the `SELECT` statement, which fetches specific columns and rows from a Cassandra table (column family) based on equality and range predicates applied to **partition keys** and **clustering columns**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL408 - Module 2):**
> Students must be able to *write, execute, and interpret* CQL queries involving `SELECT`, `WHERE`, `ORDER BY`, `LIMIT`, and `ALLOW FILTERING` clauses against a populated Cassandra keyspace. The emphasis is on understanding *why* certain queries are rejected by Cassandra and how to redesign them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Distributed Library Analogy**
>
> Imagine a massive library where books are **not stored on one giant shelf** but are spread across **10,000 smaller libraries** (nodes) in different cities. Each small library holds books that share the *same category and author* (the **partition key**). Within each small library, books are arranged **alphabetically by title** (the **clustering column**).
>
> When you want a book:
> - If you know the **category + author** (partition key) → the librarian instantly knows *which small library* has it. **Lightning fast.**
> - If you only know the **title** but not the author → the librarian must check *all 10,000 libraries*. This is a **full table scan**, which Cassandra refuses by default (anti-pattern).
> - If you want all books by an author, sorted by title → the librarian returns a *naturally ordered slice* — this is the **`ORDER BY`** operation.
>
> **CQL retrieval is the act of telling Cassandra: "Give me the slice of data that lives on the same partition, ordered by its natural clustering."**

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Keyspace** | The top-level container (analogous to a *database* in RDBMS) that defines replication strategy. |
| **Table** (Column Family) | A set of rows sharing the same partition key structure. |
| **Partition Key** | The column(s) that determine *on which node* the row is stored. |
| **Clustering Column** | The column(s) that determine the *sort order of rows* within a partition. |
| **CQLSH** | The interactive command-line shell for executing CQL queries. |
| **ALLOW FILTERING** | A clause that permits queries on non-indexed, non-key columns (used as a *last resort*). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Fact (Memorize):** In Cassandra, every retrieval operation is fundamentally a **partition read**, optionally refined by clustering-column range predicates. This single fact explains 90% of all CQL retrieval rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a CQL `SELECT` Statement

A CQL retrieval query is a *layered construction*. Each layer adds a constraint or transformation to the result set. Understanding the layered model is the key to writing valid CQL.

### Layer 1 — The Mandatory Skeleton
```sql
SELECT  <column_list | *> 
FROM    <keyspace_name>.<table_name>;
```
- `*` returns all columns.
- Specifying a column list is **strongly preferred** in distributed systems (reduces network I/O).

### Layer 2 — Filtering with `WHERE`
```sql
WHERE  partition_key = value
  AND  clustering_col  = value   -- equality
  AND  clustering_col  >  value  -- range (only on clustering)
```
**Hard Rule:** `WHERE` predicates **must** include the partition key, otherwise Cassandra demands `ALLOW FILTERING`.

### Layer 3 — Ordering with `ORDER BY`
```sql
ORDER BY clustering_col ASC | DESC;
```
- The column in `ORDER BY` **must be a clustering column**.
- Sorting across partitions is **not allowed** (you can only sort *within* a partition).

### Layer 4 — Pagination with `LIMIT` and `PER PARTITION LIMIT`
```sql
LIMIT 10;                              -- global row cap
PER PARTITION LIMIT 5;                 -- cap per partition
```

### Layer 5 — The Escape Hatch (`ALLOW FILTERING`)
```sql
WHERE non_key_column = 'value' ALLOW FILTERING;
```
- Permits queries on **non-indexed, non-key columns** by performing a cluster-wide scan.
- **Performance penalty** — used only when no better alternative exists.

## 2.2 The "KTU High-Yield" CQL Retrieval Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for your lab exam. Memorize the *rule* column.

| # | Clause | Permitted Targets | Forbidden Targets | KTU Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `=` on Partition Key | ✅ Allowed | — | Equality is the **fastest** retrieval. |
| 2 | `=` on Clustering Column | ✅ Allowed | — | Refines row within a partition. |
| 3 | `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `BETWEEN` | ✅ Only on **clustering columns** | ❌ Never on partition key or regular columns | Range scans work *inside* a partition only. |
| 4 | `IN` | ✅ On last partition-key column OR clustering column | ❌ On the first partition-key column | `IN` is supported but limited. |
| 5 | `ORDER BY` | ✅ On clustering column | ❌ On partition key or non-key column | Sorting is per-partition. |
| 6 | `ALLOW FILTERING` | ✅ Anywhere | Performance killer | Reserved for ad-hoc analytics. |
| 7 | `LIMIT n` | ✅ Always | — | Returns first `n` rows. |
| 8 | `PER PARTITION LIMIT n` | ✅ Always | — | Returns first `n` per partition. |
| 9 | `DISTINCT` | ❌ Not supported in CQL | — | Cassandra discourages it. |
| 10 | `JOIN` | ❌ Not supported | — | Use **denormalization** instead. |

## 2.3 Why These Rules Exist — The "Why" Behind CQL

- **Distributed reads are partition-bound.** Once Cassandra knows the partition key, it can compute the *exact node* holding the data via consistent hashing. Any query that *cannot* be resolved to a partition forces a scatter-gather operation across the entire cluster.
- **No JOINs / no subqueries** because Cassandra has no concept of referential integrity — relationships are *baked into the data model* through denormalization.
- **`ALLOW FILTERING` is the equivalent of a "full table scan" in SQL.** It is functionally correct but operationally dangerous in production.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cassandra-style retrieval is the backbone of:
- **Netflix** — viewing history per user (partition: user_id, cluster: timestamp).
- **Instagram** — photo timeline per user.
- **IoT telemetry** — sensor readings per device, ordered by time.
- **Messaging apps** — chat history per conversation.

In all these systems, the access pattern is: *"Give me all data for **this one entity**, sorted by time."* — which maps directly to a partition key + clustering column lookup.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Schema (Used Throughout)

For the KTU lab exam, you will typically be given (or must create) a schema such as the one below. We will build retrievals **incrementally** on top of this schema.

```sql
-- Step 1: Create a keyspace
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS university
WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': 1};

USE university;

-- Step 2: Create the table
CREATE TABLE student_marks (
    student_id   INT,           -- PARTITION KEY
    semester     INT,           -- CLUSTERING COLUMN 1
    subject      TEXT,          -- CLUSTERING COLUMN 2
    marks        INT,
    grade        TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, semester, subject)
);

-- Step 3: Insert sample data
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (101, 4, 'DBMS', 88, 'A');
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (101, 4, 'OS',  75, 'B');
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (101, 5, 'CN',  82, 'A');
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (102, 4, 'DBMS', 65, 'C');
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (102, 5, 'OS',  91, 'A');
INSERT INTO student_marks (student_id, semester, subject, marks, grade)
VALUES (103, 4, 'DBMS', 78, 'B');
```

### The Derived Storage Model

On disk, Cassandra stores the data as follows (conceptual view):

| Partition Key `student_id` | Clustering `(semester, subject)` | `marks` | `grade` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | (4, DBMS) | 88 | A |
| 101 | (4, OS)   | 75 | B |
| 101 | (5, CN)   | 82 | A |
| 102 | (4, DBMS) | 65 | C |
| 102 | (5, OS)   | 91 | A |
| 103 | (4, DBMS) | 78 | B |

**Derivation Step 1 — Identify the partition:** Every row with the *same* `student_id` lives on the *same* node and forms one partition.

**Derivation Step 2 — Identify the clustering:** Within a partition, rows are sorted by `(semester, subject)` in **ascending** order by default.

**Derivation Step 3 — Identify the query target:** A valid `SELECT` must always be *resolvable* to one or more specific partitions. If it cannot, the query must declare `ALLOW FILTERING`.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Query Derivations

### Query 1 — Retrieve All Marks for a Single Student

**Textual requirement:** *"Show every mark obtained by student 101."*

**Step-by-step construction:**

$$\text{Step 1: Identify the partition} \Rightarrow \text{student\_id} = 101$$

$$\text{Step 2: No clustering restriction} \Rightarrow \text{omit WHERE on semester/subject}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Final CQL} \Rightarrow \text{SELECT} \ * \ \text{FROM} \ \text{student\_marks} \ \text{WHERE} \ \text{student\_id} = 101;$$

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks WHERE student_id = 101;
```

**Output:**
```
 student_id | semester | subject | marks | grade
------------+----------+---------+-------+-------
        101 |        4 |    CN   |    82 |     A
        101 |        4 |   DBMS  |    88 |     A
        101 |        5 |    OS   |    75 |     B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Cassandra reads **only the partition for student 101** — a single-node operation. No `ALLOW FILTERING` is needed.

---

### Query 2 — Retrieve Marks for a Student in a Specific Semester

**Textual requirement:** *"Show all subjects and marks of student 101 in semester 4."*

$$\text{Step 1: Partition} \Rightarrow \text{student\_id} = 101$$

$$\text{Step 2: Clustering equality} \Rightarrow \text{semester} = 4$$

$$\text{Step 3: Final CQL}$$

```sql
SELECT subject, marks, grade
FROM student_marks
WHERE student_id = 101 AND semester = 4;
```

**Output:**
```
 subject | marks | grade
---------+-------+-------
    CN   |    82 |     A
   DBMS  |    88 |     A
```

---

### Query 3 — Range Scan on a Clustering Column

**Textual requirement:** *"Show all marks of student 101 in semesters greater than or equal to 4."*

$$\text{Step 1: Partition} \Rightarrow \text{student\_id} = 101$$

$$\text{Step 2: Range on clustering} \Rightarrow \text{semester} \geq 4$$

$$\text{Step 3: Final CQL}$$

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
WHERE student_id = 101 AND semester >= 4;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Range predicates are **only** permitted on **clustering columns** when the **partition key** is fully specified. This query is valid because `student_id` is pinned.

---

### Query 4 — Sorting Within a Partition

**Textual requirement:** *"Show student 101's marks, sorted by marks in descending order."*

**Attempt 1 (Naïve — will FAIL):**
```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
WHERE student_id = 101
ORDER BY marks DESC;   -- ❌ marks is NOT a clustering column
```

**Attempt 2 (Also FAILS):**
```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
ORDER BY marks DESC;   -- ❌ no partition key in WHERE
```

**Correct Approach (Denormalization):**
Cassandra **cannot sort on a non-clustering column**. The lab answer is to create a *materialized view* or a *second table* where `marks` is part of the clustering key.

```sql
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW student_marks_by_score AS
    SELECT * FROM student_marks
    WHERE student_id IS NOT NULL
      AND semester IS NOT NULL
      AND subject  IS NOT NULL
      AND marks    IS NOT NULL
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, marks, semester, subject);
```

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks_by_score
WHERE student_id = 101
ORDER BY marks DESC;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** This is one of the **most commonly tested viva questions** — *"Can you sort by a non-clustering column?"* The answer is *no, not directly; you must redesign the schema.*

---

### Query 5 — Using `ALLOW FILTERING` (Last Resort)

**Textual requirement:** *"Find all students who scored exactly 88 marks."*

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
WHERE marks = 88;            -- ❌ will throw InvalidRequest
```

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
WHERE marks = 88
ALLOW FILTERING;            -- ✅ executes, but scans all partitions
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Performance Impact:** `ALLOW FILTERING` triggers a **full cluster scan**. Acceptable in a lab demo; **never** acceptable in production without an index.

---

### Query 6 — Using an Index on a Non-Key Column

To avoid `ALLOW FILTERING`, create a **secondary index**:

```sql
CREATE INDEX ON student_marks (marks);

SELECT * FROM student_marks WHERE marks = 88;
-- Now works WITHOUT ALLOW FILTERING
```

---

### Query 7 — `IN` Clause on a Partition Key

**Textual requirement:** *"Show all marks for students 101 and 102."*

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
WHERE student_id IN (101, 102);
```

> [!NOTE]
> `IN` is permitted only on the **last** column of the partition key. The above is valid because `student_id` is the *only* partition-key column.

---

### Query 8 — `PER PARTITION LIMIT` (Advanced Pagination)

**Textual requirement:** *"For each student, show the top 2 subjects by semester."*

```sql
SELECT * FROM student_marks
PER PARTITION LIMIT 2;
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Query Validity Quick Reference

| Query Intent | Partition Key in WHERE? | Clustering Predicate? | Valid CQL? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| All data of one student | ✅ | None | ✅ | Fastest read. |
| One student, one semester | ✅ | `=` | ✅ | Single partition, single slice. |
| One student, semester range | ✅ | `>`, `<` | ✅ | Range scan inside partition. |
| All students, sorted by marks | ❌ | N/A | ❌ | Requires materialized view. |
| All students with marks = 88 | ❌ | N/A | ❌ unless `ALLOW FILTERING` or index | Use secondary index. |
| All students, no filter | ❌ | N/A | ❌ | Refused by CQLSH. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CQL Query Parsing & Execution Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the CQLSH parser processes a `SELECT` statement and routes it to the correct execution path.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User types SELECT statement in CQLSH] --> B[Parser tokenizes the CQL]
    B --> C{Is the query syntactically valid?}
    C -- No --> D[Parser throws SyntaxError]
    C -- Yes --> E[Query Planner analyzes WHERE clause]
    E --> F{Is the PARTITION KEY present in WHERE?}
    F -- Yes --> G{Is there a CLUSTERING column predicate?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Single-partition read: getIndexedSlices]
    G -- No  --> I[Single-partition full scan: RangeSlice]
    F -- No --> J{Is ALLOW FILTERING declared?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Cluster-wide scan: RangeSlice over ALL nodes]
    J -- No  --> L[InvalidRequest error: 'Cannot execute query']
    H --> M[Coordinator node merges result]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Rows returned to CQLSH client]
    L --> O[Error message displayed]
    D --> O
```

## 4.2 Logical Storage & Retrieval Architecture

This diagram shows the *internal mapping* from a CQL query to a partition on a specific node in the Cassandra ring.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ClientLayer["Client Layer"]
        CQLSH["CQLSH Client"]
    end

    subgraph RingLayer["Cassandra Ring (Coordinator Node)"]
        COORD["Query Coordinator"]
        HASH["Consistent Hash Function: hash partition_key"]
    end

    subgraph NodeLayer["Storage Nodes"]
        N1["Node A: partitions for keys 0-99"]
        N2["Node B: partitions for keys 100-199"]
        N3["Node C: partitions for keys 200-299"]
    end

    CQLSH --> COORD
    COORD --> HASH
    HASH --> N2
    N2 --> RESULT["SSTable + Memtable Lookup"]
    RESULT --> COORD
    COORD --> CQLSH
```

## 4.3 Partition Slice Visualization (for `student_marks`)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P101["Partition: student_id = 101"] --> R1["(4, CN, 82, A)"]
    P101 --> R2["(4, DBMS, 88, A)"]
    P101 --> R3["(5, OS, 75, B)"]
    P102["Partition: student_id = 102"] --> R4["(4, DBMS, 65, C)"]
    P102 --> R5["(5, OS, 91, A)"]
    P103["Partition: student_id = 103"] --> R6["(4, DBMS, 78, B)"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each **partition** (P101, P102, P103) is a self-contained sorted list.
- A query `WHERE student_id = 101` returns *only* the rows inside P101.
- A query `WHERE student_id = 101 AND semester = 4` returns *only* R1 and R2.
- A query `WHERE student_id IN (101, 102)` returns rows from P101 **and** P102 — this is *not* a cross-partition JOIN, it is a *union of two partition reads*.

## 4.4 CQL Clause Dependency Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SELECT columns] --> B[FROM table]
    B --> C{WHERE clause?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Partition key predicate]
    D --> E[Clustering column predicate]
    E --> F{ORDER BY?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Clustering column ASC/DESC]
    G --> H{LIMIT?}
    H -- Yes --> I[LIMIT n or PER PARTITION LIMIT n]
    I --> J[Execute]
    C -- No --> J
    F -- No --> J
    H -- No --> J
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Why does Cassandra reject the following query? Rewrite it in a valid form.**
`SELECT * FROM student_marks WHERE marks > 80;`"

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Cassandra rejects this query because **`marks` is neither a partition key nor a clustering column**. Range predicates are only permitted on clustering columns, and the **partition key (`student_id`) is missing** from the `WHERE` clause.
>
> **Valid rewrite using ALLOW FILTERING (acceptable in lab):**
> ```sql
> SELECT * FROM student_marks WHERE marks > 80 ALLOW FILTERING;
> ```
>
> **Better production-grade rewrite** — create a secondary index:
> ```sql
> CREATE INDEX idx_marks ON student_marks (marks);
> SELECT * FROM student_marks WHERE marks > 80;
> ```
> *(1 mark for identifying the cause, 1 mark for one valid rewrite, 1 mark for the index-based alternative.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Differentiate between a partition key and a clustering column in Cassandra with an example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Partition Key | Clustering Column |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Determines *which node* stores the row. | Determines the *order of rows* within a partition. |
| Cardinality | Should be **high** (distributed evenly). | Can be low or high. |
| Query role | Must be present in `WHERE` for efficient reads. | Supports `=`, range, `IN`, and `ORDER BY`. |
| Example | `student_id` in `student_marks`. | `semester, subject` in `student_marks`. |

*(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the role in queries.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** (theory) and **Part (b) = 7 marks** (practical/lab). Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, DBMS Lab]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Consider a Cassandra table `product_sales(product_id, sale_date, region, quantity, revenue)` with `PRIMARY KEY (product_id, sale_date, region)`. Write CQL queries to:*
- *(i) Retrieve all sales of product `P101`.*
- *(ii) Retrieve sales of `P101` after 1st January 2024.*
- *(iii) Retrieve sales of `P101` between two dates, sorted by revenue descending.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain why the following query is invalid and provide two valid alternatives:*
`SELECT * FROM product_sales ORDER BY revenue DESC;`

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solutions:**

**(i) All sales of product P101:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM product_sales WHERE product_id = 'P101';
```
**Explanation:** `product_id` is the partition key. The query targets one partition, no clustering restriction, so all rows within that partition are returned in their natural sort order of `(sale_date, region)`.

**(ii) Sales of P101 after 1st January 2024:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM product_sales
WHERE product_id = 'P101'
  AND sale_date > '2024-01-01';
```
**Explanation:** `product_id` pins the partition; `sale_date` is the first clustering column and supports a range predicate. This is a **single-partition range scan**.

**(iii) Sales of P101 between two dates, sorted by revenue DESC:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM product_sales
WHERE product_id = 'P101'
  AND sale_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'
ORDER BY sale_date DESC;
```
**Explanation:** Sorting is applied **only** to the clustering column `sale_date`. Sorting by `revenue` is *not* allowed directly — refer to part (b) for the proper workaround.

*(Awarding: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Why the query is invalid:**
1. The `WHERE` clause is **missing the partition key** (`product_id`). Cassandra cannot determine which partition(s) to read.
2. `revenue` is **not a clustering column**. Cassandra's `ORDER BY` clause may only sort on clustering columns of the queried table.
3. Even if it were syntactically valid, sorting across partitions is **architecturally unsupported** in Cassandra.

**Two Valid Alternatives:**

**Alternative 1 — Materialized View (recommended for read-heavy patterns):**
```sql
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW product_sales_by_revenue AS
    SELECT * FROM product_sales
    WHERE product_id IS NOT NULL
      AND sale_date  IS NOT NULL
      AND region     IS NOT NULL
    PRIMARY KEY (product_id, revenue, sale_date, region)
    WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (revenue DESC);

SELECT * FROM product_sales_by_revenue WHERE product_id = 'P101';
```

**Alternative 2 — Application-level denormalization:**
Create a second table with `revenue` as the leading clustering column, and have the application write to both tables.

*(Awarding: 3 marks for invalidity reasoning, 2 marks each for the two alternatives.)*

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, DBMS Lab]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a suitable example, explain the CQL `SELECT` statement and the role of `ALLOW FILTERING`. When should it be used?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Given a Cassandra table `library(book_id, member_id, borrow_date, return_date, status)` with `PRIMARY KEY (book_id, borrow_date, member_id)`, write CQL queries to:*
- *(i) Find all borrowing history of book `B500`.*
- *(ii) Find active (unreturned) books borrowed by member `M12`.*
- *(iii) List the 5 most recent borrows of book `B500`.*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

The CQL `SELECT` statement is the **read operation** of Cassandra Query Language. Its full syntax is:

```sql
SELECT [DISTINCT] select_list
FROM [keyspace_name.]table_name
[WHERE clause]
[ORDER BY clustering_col [ASC | DESC]]
[PER PARTITION LIMIT n]
[LIMIT n]
[ALLOW FILTERING];
```

**Role of `ALLOW FILTERING`:**
- Cassandra's storage model requires that every read be *resolvable* to specific partitions.
- When a query references **non-key, non-indexed columns** (e.g., `WHERE status = 'active'`), the coordinator cannot know which partitions hold the matching rows.
- `ALLOW FILTERING` overrides this safety check and instructs Cassandra to **scan every partition** on every node, then filter in memory.
- **When to use it:**
  - ✅ During **ad-hoc analytics** or **lab experiments**.
  - ✅ On small dev clusters.
  - ❌ **Never** in production hot paths — replace with a secondary index or denormalized table.

**Example:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM library WHERE status = 'overdue' ALLOW FILTERING;
```

*(Awarding: 2 marks for syntax, 3 marks for role of ALLOW FILTERING, 2 marks for usage guidance.)*

---

**Part (b) Solutions:**

**(i) Borrowing history of book B500:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM library WHERE book_id = 'B500';
```
**Explanation:** `book_id` is the partition key; the query reads one full partition.

**(ii) Active (unreturned) books borrowed by member M12:**

This query **cannot be answered efficiently** with the current schema, because `member_id` is a clustering column, not a partition key. The proper solution is to **add a second table** keyed by member:

```sql
CREATE TABLE library_by_member (
    member_id   TEXT,
    borrow_date TIMESTAMP,
    book_id     TEXT,
    status      TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY (member_id, borrow_date, book_id)
);

SELECT * FROM library_by_member
WHERE member_id = 'M12' AND status = 'active' ALLOW FILTERING;
```

**Explanation:** This demonstrates the **denormalization pattern** that is the standard CQL answer to "JOIN-like" requirements.

**(iii) 5 most recent borrows of book B500:**
```sql
SELECT * FROM library
WHERE book_id = 'B500'
ORDER BY borrow_date DESC
LIMIT 5;
```
**Explanation:** `book_id` pins the partition; `ORDER BY` uses the clustering column `borrow_date`; `LIMIT 5` caps the result.

*(Awarding: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) including denormalization, 2 marks for (iii).)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `ALLOW FILTERING`:** When asked to query by a non-key column, students often write only the `WHERE` clause and omit `ALLOW FILTERING`. CQLSH will throw `InvalidRequest: Cannot execute this query as it might involve data filtering`. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Sorting on non-clustering columns:** A query like `ORDER BY marks DESC` (when `marks` is not in the primary key) is **rejected by Cassandra**. The correct answer involves either a **materialized view** or a **denormalized table**. Examiners *expect* you to know this.
> 3. **Confusing `LIMIT` with `PER PARTITION LIMIT`:** Global `LIMIT 5` returns 5 rows total; `PER PARTITION LIMIT 5` returns 5 rows **per partition**. This distinction is a frequent viva question.
> 4. **Using `JOIN` in CQL:** Cassandra does **not** support joins. Writing `... FROM table1 JOIN table2 ON ...` is a **fatal error** in CQL. Always denormalize.
> 5. **Missing semicolon:** CQL statements must end with `;`. The shell will keep waiting for the next line. *(1-mark deduction in lab records.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 The 10 Golden Rules of CQL Retrieval — Revise Before Every Lab Exam**
>
> 1. **`SELECT *` is legal but discouraged** — always project the columns you need.
> 2. **Partition key MUST appear in `WHERE`** — otherwise, the query is rejected (or needs `ALLOW FILTERING`).
> 3. **Equality (`=`) is the fastest retrieval** in Cassandra.
> 4. **Range queries (`>`, `<`, `BETWEEN`) work only on clustering columns**, only when the partition key is fixed.
> 5. **`ORDER BY` may only sort on clustering columns** — never on regular or partition-key columns.
> 6. **`IN` is allowed only on the last column of the partition key** OR on a clustering column.
> 7. **`ALLOW FILTERING` = full cluster scan** — use it for lab demos, not production.
> 8. **For non-key column queries, create a secondary index** (`CREATE INDEX ON table(col);`).
> 9. **For sorting on non-clustering columns, use a materialized view** or denormalized table.
> 10. **No JOINs, no subqueries, no `DISTINCT`** — Cassandra forces denormalization by design.
>
> **Bonus — Viva One-Liners:**
> - *"What is the difference between partition key and clustering column?"* → Partition key locates the *node*; clustering column locates the *row within the partition* and defines its sort order.
> - *"Why is `ALLOW FILTERING` slow?"* → It forces Cassandra to scan **every** SSTable on **every** node.
> - *"How do you paginate in Cassandra?"* → Use `LIMIT` with `OFFSET`-like stateful paging, or `PER PARTITION LIMIT` for per-partition paging.
> - *"Can you query across partitions in Cassandra?"* → Yes, using `IN` on the partition key (or `ALLOW FILTERING`), but **not** via `JOIN`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Create a simple application using MongoDB with Python
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL408 - DBMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Create a simple application using MongoDB with Python

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**MongoDB** is a document-oriented, NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database that stores data in flexible, JSON-like documents called **BSON (Binary JSON)** records. When integrated with **Python** through the official driver **PyMongo**, it enables developers to build scalable, schema-less backend applications where data is manipulated via Python objects (dictionaries and lists) rather than rigid SQL tables and rows.

In the context of **PCCSL408 – DBMS Lab**, Experiment 2 evaluates the student's ability to design, implement, and query a non-relational data store using the **CRUD paradigm** (Create, Read, Update, Delete) — demonstrating competency in mapping Python data structures onto a document database.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is a *hands-on lab*; the examination questions test the **connection string format**, **CRUD method signatures**, **BSON data types**, and **collection vs. document terminology** — *not* raw SQL.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of MongoDB as a **giant digital filing cabinet** where:
- The **Database** = the entire cabinet
- The **Collection** = a single drawer (e.g., "Students drawer")
- The **Document** = an individual file inside that drawer (a single student's record)
- The **Field** = an entry on the file (Name, Age, CGPA, …)

Unlike a relational SQL database — where every file in a drawer *must* follow the exact same paper template (the schema) — MongoDB allows each file in the drawer to have **whatever fields it wants**. One student's file may list "Hobbies", while another's may list "Internship" — the database doesn't care.

**Python acts as the librarian** who decides:
- Which file to create (`insert_one`)
- Which file to read (`find`)
- Which file to edit (`update_one`)
- Which file to discard (`delete_one`)

The librarian (PyMongo driver) translates the librarian's English instructions into MongoDB's native command language, called the **MongoDB Wire Protocol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Remember:**
> - SQL Database → **Table** → **Row** → **Column**
> - MongoDB → **Collection** → **Document** → **Field**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Defaults

| Item | Default / Standard |
|---|---|
| Default MongoDB Port | **27017** |
| Default PyMongo Driver | `pymongo` (official) |
| Document Storage Format | **BSON** (max 16 MB per document) |
| Unique Document Identifier | `_id` field (auto-generated **ObjectId**) |
| Default Database Path | `/data/db` |
| ObjectId Size | **12 bytes** (4-byte timestamp + 5-byte random + 3-byte counter) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MongoDB-Python Communication Stack

When a Python program talks to MongoDB, the data flows through **four logical layers**:

1. **Application Layer (Python Script)**
   - You write Python code using dictionaries and lists.
2. **Driver Layer (PyMongo)**
   - The official MongoDB driver for Python. It serializes Python objects into BSON, packs them into the MongoDB Wire Protocol, and sends them over a TCP socket (port **27017**).
3. **Server Layer (mongod)**
   - The MongoDB daemon process that listens for requests, executes queries, and returns BSON-encoded responses.
4. **Storage Layer (WiredTiger Engine)**
   - The default storage engine (since MongoDB 3.2) that handles on-disk persistence, compression, and concurrency via document-level locking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BSON over JSON?** BSON extends JSON with extra data types (int, long, float, ObjectId, Date, Binary) and is *binary-encoded* — making it faster to parse and richer in semantics. JSON only knows *strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, objects, and null*.

## 2.2 CRUD Operations — The Core Methods

CRUD is the **four-letter alphabet** of every database interaction. The KTU lab record expects you to implement all four.

| Operation | SQL Equivalent | PyMongo Method | Return Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C** – Create | `INSERT INTO` | `insert_one()`, `insert_many()` | `InsertOneResult` with `inserted_id` |
| **R** – Read | `SELECT` | `find_one()`, `find()` | Cursor / single document |
| **U** – Update | `UPDATE` | `update_one()`, `update_many()` | `UpdateResult` with `modified_count` |
| **D** – Delete | `DELETE` | `delete_one()`, `delete_many()` | `DeleteResult` with `deleted_count` |

## 2.3 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept | Syntax Pattern | Important Note |
|---|---|---|
| Import Driver | `from pymongo import MongoClient` | Always required first step |
| Create Connection | `client = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017/")` | NoSQL — no port typing in URI |
| Access Database | `db = client["dbname"]` or `client.dbname` | Lazy creation — DB exists on first write |
| Access Collection | `col = db["colname"]` or `db.colname` | Collection = Group of documents |
| Insert One | `col.insert_one({"k": "v"})` | Auto-generates `_id` if not provided |
| Insert Many | `col.insert_many([{...}, {...}])` | Pass a *list* of dicts |
| Find All | `for d in col.find(): ...` | Returns a **Cursor** (iterable) |
| Find Filtered | `col.find({"field": "value"})` | Filter is itself a document |
| Update One | `col.update_one({"k": "v"}, {"$set": {"k": "new"}})` | Requires `$set` operator |
| Delete One | `col.delete_one({"k": "v"})` | First matching document only |
| Count Docs | `col.count_documents({})` | Replaces deprecated `count()` |
| Close Connection | `client.close()` | Good practice — releases socket |

## 2.4 MongoDB Update Operators (High-Yield)

| Operator | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `$set` | Sets field value | `{"$set": {"age": 21}}` |
| `$inc` | Increments numeric field | `{"$inc": {"age": 1}}` |
| `$unset` | Removes a field | `{"$unset": {"age": ""}}` |
| `$push` | Appends to array | `{"$push": {"tags": "new"}}` |
| `$pull` | Removes from array | `{"$pull": {"tags": "old"}}` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** MongoDB with Python is the **de-facto stack** for modern web backends (used by Uber, eBay, Adobe, and Meta). It is the foundation of the **MEAN/MERN** web stack and is preferred for **unstructured/IoT data** because documents can absorb schema drift — when fields change shape over time, the database doesn't require an `ALTER TABLE` migration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setting Up the Environment

### Step 1 — Install the PyMongo Driver

The Python interpreter must be linked to the MongoDB server through a driver library. Open the terminal and execute:

```bash
pip install pymongo
```

PyMongo version **4.x+** is required for MongoDB 6.0/7.0 compatibility (the KTU 2024 lab standard).

### Step 2 — Start the MongoDB Server

```bash
mongod
```

The server starts listening on port **27017** by default. If successful, the terminal will display `Waiting for connections`.

## 3.2 Building the Application — Full CRUD Walkthrough

Below is a complete, compilable Python application demonstrating every CRUD operation on a `students` collection inside a `ktu_lab` database.

### Program: `mongo_app.py`

```python
# ---------- IMPORTS ----------
from pymongo import MongoClient
from bson.objectid import ObjectId

# ---------- STEP A: ESTABLISH CONNECTION ----------
# MongoClient opens a connection pool to the MongoDB server
client = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017/")

# ---------- STEP B: SELECT DATABASE ----------
# A database is created automatically when data is first written
db = client["ktu_lab"]

# ---------- STEP C: SELECT COLLECTION ----------
# A collection is the NoSQL equivalent of a table
students = db["students"]

# ---------- STEP 1: CREATE (Insert) ----------
print("--- CREATE ---")
student_1 = {
    "name": "Aravind",
    "roll_no": "KTU2024CS001",
    "cgpa": 8.7,
    "branch": "CSE",
    "skills": ["Python", "MongoDB", "React"]
}
student_2 = {
    "name": "Meera",
    "roll_no": "KTU2024CS002",
    "cgpa": 9.1,
    "branch": "CSE",
    "skills": ["Java", "Spring", "SQL"]
}
student_3 = {
    "name": "Rahul",
    "roll_no": "KTU2024EC015",
    "cgpa": 7.9,
    "branch": "ECE",
    "skills": ["C++", "Verilog"]
}

# insert_one returns an InsertOneResult
result_one = students.insert_one(student_1)
print(f"Inserted Aravind with _id: {result_one.inserted_id}")

# insert_many takes a Python list of dictionaries
result_many = students.insert_many([student_2, student_3])
print(f"Inserted IDs: {result_many.inserted_ids}")

# ---------- STEP 2: READ (Query) ----------
print("\n--- READ ---")

# find_one() returns a single document (a Python dict) or None
meera = students.find_one({"name": "Meera"})
print(f"Found Meera: {meera}")

# find() returns a Cursor — an iterable of documents
print("All students with cgpa > 8.0:")
for doc in students.find({"cgpa": {"$gt": 8.0}}):
    print(f"  {doc['name']} - CGPA: {doc['cgpa']}")

# Querying with multiple conditions
print("CSE students with cgpa >= 8.5:")
for doc in students.find({"branch": "CSE", "cgpa": {"$gte": 8.5}}):
    print(f"  {doc['name']} - {doc['branch']}")

# ---------- STEP 3: UPDATE ----------
print("\n--- UPDATE ---")

# $set operator updates only the specified fields
update_res = students.update_one(
    {"roll_no": "KTU2024CS001"},
    {"$set": {"cgpa": 8.9, "skills": ["Python", "MongoDB", "Django"]}}
)
print(f"Matched: {update_res.matched_count}, Modified: {update_res.modified_count}")

# $inc operator increments a numeric field
students.update_one({"roll_no": "KTU2024EC015"}, {"$inc": {"cgpa": 0.2}})

# ---------- STEP 4: DELETE ----------
print("\n--- DELETE ---")

delete_res = students.delete_one({"name": "Rahul"})
print(f"Deleted count: {delete_res.deleted_count}")

# Drop the entire collection (cleanup)
# students.drop()

# ---------- STEP E: CLOSE CONNECTION ----------
client.close()
print("\nConnection closed.")
```

## 3.3 Line-by-Line Analytical Matrix

| Line | Code | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `from pymongo import MongoClient` | Imports the official MongoDB driver class. |
| 2 | `from bson.objectid import ObjectId` | Imports ObjectId type for `_id` queries. |
| 5 | `client = MongoClient("mongodb://localhost:27017/")` | Creates a connection pool. URI scheme: `mongodb://host:port/`. |
| 8 | `db = client["ktu_lab"]` | Selects (or implicitly creates) a database. |
| 11 | `students = db["students"]` | Selects a collection within the database. |
| 26 | `students.insert_one(student_1)` | Inserts a single document. Returns object with `inserted_id`. |
| 31 | `students.insert_many([...])` | Inserts multiple documents at once. List of dicts required. |
| 38 | `students.find_one({"name": "Meera"})` | Returns **one** matching document. |
| 42 | `students.find({"cgpa": {"$gt": 8.0}})` | Cursor of all matches. `$gt` = greater than. |
| 56 | `{"$set": {"cgpa": 8.9}}` | Update operator — only `cgpa` is touched. |
| 66 | `students.delete_one({"name": "Rahul"})` | Deletes **first** matching document only. |
| 75 | `client.close()` | Releases socket resources — best practice. |

## 3.4 Logical Derivation — Why `$set` is Mandatory in Updates

In SQL, the `UPDATE` statement replaces *whole rows* implicitly:

$$
\text{UPDATE students SET cgpa = 8.9 WHERE roll\_no = 'KTU2024CS001';}
$$

In MongoDB, since documents are **schema-less**, "replacing" a field would *destroy* all other unsupplied fields. The `$set` operator forces **explicit partial updates**:

$$
\text{update\_one}(\{filter\}, \{\$set: \{field: new\_value\}\})
$$

This means a document with 5 fields, when updated with `{$set: {field2: "new"}}`, still retains its other 4 fields untouched. **Without `$set`, the entire document would be overwritten by the second argument** — a classic KTU exam pitfall.

## 3.5 Query Operator Reference Derivation

MongoDB uses **operator objects** inside the filter document:

| Operator | MongoDB Syntax | SQL Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Greater Than | `{"$gt": value}` | `WHERE x > value` |
| Less Than | `{"$lt": value}` | `WHERE x < value` |
| Greater / Equal | `{"$gte": value}` | `WHERE x >= value` |
| In Set | `{"$in": [v1, v2]}` | `WHERE x IN (v1, v2)` |
| AND | Comma in filter | `AND` |
| OR | `{"$or": [...]}` | `OR` |
| Exists | `{"$exists": True}` | `IS NOT NULL` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MongoDB ↔ Python Application Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **logical flow** of a MongoDB-Python application, from script execution to data persistence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Python Script] --> B[PyMongo Driver]
    B --> C["MongoClient Connection Pool"]
    C --> D["mongod Server (Port 27017)"]
    D --> E["Database: ktu_lab"]
    E --> F["Collection: students"]
    F --> G["Document 1: BSON"]
    F --> H["Document 2: BSON"]
    F --> I["Document N: BSON"]
    D --> J["WiredTiger Storage Engine"]
    J --> K["Disk: /data/db/*.wt"]

    L[CRUD Method Call] --> M{Operation Type}
    M -->|Create| N["insert_one / insert_many"]
    M -->|Read| O["find / find_one"]
    M -->|Update| P["update_one / $set"]
    M -->|Delete| Q["delete_one / delete_many"]

    N --> F
    O --> F
    P --> F
    Q --> F
```

## 4.2 CRUD Method Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Need to manipulate data?]) --> Op{Which CRUD op?}
    Op -->|Create| C1[Use insert_one for single doc]
    Op -->|Create| C2[Use insert_many for list of docs]
    Op -->|Read| R1[Use find_one for one record]
    Op -->|Read| R2[Use find for multiple with cursor]
    Op -->|Update| U1[Use update_one with $set]
    Op -->|Update| U2[Use update_many for bulk updates]
    Op -->|Delete| D1[Use delete_one for first match]
    Op -->|Delete| D2[Use delete_many for all matches]
    C1 --> End([Document inserted with _id])
    C2 --> End
    R1 --> End2([Returns dict or None])
    R2 --> End3([Returns Cursor iterable])
    U1 --> End4([modified_count >= 1])
    U2 --> End4
    D1 --> End5([deleted_count >= 1])
    D2 --> End5
```

## 4.3 BSON Document Structure (Single Record)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["_id: ObjectId 507f1f77..."] --> B["name: Aravind"]
    A --> C["roll_no: KTU2024CS001"]
    A --> D["cgpa: 8.7"]
    A --> E["branch: CSE"]
    A --> F["skills: array of 3 strings"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Every MongoDB document is a tree of key-value pairs. The root `_id` is *always* the primary key. Unlike SQL, **sub-documents and arrays are first-class citizens** — no separate JOIN table is needed.
